EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 3.49 inches over the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave following a large scale trough earlier in the day provided enough lift to break a weak cap, and sparked an isolated hail-producing storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very little wind with a few quarter-sized hailstones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gradual decrease in river levels occurred as water was routed downstream along the Little Sioux River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the Little Sioux River near Montgomery (gage site MLSI4) began the month at 0.62 feet above flood stage.  Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded, and further impeded spring planting.

Water inundated 180th Street between 170th and 190th Avenues as well as 190th Avenue near 240th Street.  Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded, and further impeded spring planting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nocturnal thunderstorms moved through during the early morning hours of Friday, May 24, 2019.  A tornado watch was in effect. A flash flood watch was also in effect. A squall line developed, with several possible embedded tornadic rotations. 24 hour rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 8.75 inches, the highest amount in southwest Iowa. River flooding developed on local streams, including the West Nishnabotna River, the Nishnabotna River, The Nodaway River, the Little Nemaha River, Weeping Water Creek, and the Big Blue River. The rainfall also pushed the Missouri River at Rulo back into the moderate category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 5.5 inches of rain fell northeast of Red Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued along the Missouri River through the month of May. Increased flood levels prompted the closure of Interstate 29 once again, from Highway 34 southward to the Missouri Border, and from I680/29 split northward to the I680/80 split. Gavins Point Dam releases were increased from 55,000 cfs to 75,000 cfs by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 680 between the Missouri River and Interstate 29 closed again in both directions due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nocturnal thunderstorms moved across the region north of a stalled frontal boundary that set up across the Nebraska and Kansas border. A severe thunderstorm watch was in effect. A flash flood watch was also in effect.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were blown down in Clarinda. The tree limbs landed on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving northward through the region triggered a cluster of severe thunderstorms across southeast Nebraska and southwest IA during the evening of May 21 and early in the morning on May 22. Two brief EF0 tornado touchdowns were reported in southeast Nebraska.

A warm front moving northward through the region triggered a cluster of severe thunderstorms across southeast Nebraska and southwest IA during the evening of May 21 and early in the morning on May 22. Two brief EF0 tornado touchdowns were reported in southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported blown down by the public on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reports from the 13h through the 19th in this entry. ||A relatively deep and widespread snowpack existed across the region during early to mid March. Many areas in central and northern Iowa had around a foot to two feet of snow on the ground, along with many areas in upstream river basins in Minnesota and the Dakotas having similar or deeper snowpacks on the ground. Additionally, with the below normal temperatures throughout the majority of the 2019 year to date, soils were frozen to depths of a foot or more throughout the aforementioned areas. All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported Iowa 4 closed between Wallingford in Emmet County and Graettinger in Palo Alto County due to water over the road.

All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported an ice jam in Iowa Falls has caused rapid rises along the Iowa river. This backed up portions of the river flooding low lying areas and roads across southwestern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level low and surface low reflection made their way off the Rockies and into the central and southern Plains. The upper level and surface features generally slide eastward across southern and central Missouri on the 12th of January, leaving Iowa in the wrap around and deformation regions of the low. Temperatures were generally of little issue with surface and above surface temperatures below freezing, resulting in snow from onset to finish of the event. Initially, indications were for the event to primarily be sub-warning criteria (6 inches in 12 hours, or 8 inches in 24 hours), but out performed initial forecasts to the tune of 10 to 14 inches in select areas and widespread 6 to 8 inches or more across the southern 3 tiers of the state.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values as cold as -40F. A man was found outside Carter Lake around 1130 am January 30th in critical condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure situated over the eastern Pacific and a slow moving upper level closed low over the Great Lakes combined to put the upper Midwest, including Iowa, right in the cross hairs of northwest upper level flow and an Arctic air mass intrusion. Air temperatures plummeted below zero the evening of the 29th across much of the state, and did not rise back above zero in most locations until the afternoon on the 31st. Coldest temperatures were seen across northern and northeast Iowa where temperatures in the 20 to nearly 30 below range were common.  Given the temperatures, even nominal winds put areas into Wind Chill Advisory (-20 to -30 degrees F) with ease. Throughout the time period, many locations experienced wind chill values in the -40 to -55 degrees F range which were some of the coldest wind chills in over two decades.

An Alberta Clipper system came roaring through the flow, bringing with it very strong winds, bitter arctic temperatures, and snowfall across large portions of Iowa. Fortunately, the strongest winds were not concurrent with the greatest snowfall. As the surface low pressure system came ripping through the Dakotas during the afternoon of the 27th, isentropic lift/warm air advection into the system increased over Iowa, especially northern and northeast Iowa. The surface low approached and traversed across Iowa during the overnight hours of the 27th into the 28th, leaving northern and northeast Iowa in the warm air advection wing. While winds did reach into the mid to upper 20s mph sustained at times, the main concern was snowfall. When all said and done, roughly 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the warning area over a roughly 12 hour period with periods of blowing snow mixed in. Two of the strongest wind gusts recorded with the storm were 58 kts observed on the 28th at both the Iowa DOT Rockwell City RWIS site at 3:20 CST and at the Des Moines Airport ASOS at 05:22 CST.

A strong arctic high pressure system brought historic cold to much of the Midwest January 29th through January 31st.  Numerous low temperature records were broken, including all-time record lows at Cedar Rapids Moline.  In addition, life threatening wind chills were reported with values below -30 degrees for many hours.  Numerous schools, churches, and businesses closed during the arctic outbreak.

A significant winter storm system brought accumulating snow and hazardous travel conditions to the region Friday afternoon (Jan. 18) through much of the day Saturday (Jan. 19). Total snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the area, with the higher amounts of 7 to 9 inches in the Freeport, IL area. In addition, strong winds resulted in some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports in neighboring counties were around 4.0 inches. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 MPH caused blowing and drifting snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 5.0 inches 1 mile west of Burlington to 7.0 inches 1 mile east northeast of the Burlington Regional Airport.   Wind gusts of 20 to 30 MPH caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved into the area during the early morning hours of Monday, January 28th. This sent temperatures sharply lower, but also ushered in very strong northwest winds. Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common, with gusts up to 50 to 68 mph reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted up to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought mixed precipitation to the area, with some minor ice accumulations and several inches of snow to southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. A winter weather advisory was issued for all areas. Highest snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8.5 inches, generally higher than expected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported up to 8.5 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chill value reached was -57 at 413 AM on January 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure situated over the eastern Pacific and a slow moving upper level closed low over the Great Lakes combined to put the upper Midwest, including Iowa, right in the cross hairs of northwest upper level flow and an Arctic air mass intrusion. Air temperatures plummeted below zero the evening of the 29th across much of the state, and did not rise back above zero in most locations until the afternoon on the 31st. Coldest temperatures were seen across northern and northeast Iowa where temperatures in the 20 to nearly 30 below range were common.  Given the temperatures, even nominal winds put areas into Wind Chill Advisory (-20 to -30 degrees F) with ease. Throughout the time period, many locations experienced wind chill values in the -40 to -55 degrees F range which were some of the coldest wind chills in over two decades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively complicated forecast resulted in the issuance of a widespread winter storm warning across Iowa. Unfortunately, it did not come to fruition in many of the warned counties as snowfall amounts under 4 inches over 12 or more hours. A few areas in northern and south central Iowa did reach warning criteria, with upwards of 7 to 9 inches of snow. ||The overall setup saw a situation where an open wave/trough in the 500 mb pattern was swinging through the central third of the country, including accompanying southern and northern jet streams. The southern branch of the jet stream was more amplified in nature versus the northern stream, and associated with a developing surface low in the Oklahoma area. In both cases, shortwaves within the larger 500 mb flow moved towards Iowa, roughly affecting the state within the same time windows (morning to afternoon of the 18th and on through the day and evening) and proceeded to produce areas of snowfall devoid of significant winds.

A broad surface low pressure system was meandering across Kansas and Oklahoma on the 22nd of January, eventually sliding into and across central Missouri. As such, Iowa resided well north of the surface low center and received wrap around/warm conveyor belt precipitation as the system progressed northeastward. Initially, precipitation was of the freezing drizzle and wintry mix variety before the column (and surface) cooled sufficiently and the column saturated deep thoroughly enough for consistent ice introduction. Rather than a consistent stratiform type of snowfall, banded elements generally existed throughout the event and provided alternating periods of light to no snow and moderate snow. In all, northern counties within the winter storm warning generally did not receive much snowfall, while central and southern counties were predominantly in the 6 to 8 inch range. A few spots in central Iowa did report 8 to 10 inches.

Freezing drizzle and snow occurred across a portion of northeast Iowa on January 22nd and 23rd as a winter storm moved across the Midwest. The storm produced several rounds of precipitation which started out as freezing drizzle during the morning of the 22nd that coated most surfaces with a light glaze. Snow then occurred with accumulations of 3 to 5 inches. The highest reported total was 5 inches in Guttenberg (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 3 and 5 inches of snow fell across Clayton County. The highest reported total was 5 inches in Guttenberg. The snow was preceded by a period of freezing drizzle that produced a light glaze of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds developed behind a cold front that pushed through Iowa during the evening hours. Most gusts were in the range of 40 to 50 mph, however one especially strong gust to 58 mph was recorded at the Mason City Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mason City Airport ASOS recorded a 58 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With arctic air in place over northeast Iowa, air temperatures dropped into the 20s below zero from January 29th into January 31st. A few sites recorded temperatures in the 30s below zero on the morning of the 31st. When combined with wind speeds of 5 to 25 mph, wind chills of 35 to 60 below were common. The coldest wind chill to occur was 60 below zero in Charles City (Floyd County) during the late evening of January 29th. Most northeast Iowa schools were closed from January 29th through the 31st. The U.S. Post Office suspended delivery operations across all of northeast Iowa on January 30th and 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 60 below zero were common across Allamakee County from the late afternoon of January 29th into the morning of the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm system brought accumulating snow and hazardous travel conditions to the region Friday afternoon (Jan. 18) through much of the day Saturday (Jan. 19). Total snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the area, with the higher amounts of 7 to 9 inches in the Freeport, IL area. In addition, strong winds resulted in some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 4.0 inches 3 miles northwest of DeWitt. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 MPH led to widespread blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought some ice to the area. Initial forecasts also called for 2 to 5 inches of snow, but dry air in the mid levels resulted in freezing drizzle throughout the duration, and snowfall of one half inch or less. Ice accumulation ranged one or two tenths of an inch  in some locations. A few power outages were noted and travel was at times difficult.  A winter weather advisory was issued for all counties, and eventually a winter storm warning for a few counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported numerous accidents across Council Bluffs due to a prolonged period of freezing drizzle.

High pressure situated over the eastern Pacific and a slow moving upper level closed low over the Great Lakes combined to put the upper Midwest, including Iowa, right in the cross hairs of northwest upper level flow and an Arctic air mass intrusion. Air temperatures plummeted below zero the evening of the 29th across much of the state, and did not rise back above zero in most locations until the afternoon on the 31st. Coldest temperatures were seen across northern and northeast Iowa where temperatures in the 20 to nearly 30 below range were common.  Given the temperatures, even nominal winds put areas into Wind Chill Advisory (-20 to -30 degrees F) with ease. Throughout the time period, many locations experienced wind chill values in the -40 to -55 degrees F range which were some of the coldest wind chills in over two decades.

A strong area of low pressure tracked across the lower Mississippi River Valley,  bringing heavy snow from central Missouri and southern Illinois up to northwest Illinois January 11th through January 12th. The heaviest snow amounts fell from Missouri into Ohio, where amounts over a foot were common. Further north, snowfall totals ranged from around 1-3 inches along the Highway 20 corridor, to 4-8 inches around the I-80 corridor, to 8-12 inches along and south of Highway 34.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 9.0 inches from a trained spotter in Burlington to 11 inches from a COOP Observer 2 miles south of Burlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad surface low pressure system was meandering across Kansas and Oklahoma on the 22nd of January, eventually sliding into and across central Missouri. As such, Iowa resided well north of the surface low center and received wrap around/warm conveyor belt precipitation as the system progressed northeastward. Initially, precipitation was of the freezing drizzle and wintry mix variety before the column (and surface) cooled sufficiently and the column saturated deep thoroughly enough for consistent ice introduction. Rather than a consistent stratiform type of snowfall, banded elements generally existed throughout the event and provided alternating periods of light to no snow and moderate snow. In all, northern counties within the winter storm warning generally did not receive much snowfall, while central and southern counties were predominantly in the 6 to 8 inch range. A few spots in central Iowa did report 8 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm system brought hazardous travel conditions to the region the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, Jan. 22, through the morning of Wednesday, Jan. 23.  Freezing drizzle and rain fell for much of the morning and afternoon on Tuesday causing slick roads.  Precipitation quickly changed to snow mainly north of a line from Keosauqua to Burlington.  Snowfall totals of 2 to 6 inches were common over much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  Rainfall totals over three quarters of an inch were seen over west central and north central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 4.1 inches in Stanley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively complicated forecast resulted in the issuance of a widespread winter storm warning across Iowa. Unfortunately, it did not come to fruition in many of the warned counties as snowfall amounts under 4 inches over 12 or more hours. A few areas in northern and south central Iowa did reach warning criteria, with upwards of 7 to 9 inches of snow. ||The overall setup saw a situation where an open wave/trough in the 500 mb pattern was swinging through the central third of the country, including accompanying southern and northern jet streams. The southern branch of the jet stream was more amplified in nature versus the northern stream, and associated with a developing surface low in the Oklahoma area. In both cases, shortwaves within the larger 500 mb flow moved towards Iowa, roughly affecting the state within the same time windows (morning to afternoon of the 18th and on through the day and evening) and proceeded to produce areas of snowfall devoid of significant winds.

Freezing drizzle and rain fell for much of the morning and afternoon on Tuesday causing slick roads.  Precipitation quickly changed to snow mainly north of a line from Keosauqua to Burlington.  Snowfall totals of 2 to 6 inches were common over much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  Rainfall totals over three quarters of an inch were seen over west central and north central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 5.0 inches in Durant by a trained spotter to 6.6 inches by the COOP Observer in Lowden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively complicated forecast resulted in the issuance of a widespread winter storm warning across Iowa. Unfortunately, it did not come to fruition in many of the warned counties as snowfall amounts under 4 inches over 12 or more hours. A few areas in northern and south central Iowa did reach warning criteria, with upwards of 7 to 9 inches of snow. ||The overall setup saw a situation where an open wave/trough in the 500 mb pattern was swinging through the central third of the country, including accompanying southern and northern jet streams. The southern branch of the jet stream was more amplified in nature versus the northern stream, and associated with a developing surface low in the Oklahoma area. In both cases, shortwaves within the larger 500 mb flow moved towards Iowa, roughly affecting the state within the same time windows (morning to afternoon of the 18th and on through the day and evening) and proceeded to produce areas of snowfall devoid of significant winds.

A broad surface low pressure system was meandering across Kansas and Oklahoma on the 22nd of January, eventually sliding into and across central Missouri. As such, Iowa resided well north of the surface low center and received wrap around/warm conveyor belt precipitation as the system progressed northeastward. Initially, precipitation was of the freezing drizzle and wintry mix variety before the column (and surface) cooled sufficiently and the column saturated deep thoroughly enough for consistent ice introduction. Rather than a consistent stratiform type of snowfall, banded elements generally existed throughout the event and provided alternating periods of light to no snow and moderate snow. In all, northern counties within the winter storm warning generally did not receive much snowfall, while central and southern counties were predominantly in the 6 to 8 inch range. A few spots in central Iowa did report 8 to 10 inches.

High pressure situated over the eastern Pacific and a slow moving upper level closed low over the Great Lakes combined to put the upper Midwest, including Iowa, right in the cross hairs of northwest upper level flow and an Arctic air mass intrusion. Air temperatures plummeted below zero the evening of the 29th across much of the state, and did not rise back above zero in most locations until the afternoon on the 31st. Coldest temperatures were seen across northern and northeast Iowa where temperatures in the 20 to nearly 30 below range were common.  Given the temperatures, even nominal winds put areas into Wind Chill Advisory (-20 to -30 degrees F) with ease. Throughout the time period, many locations experienced wind chill values in the -40 to -55 degrees F range which were some of the coldest wind chills in over two decades.

An Alberta Clipper system came roaring through the flow, bringing with it very strong winds, bitter arctic temperatures, and snowfall across large portions of Iowa. Fortunately, the strongest winds were not concurrent with the greatest snowfall. As the surface low pressure system came ripping through the Dakotas during the afternoon of the 27th, isentropic lift/warm air advection into the system increased over Iowa, especially northern and northeast Iowa. The surface low approached and traversed across Iowa during the overnight hours of the 27th into the 28th, leaving northern and northeast Iowa in the warm air advection wing. While winds did reach into the mid to upper 20s mph sustained at times, the main concern was snowfall. When all said and done, roughly 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the warning area over a roughly 12 hour period with periods of blowing snow mixed in. Two of the strongest wind gusts recorded with the storm were 58 kts observed on the 28th at both the Iowa DOT Rockwell City RWIS site at 3:20 CST and at the Des Moines Airport ASOS at 05:22 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked across the lower Mississippi River Valley,  bringing heavy snow from central Missouri and southern Illinois up to northwest Illinois January 11th through January 12th. The heaviest snow amounts fell from Missouri into Ohio, where amounts over a foot were common. Further north, snowfall totals ranged from around 1-3 inches along the Highway 20 corridor, to 4-8 inches around the I-80 corridor, to 8-12 inches along and south of Highway 34.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Keota reported 6.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm system brought accumulating snow and hazardous travel conditions to the region Friday afternoon (Jan. 18) through much of the day Saturday (Jan. 19). Total snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the area, with the higher amounts of 7 to 9 inches in the Freeport, IL area. In addition, strong winds resulted in some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 6.3 inches of snow in Independence. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 MPH caused widespread and drifting of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values as cold as -40F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level low and surface low reflection made their way off the Rockies and into the central and southern Plains. The upper level and surface features generally slide eastward across southern and central Missouri on the 12th of January, leaving Iowa in the wrap around and deformation regions of the low. Temperatures were generally of little issue with surface and above surface temperatures below freezing, resulting in snow from onset to finish of the event. Initially, indications were for the event to primarily be sub-warning criteria (6 inches in 12 hours, or 8 inches in 24 hours), but out performed initial forecasts to the tune of 10 to 14 inches in select areas and widespread 6 to 8 inches or more across the southern 3 tiers of the state.

A winter storm covered northeast Iowa with a fresh blanket of snow in late January. The snow fell from the evening of January 27th through much of the day on the 28th. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches were common and the highest reported totals were 7.3 inches in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County) and 6.5 inches near Cresco and in Riceville (Howard County). Most northeast Iowa schools were closed January 28th following the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 and 6 inches of snow fell across Allamakee County. The highest reported total was 6 inches near Waterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure situated over the eastern Pacific and a slow moving upper level closed low over the Great Lakes combined to put the upper Midwest, including Iowa, right in the cross hairs of northwest upper level flow and an Arctic air mass intrusion. Air temperatures plummeted below zero the evening of the 29th across much of the state, and did not rise back above zero in most locations until the afternoon on the 31st. Coldest temperatures were seen across northern and northeast Iowa where temperatures in the 20 to nearly 30 below range were common.  Given the temperatures, even nominal winds put areas into Wind Chill Advisory (-20 to -30 degrees F) with ease. Throughout the time period, many locations experienced wind chill values in the -40 to -55 degrees F range which were some of the coldest wind chills in over two decades.

An Alberta Clipper system came roaring through the flow, bringing with it very strong winds, bitter arctic temperatures, and snowfall across large portions of Iowa. Fortunately, the strongest winds were not concurrent with the greatest snowfall. As the surface low pressure system came ripping through the Dakotas during the afternoon of the 27th, isentropic lift/warm air advection into the system increased over Iowa, especially northern and northeast Iowa. The surface low approached and traversed across Iowa during the overnight hours of the 27th into the 28th, leaving northern and northeast Iowa in the warm air advection wing. While winds did reach into the mid to upper 20s mph sustained at times, the main concern was snowfall. When all said and done, roughly 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the warning area over a roughly 12 hour period with periods of blowing snow mixed in. Two of the strongest wind gusts recorded with the storm were 58 kts observed on the 28th at both the Iowa DOT Rockwell City RWIS site at 3:20 CST and at the Des Moines Airport ASOS at 05:22 CST.

A significant winter storm system brought accumulating snow and hazardous travel conditions to the region Friday afternoon (Jan. 18) through much of the day Saturday (Jan. 19). Total snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the area, with the higher amounts of 7 to 9 inches in the Freeport, IL area. In addition, strong winds resulted in some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports ranged from 5.0 inches in Belle Plaine to 6.0 inches of snow 3 miles south of Urbana. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 MPH caused widespread and drifting of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm system brought hazardous travel conditions to the region the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, Jan. 22, through the morning of Wednesday, Jan. 23.  Freezing drizzle and rain fell for much of the morning and afternoon on Tuesday causing slick roads.  Precipitation quickly changed to snow mainly north of a line from Keosauqua to Burlington.  Snowfall totals of 2 to 6 inches were common over much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  Rainfall totals over three quarters of an inch were seen over west central and north central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as a mix of rani, freezing rain, and sleet before changing over to all snow during the afternoon. the official NWS observation at the Davenport Municipal Airport was 6.4 inches. Trained spotter reports ranged from 5.8 inches in Walcott to 8.4 inches 1 mile east notheast of Montpelier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved into the area during the early morning hours of Monday, January 28th. This sent temperatures sharply lower, but also ushered in very strong northwest winds. Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph were common, with gusts up to 50 to 68 mph reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted up to 68 mph.

A strong arctic high pressure system brought historic cold to much of the Midwest January 29th through January 31st.  Numerous low temperature records were broken, including all-time record lows at Cedar Rapids Moline.  In addition, life threatening wind chills were reported with values below -30 degrees for many hours.  Numerous schools, churches, and businesses closed during the arctic outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures at the Iowa City Airport ASOS on the 30th and 31st were -26 and -29 Fahrenheit.  The coldest wind chill during this period were -51 at 411 AM.

Numerous low temperature records were broken, including all-time record lows at Cedar Rapids Moline.  In addition, life threatening wind chills were reported with values below -30 degrees for many hours.  Numerous schools, churches, and businesses closed during the arctic outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record low temperatures were set on the mornings of the 30th and 31st when the ASOS at Burlington recorded record low temperatures of -20 and -21 degrees Fahrenheit. The coldest wind chill measured at the Burlington Regional Airport ASOS was -46 at 253 AM on the 30th.

In addition, life threatening wind chills were reported with values below -30 degrees for many hours.  Numerous schools, churches, and businesses closed during the arctic outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record low temperatures were set on January 30t and 31t when low temperatures dipped to -26 and -30 respectively. The -30 on the 31st also set the all time record low for the month and the all time record low at Cedar Rapids. Wind chill were also bitterly cold during this period with a wind chill value of -55 at 1232 AM on the 30th. There were wind chills of -30 to -55 during this period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter continued its grip on Iowa, with persistent cold air advection after the departure of a combination of blizzard conditions, freezing rain, and blowing snow. As the parent surface low and 500 mb trough departed the area, through the 7th of February, gusty northwest winds to 20 to 25+ mph ushered in a push of Arctic air that peaked overnight on the 7th into the morning of the 8th. As a result, morning lows dropped down to around -10 degrees F across portions of northern and northwest Iowa, while near zero to below zero temperatures prevailed all the way to the Iowa-Missouri border.  While the surface pressure gradient and resulting winds relaxed through the evening of the 7th, the bitter cold did not require strong winds to result in persistent -25 to -35 degree F wind chill values overnight and into the morning hours. Winds in the 10 to 20 mph range were sufficient to achieve the previously mentioned wind chill values.  Incoming surface high pressure continued to relax winds, allowing wind chill values to warm above the -20 degrees F range or warmer by noon on the 8th.

A strong area of low pressure tracked across the lower Mississippi River Valley,  bringing heavy snow from central Missouri and southern Illinois up to northwest Illinois January 11th through January 12th. The heaviest snow amounts fell from Missouri into Ohio, where amounts over a foot were common. Further north, snowfall totals ranged from around 1-3 inches along the Highway 20 corridor, to 4-8 inches around the I-80 corridor, to 8-12 inches along and south of Highway 34.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Lowden reported 6.0 inches of snow. A trained spotter reported 5.0 inches in Durant.

Numerous low temperature records were broken, including all-time record lows at Cedar Rapids Moline.  In addition, life threatening wind chills were reported with values below -30 degrees for many hours.  Numerous schools, churches, and businesses closed during the arctic outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record cold temperatures occurred on the 30th and 31st when the low temperatures were -29 and -31 respectively. A record cool high of -14 occurred on the 30th.  The minimum wind chill at the Dubuque Airport ASOS was -55.

A significant winter storm system brought accumulating snow and hazardous travel conditions to the region Friday afternoon (Jan. 18) through much of the day Saturday (Jan. 19). Total snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches fell across the area, with the higher amounts of 7 to 9 inches in the Freeport, IL area. In addition, strong winds resulted in some blowing and drifting snow and hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 4.8 inches in Muscatine  Wind gusts of 20 to 30 MPH caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported a broken power pole at the intersection of White Pole Road and Adair Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow northerly push of colder air strengthened heading through February 3. As a weak disturbance lifted northeast from the Rockies during the overnight hours, drying aloft on top of the shallow cold and moist layer along with weak lift resulted in widespread freezing drizzle. A glaze of ice accumulation ranging from a few hundredths to a tenth of an inch impeded travel during the evening and overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels remained high across much of the state through the latter half of April, so additional rainfall sent a few stations back into flood. The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Humboldt remained in flood through the end of April and into May. Flood periods earlier in the month are listed in separate episodes for both the Emmetsburg and Humboldt areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Emmetsburg crested at 11.06 feet on 24 April 2019 at 05:00 UTC.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Emmetsburg crested at 11.44 feet on 13 April 2019 at 11:30 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More snow moved through the area during starting during the afternoon on Saturday, February 16 and lingering into Sunday morning February 17. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 9.5 inches occurred. Winds remained on the lighter side during the event generally less than 15 mph.  A winter weather advisory was issued for the entire area on Friday, and a few counties were upgraded to a winter storm warning Saturday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported up to 9.5 inches of snow via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large dynamic system was moving into the Plains, tracking northeastward through the Panhandles of Texas and Oklahoma and towards northwest Missouri and southwest Iowa. There were large surface temperature disparities on either side of the warm front, with large portions of Iowa in the mid 30s to mid 40s, and areas down into Missouri quickly up into the 70s. The 850mb warm front was situated further north into central and northern Iowa, helping boost MUCAPE values in excess of 1000 J/kg. Effective shear, understandable, was better further west and south closer to the low pressure center. While most of the convection was linear and sub-severe in nature, a pair of severe reports were received in separate portions of the state, one each in northern and western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm moved through the region, bringing mixed wintry precipitation, heavy snow, and very strong winds. The storm created widespread blizzard conditions and 6 to 12 inches of snow. 24 hours of freezing fog, freezing rain, and a wintry mix led up to the blizzard, which compounded travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby counties reported one quarter mile visibility and wind gusts 35 to 50 mph which created whiteout conditions. 4 to 8.5 inches of snow was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and melting snow across the Upper Midwest produced high flows along the Mississippi River in late March. Flooding occurred from McGregor (Clayton County) downstream to Guttenberg (Clayton County) and continued into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rain and melting snow across the Upper Midwest produced high flows along the Mississippi River. Flooding began in McGregor on March 18th and continued into April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter weather commenced late evening on Thursday, November 28 with a mix of light snow and freezing rain, which produced up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation before surface temperatures warmed above freezing on the morning of November 30.  A narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall enhanced over the area during the afternoon and evening of November 30, producing 4.5 inches of snowfall at Sibley and 7.5 inches near Round Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than average snowfall through the winter created a wet snowpack across much of Iowa which was still present into early March. A large storm system impacted the region through the middle of March which brought heavy rain and warm temperatures to the state. This system allowed much of the snowpack to melt across the state within a few days to a week. This heavy rain fell on frozen ground allowing nearly all of the rainfall and snowmelt to runoff into the river systems. This led to flooding on nearly all rivers and nearly all river points throughout central Iowa. The majority of the river flooding had ended with rivers returning to their banks by late March. However portions of the upper Des Moines Basin remained in flood into April. These points were the Des Moines River at Fort Dodge, and the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville, Emmetsburg, and Humboldt. Many of the rises on the rivers were also due to ice jams that formed on the river channel due to break up jams as temperatures warmed and river levels rose.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested at 13.22 feet likely due to an ice jam on 12 March 2019 at 15:00 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the Spring of 2019.  Moderate to major flooding was observed on the Mississippi River due to snowmelt, frozen ground, ice jams, saturated soils, and rainfall from mid March, through the end of the month and into April and May. An initial crest occurred during the third and fourth weeks of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt across the region caused the Mississippi River at New Boston and Keithsburg  to rise above their major flood stages of 18.5 feet and 17 feet, respectively, on March 16th, and remain above those stages through the end of the month. An initial crest of 20.8 feet occurred at New Boston the 24th into the 25th. An initial crest of 19.09 feet occurred at Keithsburg late in the day on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed across Iowa through the evening of the 10th of April and on through the morning of April 11th as a low pressure system off the Rockies further developed and traversed initially eastward across Kansas and then northward roughly along the Kansas-Missouri and Nebraska-Iowa borders. With broad high pressure situated over the Hudson Bay area and south through much of Canada, the surface pressure gradient tightened across northern Iowa to the point where numerous wind gusts in the upper 50s to low 60s mph were reported along with a number of sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph. Impacts reported primarily revolved around minor structure damage and multiple semis overturned along Highways and Interstates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air began filtering into the region March 2nd and 3rd, culminating in Arctic temperatures overnight March 3rd into the morning of March 4th with air temperatures dropping into the single digits below zero across the state, and the 9 to 12 below zero range across the coldest portions of northern Iowa. With the core of the surface high pressure situated to the west over Nebraska and Kansas, west to northwest winds around 10 to 15 kts dropped wind chill values into the 25 to 35 degrees below zero range.

With the core of the surface high pressure situated to the west over Nebraska and Kansas, west to northwest winds around 10 to 15 kts dropped wind chill values into the 25 to 35 degrees below zero range.

Strong winds developed across Iowa through the evening of the 10th of April and on through the morning of April 11th as a low pressure system off the Rockies further developed and traversed initially eastward across Kansas and then northward roughly along the Kansas-Missouri and Nebraska-Iowa borders. With broad high pressure situated over the Hudson Bay area and south through much of Canada, the surface pressure gradient tightened across northern Iowa to the point where numerous wind gusts in the upper 50s to low 60s mph were reported along with a number of sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph. Impacts reported primarily revolved around minor structure damage and multiple semis overturned along Highways and Interstates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa Falls AWOS measured 45 mph sustained winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than average snowfall through the winter created a wet snowpack across much of Iowa which was still present into early March. A large storm system impacted the region through the middle of March which brought heavy rain and warm temperatures to the state. This system allowed much of the snowpack to melt across the state within a few days to a week. This heavy rain fell on frozen ground allowing nearly all of the rainfall and snowmelt to runoff into the river systems. This led to flooding on nearly all rivers and nearly all river points throughout central Iowa. The majority of the river flooding had ended with rivers returning to their banks by late March. However portions of the upper Des Moines Basin remained in flood into April. These points were the Des Moines River at Fort Dodge, and the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville, Emmetsburg, and Humboldt. Many of the rises on the rivers were also due to ice jams that formed on the river channel due to break up jams as temperatures warmed and river levels rose.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the second period of flood during the month of March for the Des Moines River at Fort Dodge. Two of the 8 crests that occurred during the flood periods in March occurred during this flood period and were likely due to ice jams. The first crest during this period (third overall for March) was 11.28 feet on 15 March 2019 at 22:45 UTC and the second crest this period (fourth overall for March) was 11.28 feet on 16 March 2019 at 04:45 UTC.

Flooding continued along the Missouri River throughout the month of April.  Although river levels went down during the month of April, compromised levees along the river kept flooding major highways such as US 34, Iowa Highway 2, and Interstate 29.  The towns of Hamburg, Pacific Junction, Thurman, and McPaul were flooded or access to the towns was flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued on the Missouri River due to snow melt in the Dakotas and over the Northern Rockies, a few episodes of rain, and increased discharges from dams on the Missouri River over the Dakotas.  In addition flooding continued due to levee breaks on the river.  Towns that continued to have flooding included Bartlett, McPaul, Thurman, Percival and Hamburg.  Interstate 29 remained closed with water over the road through much of the county.  Iowa Highway 2 was also under water and suffered extreme damage. Businesses just west of Interstate 29 on Highway 2 were also under water.

The Raceway Park of the Midlands continued to be under water as well as Interstate 29 south of US 34 and US 34 from the Missouri River east.  US 34 had significant erosion damage due to the moving water. Damage estimates for Mills County were included in March Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reports from the 13h through the 19th in this entry. ||A relatively deep and widespread snowpack existed across the region during early to mid March. Many areas in central and northern Iowa had around a foot to two feet of snow on the ground, along with many areas in upstream river basins in Minnesota and the Dakotas having similar or deeper snowpacks on the ground. Additionally, with the below normal temperatures throughout the majority of the 2019 year to date, soils were frozen to depths of a foot or more throughout the aforementioned areas. All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported Big Creek out of its banks east of Boone causing agricultural flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air began filtering into the region March 2nd and 3rd, culminating in Arctic temperatures overnight March 3rd into the morning of March 4th with air temperatures dropping into the single digits below zero across the state, and the 9 to 12 below zero range across the coldest portions of northern Iowa. With the core of the surface high pressure situated to the west over Nebraska and Kansas, west to northwest winds around 10 to 15 kts dropped wind chill values into the 25 to 35 degrees below zero range.

Higher than average snowfall through the winter created a wet snowpack across much of Iowa which was still present into early March. A large storm system impacted the region through the middle of March which brought heavy rain and warm temperatures to the state. This system allowed much of the snowpack to melt across the state within a few days to a week. This heavy rain fell on frozen ground allowing nearly all of the rainfall and snowmelt to runoff into the river systems. This led to flooding on nearly all rivers and nearly all river points throughout central Iowa. The majority of the river flooding had ended with rivers returning to their banks by late March. However portions of the upper Des Moines Basin remained in flood into April. These points were the Des Moines River at Fort Dodge, and the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville, Emmetsburg, and Humboldt. Many of the rises on the rivers were also due to ice jams that formed on the river channel due to break up jams as temperatures warmed and river levels rose.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Middle River at Indianola crested at 23.92 feet likely due to an ice jam on 12 March 2019 at 07:30 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of rainfall and a melting snowpack, caused some flooding to occur across Clayton County. An ice jam on the Turkey River caused flooding to occur in the Millville area with water going over U.S. Highway 52. Near the mouth of the Turkey River, the ice jam took out a railroad bridge. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reports from the 13h through the 19th in this entry. ||A relatively deep and widespread snowpack existed across the region during early to mid March. Many areas in central and northern Iowa had around a foot to two feet of snow on the ground, along with many areas in upstream river basins in Minnesota and the Dakotas having similar or deeper snowpacks on the ground. Additionally, with the below normal temperatures throughout the majority of the 2019 year to date, soils were frozen to depths of a foot or more throughout the aforementioned areas. All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported that US 71 north and southbound will be closed due to flooding between 2nd St and US 30 in Carroll.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early spring storm system produced snow, sleet and freezing rain across northeast Iowa from April 10th into the 11th. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches. Strong east winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts, brought down power lines across portions of Fayette County where enough freezing rain occurred to coat exposed surfaces. The strong winds also blew over a semi truck on State Highway 150 north of West Union (Fayette County) causing the road to be closed for almost two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds from an early spring storm gusted has high as 61 mph near West Union. The strong winds flipped a semi onto its side north of West Union causing State Highway 150 to be closed for almost two hours. Trees and power lines were also downed by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of precipitation through the region. The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west. There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation. Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation. ||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm. The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage. ||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota. Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind. At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power. Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm.||In fact, the strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color. Ironically, the blizzard postponed the statewide tornado warning drill which was a part of Minnesota Severe Weather Awareness Week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to one-quarter to one-third inch, mainly across the northern half of the county, and mainly from late evening on April 10 through early morning April 11. As winds increased early April 11 with gusts reaching 45 to 50 mph at times, power poles and transmission lines bore the brunt of the worst conditions.  Twenty power transmission poles were snapped between Sioux Center and Orange City due to the weight of the ice accumulation and stress of the high winds. Property damage costs include damage to utilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the Spring of 2019.  Moderate to major flooding was observed on the Mississippi River due to snowmelt, frozen ground, ice jams, saturated soils, and rainfall from mid March, and continued through the end of the month and into April and May. An initial crest occurred during the third and fourth weeks of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt across the region caused the Mississippi River to rise above its major flood stage of 20.0 feet on March 22nd, and remain above major flood stage through the end of the month. An initial crest of 20.71 feet occurred early in the morning of the 25th, and again late in the day on the 25th through the early morning hours of the 26th.

All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported Iowa 188 closed between Iowa 3 and County Road T64, cross road is 140th St, due to flooding. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic late-season, multi-day winter storm developed rapidly across the central Plains on Wednesday, April 10. This system spiraled several periods of precipitation through the region. The leading precipitation on April 10 was a mix of rain, freezing rain and sleet from far eastern South Dakota through the Interstate 90 areas of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, changing to snow to the north and west. There were even a few elevated thunderstorms which occurred overnight on April 10-11 and produced some near severe hail, which actually occurred after some initial wintry precipitation. Significant icing was centered around 30 miles either side of the Interstate 90 corridor, with as much as a third to three-quarters inch ice accumulation. ||Heavy snowfall developed from the early morning of April 11 and continued into the early morning of April 12 across much of southeast South Dakota into northern portions of southwest Minnesota, which accumulated to 1 to 2 feet during the storm. The most persistent snowfall occurred for areas west to north of Sioux Falls, but even there, a brief period of freezing rain or sleet occurred at some point ahead of the main upper trough passage. ||The other aspect of this storm was the intense winds, which gusted at the peak from 40 to 60 mph, producing widespread blizzard conditions across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota. Considerable power outages occurred as transmission lines succumbed to the ice accumulation and wind. At the peak, electric companies and cooperatives estimated as many as 25,000 customers were without power. Many areas were without power for a day or two due to the intensity of the storm.||In fact, the strong winds lofted dust from the Texas/Oklahoma panhandles which caused some of the snowfall toward the latter portions of the event to fall with a brownish color. Ironically, the blizzard postponed the statewide tornado warning drill which was a part of Minnesota Severe Weather Awareness Week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to one-quarter to one-third inch, mainly across the northern half of the county, and mainly from late evening on April 10 through early morning April 11. As winds increased early April 11 with gusts reaching 45 to 50 mph at times, power poles and transmission lines bore the brunt of the worst conditions.  More than 50 power transmission poles were snapped between Rock Valley and Inwood due to the weight of the ice accumulation and stress of the high winds. Several roads were closed due to power structures across the roadway. Property damage costs include damage to utilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than average snowfall through the winter created a wet snowpack across much of Iowa which was still present into early March. A large storm system impacted the region through the middle of March which brought heavy rain and warm temperatures to the state. This system allowed much of the snowpack to melt across the state within a few days to a week. This heavy rain fell on frozen ground allowing nearly all of the rainfall and snowmelt to runoff into the river systems. This led to flooding on nearly all rivers and nearly all river points throughout central Iowa. The majority of the river flooding had ended with rivers returning to their banks by late March. However portions of the upper Des Moines Basin remained in flood into April. These points were the Des Moines River at Fort Dodge, and the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville, Emmetsburg, and Humboldt. Many of the rises on the rivers were also due to ice jams that formed on the river channel due to break up jams as temperatures warmed and river levels rose.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Skunk River at Colfax crested at 18.80 feet on 16 March 2019 at 15:30 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reports from the 13h through the 19th in this entry. ||A relatively deep and widespread snowpack existed across the region during early to mid March. Many areas in central and northern Iowa had around a foot to two feet of snow on the ground, along with many areas in upstream river basins in Minnesota and the Dakotas having similar or deeper snowpacks on the ground. Additionally, with the below normal temperatures throughout the majority of the 2019 year to date, soils were frozen to depths of a foot or more throughout the aforementioned areas. All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported that several streets near the Mason City Fire Station are closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed across Iowa through the evening of the 10th of April and on through the morning of April 11th as a low pressure system off the Rockies further developed and traversed initially eastward across Kansas and then northward roughly along the Kansas-Missouri and Nebraska-Iowa borders. With broad high pressure situated over the Hudson Bay area and south through much of Canada, the surface pressure gradient tightened across northern Iowa to the point where numerous wind gusts in the upper 50s to low 60s mph were reported along with a number of sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph. Impacts reported primarily revolved around minor structure damage and multiple semis overturned along Highways and Interstates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KAAL-TV viewer shared picture of Casey's Gas Station overhang shelter blown over. Forest City AWOS was not providing reports at the time. Powerlines were also reported blown down across US 69 near Leland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day with gusty winds across the state. Most sites saw gusts into the 30 to 45 mph range. However one location in Clive, IA in the DSM Metro reported some tree damage from the wind gusts. The highest gusts seemed concentrated across central, southern and southeastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures of large tree limbs down in Clive from a local tv station meteorologist on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed along a cold front and moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of May 5th. These storms dropped mainly small hail but some quarter sized hail was reported in Elkader (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in Elkader.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level open wave trough slid through the Upper Midwest on the 7th and 8th of April, dragging a surface cold front along with it across the state on the mid to late afternoon hours of the 7th. Ahead of the front, conditions were supportive of a few strong storms, including around 1000-1500 J/kg MUCAPE, 300+ J/kg hail CAPE, and borderline bulk effective shear of 25 to 30 kts. By mid to late afternoon, the cold front was draped across central Iowa and aided in initiating a handful of storms that produced at least brief periods of near severe to severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of May 18th over northeast Iowa. Some of these storms remained nearly stationary over Chickasaw County from the late afternoon into the evening and produced rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches. Flash flooding occurred in New Hampton and water went over U.S. Highway 63 north of New Hampton. Some of the initial storms also dropped some nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Des Moines Public Safety reported a tree fell on a power line, causing power flashes in south Des Moines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

A surface low pressure system slide across southern Iowa on the 27th, eventually turning southeastward into Illinois by late afternoon. Prior to exiting the state, the system did manage to produce light snowfall accumulations of 1 to just over 3 inches across portions of northern Iowa. Quickly on its heels was surface high pressure which centered itself right over Iowa during the late evening and overnight hours of the 27th into the 28th. With clear skies, fresh snow cover for some locations, and cold air having moved in, a freeze warning was issued for the entire county warning area for the overnight hours. By the morning, all but a handful of counties in the southwest managed to drop below freezing for at least a couple of hours. Fortunately for susceptible plant life, a majority of areas avoided prolonged hard freeze temperatures of 28 degrees or less.

Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of occasional light snowfall and freezing drizzle began during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and resulted in a few hundredths of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 29. Snowfall totals reached 3.1 inches in Primghar and 1.7 inches in Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.17 inches over the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding continued into April due to snowmelt coming down through the river system from Minnesota and continued rainfall through the month of April. Lingering flooding from March continued along the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville throughout the entire month of April and continued into May. The West Fork of the Des River at Humboldt briefly went out of flood for around 1.5 days toward the latter portion of April before going back into flood on the 24th of April and continuing in flood into May. That flood period will be listed in a separate episode for the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Emmetsburg lingered in flood from the month of March. There was no crest during the first flood period in April. The river continued to fall through the early portion of April, going out of flood during the day on the 6th. Two additional flood periods were observed at Emmetsburg in April. They will be listed in separate episodes later in the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding continued into April due to snowmelt coming down through the river system from Minnesota and continued rainfall through the month of April. Lingering flooding from March continued along the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville throughout the entire month of April and continued into May. The West Fork of the Des River at Humboldt briefly went out of flood for around 1.5 days toward the latter portion of April before going back into flood on the 24th of April and continuing in flood into May. That flood period will be listed in a separate episode for the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Humboldt lingered in flood from the month of March. There were two crests during the first flood period in April. After the second crest the river continued to fall and went out of flood during the overnight hours of 21 April into 22 April 2019. The river point at Humboldt went back into flood on the 24th of April. This flood period will be listed in a separate episode later in the month. The first crest during the first flood period in April was 11.5 feet on 7 April 2019 at 18:30 UTC and the second crest during the first flood period in April was 11.05 feet on 14 April 2019 at 20:45 UTC.
