EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system developed in the southern Colorado front range area on April 16th and slid east northeast through Kansas, Nebraska, and into Iowa through the 17th of April. By mid afternoon on the 17th, the surface low was situated over north central Iowa/south central Minnesota and the accompanying surface low extending back south southwest through the state. In the warm sector, SBCAPE values were in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts, nominal hail CAPE values in the 200 to 300 J/kg range, and nearly negligible DCAPE. Storms were driven by the cold front and were pulse storms in nature in most cases. Storms were predominantly sub-severe with gusty winds and small hail, though two instances of severe reports were seen in the form of a destroyed farm shed and quarter inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a destroyed farm shed, via social media.

Fortunately for susceptible plant life, a majority of areas avoided prolonged hard freeze temperatures of 28 degrees or less.

A cluster of low level jet initiated storms in central Nebraska slowly made their way eastward overnight and during the early morning hours of the 14th. With zonal flow aloft, their general motion was directly eastward. As the low level jet waned in the morning, the overall extent of the storms diminished, but never fully ceased. As diurnal heating increased the storms remained on the leading edge of the gradient, where MUCAPE values crept into the 1000-3000 J/kg neighborhood. While hail cape values were marginal, under 800 J/kg, there was enough effective bulk shear, around 40 to 50 kts at times, to allow storms to organize and produce periods of heavy rainfall and severe hail. The majority of storms produced marginally severe hail, with a couple of reports as large as ping pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system developed in the southern Colorado front range area on April 16th and slid east northeast through Kansas, Nebraska, and into Iowa through the 17th of April. By mid afternoon on the 17th, the surface low was situated over north central Iowa/south central Minnesota and the accompanying surface low extending back south southwest through the state. In the warm sector, SBCAPE values were in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts, nominal hail CAPE values in the 200 to 300 J/kg range, and nearly negligible DCAPE. Storms were driven by the cold front and were pulse storms in nature in most cases. Storms were predominantly sub-severe with gusty winds and small hail, though two instances of severe reports were seen in the form of a destroyed farm shed and quarter inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a rather chilly overnight, complete with reports of up to 3.5 inches of snow in northern Iowa, storms initiated across portions of central Iowa and produced multiple instances of severe hail. ||Throughout the day, surface high pressure centered over the state slid eastward, placing the western half of the state back in warm air advection by the evening hours, especially above surface levels. The environment was not extremely conducive to sustained severe weather, but did have indications of hail potential, including a freezing level below 10kft, moderate to steep lapse rates above the near surface inversion, and a few hundred J/kg CAPE in the hail growth zone. Elevated storms roughly initiated along the 850mb front along and near I-80 across western Des Moines and west of the metro area. Storms were not long lived, but were able to produce a number of reports of severe hail, including up to half dollar in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail south of Dexter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grinnell Municipal Airport AWOS recorded heavy rainfall of 3.19 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reports from the 13h through the 19th in this entry. ||A relatively deep and widespread snowpack existed across the region during early to mid March. Many areas in central and northern Iowa had around a foot to two feet of snow on the ground, along with many areas in upstream river basins in Minnesota and the Dakotas having similar or deeper snowpacks on the ground. Additionally, with the below normal temperatures throughout the majority of the 2019 year to date, soils were frozen to depths of a foot or more throughout the aforementioned areas. All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple secondary roads were closed across the county from snowmelt and heavy rain during the period. In addition an ice jam around Humboldt and Dakota City were causing flooding during the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of low level jet initiated storms in central Nebraska slowly made their way eastward overnight and during the early morning hours of the 14th. With zonal flow aloft, their general motion was directly eastward. As the low level jet waned in the morning, the overall extent of the storms diminished, but never fully ceased. As diurnal heating increased the storms remained on the leading edge of the gradient, where MUCAPE values crept into the 1000-3000 J/kg neighborhood. While hail cape values were marginal, under 800 J/kg, there was enough effective bulk shear, around 40 to 50 kts at times, to allow storms to organize and produce periods of heavy rainfall and severe hail. The majority of storms produced marginally severe hail, with a couple of reports as large as ping pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Audubon Fire reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

A surface low pressure system slide across southern Iowa on the 27th, eventually turning southeastward into Illinois by late afternoon. Prior to exiting the state, the system did manage to produce light snowfall accumulations of 1 to just over 3 inches across portions of northern Iowa. Quickly on its heels was surface high pressure which centered itself right over Iowa during the late evening and overnight hours of the 27th into the 28th. With clear skies, fresh snow cover for some locations, and cold air having moved in, a freeze warning was issued for the entire county warning area for the overnight hours. By the morning, all but a handful of counties in the southwest managed to drop below freezing for at least a couple of hours. Fortunately for susceptible plant life, a majority of areas avoided prolonged hard freeze temperatures of 28 degrees or less.

A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 3.06 inches so far. Beginning time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed across Iowa through the evening of the 10th of April and on through the morning of April 11th as a low pressure system off the Rockies further developed and traversed initially eastward across Kansas and then northward roughly along the Kansas-Missouri and Nebraska-Iowa borders. With broad high pressure situated over the Hudson Bay area and south through much of Canada, the surface pressure gradient tightened across northern Iowa to the point where numerous wind gusts in the upper 50s to low 60s mph were reported along with a number of sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph. Impacts reported primarily revolved around minor structure damage and multiple semis overturned along Highways and Interstates.

A surface low pressure system slide across southern Iowa on the 27th, eventually turning southeastward into Illinois by late afternoon. Prior to exiting the state, the system did manage to produce light snowfall accumulations of 1 to just over 3 inches across portions of northern Iowa. Quickly on its heels was surface high pressure which centered itself right over Iowa during the late evening and overnight hours of the 27th into the 28th. With clear skies, fresh snow cover for some locations, and cold air having moved in, a freeze warning was issued for the entire county warning area for the overnight hours. By the morning, all but a handful of counties in the southwest managed to drop below freezing for at least a couple of hours. Fortunately for susceptible plant life, a majority of areas avoided prolonged hard freeze temperatures of 28 degrees or less.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second round of runoff from rain and snowmelt caused renewed flooding to occur along the Mississippi River in Lansing. The river crested just under a foot above the flood stage at 17.83 feet on April 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River flooding continued into April due to snowmelt coming down through the river system from Minnesota and continued rainfall through the month of April. Lingering flooding from March continued along the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville throughout the entire month of April and continued into May. The West Fork of the Des River at Humboldt briefly went out of flood for around 1.5 days toward the latter portion of April before going back into flood on the 24th of April and continuing in flood into May. That flood period will be listed in a separate episode for the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville remained in flood for the entire month of April and crested 3 times during the period of flood. The first crest was 12.01 feet on 12 April 2019 at 15:45 UTC, the second crest was 11.99 feet on 22 April 2019 at 22:30 UTC, and the third crest was 11.50 feet on 29 April 2019 at 07:00 UTC.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Fort Dodge lingered in flood from the month of March. There was no crest during the flood period in April. The river continued to fall through the early portion of April, going out of flood during the day on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reports from the 13h through the 19th in this entry. ||A relatively deep and widespread snowpack existed across the region during early to mid March. Many areas in central and northern Iowa had around a foot to two feet of snow on the ground, along with many areas in upstream river basins in Minnesota and the Dakotas having similar or deeper snowpacks on the ground. Additionally, with the below normal temperatures throughout the majority of the 2019 year to date, soils were frozen to depths of a foot or more throughout the aforementioned areas. All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported Indian Creek over the road at N 19th and W 88th in Colfax.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of low level jet initiated storms in central Nebraska slowly made their way eastward overnight and during the early morning hours of the 14th. With zonal flow aloft, their general motion was directly eastward. As the low level jet waned in the morning, the overall extent of the storms diminished, but never fully ceased. As diurnal heating increased the storms remained on the leading edge of the gradient, where MUCAPE values crept into the 1000-3000 J/kg neighborhood. While hail cape values were marginal, under 800 J/kg, there was enough effective bulk shear, around 40 to 50 kts at times, to allow storms to organize and produce periods of heavy rainfall and severe hail. The majority of storms produced marginally severe hail, with a couple of reports as large as ping pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day, another round of storms within upper level zonal flow and low level jet initiated storms. In this instance, the nose of the low level jet oriented itself such that the focus was near and west of the Iowa-Nebraska border. The first storms initiated late in the evening just west of the Missouri River and propagated eastward within an environment of  2000-3000 J/kg MUCAPE, 1000-1300 J/kg DCAPE, and around 30 kts of effective bulk shear. Generally speaking, storms struggled to grow in aerial extent and remain organized. Even so, with the at least marginally supportive environment, a handful of damaging wind reports were received, primarily affecting Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported a large, healthy, tree was split in the 160 block of 30th Ave. As a result, a limb greater than 6 inches in diameter was severed from the tree. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold frontal boundary initiated storms overnight across portions of north central and northeast South Dakota that congealed into a quasi-linear convective system. That complex of storms moved east southeast into northwest Iowa and portions of central Iowa through the mid-morning hours of the 18th. As the storms had moved ahead of the best forcing/parent boundary storms remained sub-severe and weakened through the morning and into the early afternoon. As some semblance of diurnal recovery occurred, a few storms were able to rejuvenate and produce a handful marginally severe to severe wind and hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported tree branches snapped off and estimated winds around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for strong to severe storms took place on the 24th of September. A cold front entered the state from the northwest during the late afternoon and early evening hours, entering an environment with strong low level moisture inflow, SBCAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear around 40 kts. With frontal forcing and the aforementioned conditions, storms were of little question and the main questions became whether or not severe weather would present itself. Additionally, relatively steep low level lapse rates around 7 to 8 degC/km, around 200 m2/s2 0-1km SR Helicity, and DCAPE around and in excess of 1000 J/kg hinted at increased risks for funnel clouds and damaging winds.||Ultimately, widespread severe weather was avoided, but a handful of storms were able to produce funnel clouds, damaging winds, and localized water issues from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter observed a funnel cloud NW of Sac City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 971mb bomb cyclone moved out of the central Rockies on Wednesday, March 13, 2019 and helped to create widespread, moderate to major, and in many cases historic, flooding across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. From 4 to 15 inches of snow cover remained across the mid Missouri River valley, and the ground was frozen with existing frost depths of 15 to 23 inches. Warm temperatures allowed all of the snow to rapidly melt and record moisture allowed 1 to 2.5 inches of rain to fall over a 48 hour period.  Due to the frozen ground, and that 1 to 2 feet of thick ice remained in area rivers, widespread, and in many cases, catastrophic flooding developed. Due to expansive levee breaches on many rivers and streams, the flood damage was particularly devastating to many communities. Four individuals lost their lives due to the extreme conditions, and several hundred people required rescue via air or boat. Tens of thousands of people were evacuated from their homes or businesses. Nearly 50 levees were breached on the Platte, Elkhorn and Missouri Rivers due to the large volume of water. One such levee breach along the Platte River near Leshara, Nebraska forced the evacuation of National Weather Service Omaha, located in Valley, Nebraska for 8 days. Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts presented US$1.3 billion preliminary damage estimates for the entire state, including specific sector losses of: $439 million infrastructure (including roads and bridges), $85 million private homes and businesses, $400 million livestock and $440 million crop. About 2,000 miles of state roads including 15 Nebraska state highway bridges and numerous local bridges, roughly 20 percent of the entire state road system were impacted. Iowa Governor Kim Reynolds provided a $1.6 billion preliminary damage estimate due to flooding across the state. U.S. Interstate 29, extending north-south along the Missouri River, was inundated and partially destroyed, as was the Interstate 680 bypass around Council Bluffs-Omaha metropolitan area. Also noteworthy was damage to Offutt Air Force Base located near Bellevue, Nebraska. One-third of the base���s main runway was inundated, along with one-third of Offutt���s buildings, with several hundred employees, and several families displaced. Preliminary damage estimates released by the Department of Defense are on the order of $450 million. Nebraska and Iowa severe flooding impacts culminated in Federal Disaster Declaration 4420.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thurman was evacuated due to a levee break on the Missouri River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported storm total heavy rainfall of 2.5 inches. Beginning and ending times estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.15 inches over the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux and Little Sioux Rivers. For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Big Sioux River near Brandon (gage site BBSS2) remained in flood through the month. After an early month crest at 3.43 feet above flood stage on May 1, the river fell back to minor flood stage before late month rains pushed the river to a crest into moderate flood stage at 4.90 feet above flood stage on May 31. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter hits continued for the Iowa area, with yet another system moving through and dropping inches of snowfall across a wide area. In this instance, the storm system under performed expectations compared to initial forecasts, as seen with a widespread winter storm warning of which only about one third to one half of the counties verified. ||The general synoptic conditions were similar to previous events, with a mature upper level trough pivoting through, accompanied by 850mb low, and surface low features. Winds were also a non-issue during the event. One of the primary distinctions with this system, versus the system just a few days prior that outperformed expectations, were the tracks of the 850mb and surface features. In this instance, both tracked much further north, predominantly across northwest Iowa. As a result, the areas of best prolonged light to moderate snowfall resided over northern Iowa and areas further north. The resulting snowfall ranged from around 6 to 9 inches across northern Iowa, 3 to 5 inches through central Iowa, and 2 to 4 inches across southern Iowa.

February continued to remind Iowans that the mild start to winter (Dec into early Jan) did not mean the latter half of winter would continue to be mild. In this instance, a deep 500 mb trough was maturing through the central third of the United States. The mid-level and surface low pressure features developed in the Oklahoma Panhandle region on the 11th of February and proceeded to slide and deepen as they moved northeast and into the area late on the 11th and on through the 12th. Their tracks generally took them just to the southeast of the state, putting much of eastern half of Iowa in line to see inches of snowfall, gusty winds, and falling temperatures. ||As relatively warm and moist air rode the warm conveyor belt around the low pressure system, a number of areas from roughly Interstate 35 on east experienced periods of moderate snowfall that resulted in widespread 5 to 8 inches reports. As the snowfall began to wind down in magnitude on the morning of the 12th, winds picked up and carried on through the day with sustained winds often in the 25 to 30 mph range and gusts pushing 40 mph.

Most of northeast Iowa was covered with another six inches or more of snow from a storm system that passed across the region from late on February 19th into the 20th. Most snowfall amounts were in the 6 to 9 inch range with the highest reported total being 9.5 inches near Nora Springs (Floyd County). Numerous school districts utilized two hour late starts on the 21st following the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that around 6 inches of snow fell across Howard County. The highest reported total was 6 inches in Cresco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for strong to severe storms took place on the 24th of September. A cold front entered the state from the northwest during the late afternoon and early evening hours, entering an environment with strong low level moisture inflow, SBCAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear around 40 kts. With frontal forcing and the aforementioned conditions, storms were of little question and the main questions became whether or not severe weather would present itself. Additionally, relatively steep low level lapse rates around 7 to 8 degC/km, around 200 m2/s2 0-1km SR Helicity, and DCAPE around and in excess of 1000 J/kg hinted at increased risks for funnel clouds and damaging winds.||Ultimately, widespread severe weather was avoided, but a handful of storms were able to produce funnel clouds, damaging winds, and localized water issues from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager estimated wind speeds 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

A winter storm spread freezing rain and sleet across the area during the afternoon and night of Tuesday, February 5th. Ice accumulations of a light glaze to over one quarter inch were found across parts of eastern Iowa, northern and west central Illinois, and far northeast Missouri. Some sleet accumulations amounted to around a half inch.  In addition, some thunderstorms were also observed; some of which produced small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation on elevated surfaces measured 0.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread freezing rain, sleet, and rain to the region Thursday morning February, 7th across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri. This event fell on the heels of another freezing rain event on Feb. 5th.  Slick roads, driveways, and sidewalks were common with numerous accidents on major highways. Ice accumulations between a tenth and two tenths of an inch were common. Some higher ice amounts were reported in Stephenson county in Illinois.  ||Strong winds and cold air were found behind this storm with winds gusting between 35 and 50 mph. This caused many tree limbs to fall due to the weight of the ice. Widespread power line damage occurred in southeast Iowa, and northwest Illinois due to the strong west winds and ice accumulation from both the event on February 5 and the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two tenths of an inch of ice was measured on trees by National Weather Services staff at the Davenport Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought heavy rain and strong winds to all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and northeast Missouri. On the back side of this storm, high winds swept through the region, with wind gusts over 60 mph common over locations generally along and north of Interstate 80 in Iowa and Illinois. This long period of high winds brought large amounts of blowing snow from over 30 miles away from where it fell in northeast Iowa. Even though it only snowed a very small amount, well under 1 inch in northeast Iowa, this blowing snow became so significant that ground blizzard conditions took place the morning and afternoon of February 24th. North to south roads in Buchanan and Delaware counties in Iowa became impassable due to the blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management indicated the minor blowing snow issues earlier in the morning with high winds had now increased to continuous white out conditions. This further deteriorated to have snow drifts close north south rural highways in the county. This snow arrived from locations well northwest of the county line, and this is the reason conditions got worse, well after the precipitation had locally ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong winter storm moved through the region on February 19-20, 2019. Snowfall amounts of 1 to 5 inches occurred. Winds remained on the lighter side at 10 to 15 mph, initially from the southeast, but eventually changing to the west. A winter storm watch was issued Monday afternoon, with the entire area upgraded to a winter storm warning early in the morning on Tuesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observers reported snow up to 3.5 inches.

A winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest and brought a round of snow to northeast Iowa from February 11th into the 12th. Snowfall amounts ranged from around 5 inches up to 11 inches. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches near Dorchester (Allamakee County). As the storm exited the region, the winds increased and caused blowing and drifting snow to occur that resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Most schools were closed on February 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 7 and 11 inches of snow fell across Allamakee County. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches near Dorchester. As the snow was ending, the winds increased and created blowing and drifting snow that resulted in hazardous travel conditions.

A mixed precipitation winter storm swept through the Plains, bringing about periods of rain, freezing rain, and snowfall along with strong winds and at least brief blizzard conditions. Synoptically, an open 500 mb wave pivoted through the central and upper Plains, with 850 mb and surface low pressure reflections that traveled from southwest to northeast through Iowa and Missouri-Illinois respectively. Given Iowa's primarily location north of the surface and 850 mb lows, consistent moisture and moisture transport was difficult to come by and led to light precipitation primarily in the forms of freezing drizzle/light rain, and snowfall. A tight surface pressure gradient on the backside of the departing surface low produced prolonged periods of winds in excess of 30 mph across areas of northern Iowa, including one gust to 58 mph at the Mason City airport during the evening hours. As a result, periods of blizzard or near-blizzard conditions prevailed.

A winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest and brought a round of snow to northeast Iowa from February 11th into the 12th. Snowfall amounts ranged from around 5 inches up to 11 inches. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches near Dorchester (Allamakee County). As the storm exited the region, the winds increased and caused blowing and drifting snow to occur that resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Most schools were closed on February 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 5 and 6 inches of snow fell across Floyd County. The highest reported total was 6 inches in Charles City. As the snow was ending, the winds increased and created blowing and drifting snow that resulted in hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Works are writtien.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Rock Island Lock and Dam 15 began the month above its major flood stage of 18 feet. The river stage at the beginning of the month was 21.10 feet. The river would rise to a crest of 22.70 feet at 450 PM CST on May 3rd. This crest is a new all time highest crest for the Mississippi River at Rock Island. This broke the old record of 22.63 feet that had previously occurred on July 9, 1993. The river fell below major flood stage around noon on May 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Works are writtien.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Burlington (BRLI4)  began the month above its major flood stage of 18 feet and continued to see major flooding through the end of the month, with only one short period on the 17th, where it dipped back down to near 18 feet. The river stage at the beginning of the month was 20.4 feet.  The river would rise to a crest of 23.54 feet on May 3rd. This crest is preliminary the 4th highest crest. The river rose to a even higher crest in early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a heavy rain event February 23rd, rising river water and warming temperatures helped create an ice jam that resulted in major flood stage being reached on the Skunk River at Augusta, Iowa. This flood mainly impacted gravel roads near the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Des Moines County emergency management reported an ice jam near Augusta on the Skunk River. The official river gage at Augusta, Iowa reported the river exceeded major flood stage of 21 feet at 4AM, February 24th, and crested at 21.9 feet at 1130 PM, February 25th, then fell below 21 feet again at 330 PM, February 26th.

A relatively broad upper, maturing upper level trough, moved across the upper Plains on the 16th and 17th, providing the opportunity for widespread winter weather. Further down the atmosphere, and 850mb low pressure center tracked across southwest and southern Iowa, predominantly in the Saturday night through Sunday evening time frame. At the surface, a rather diffuse surface low tracked across central Missouri and on through central and southern Illinois. Surface and low-level temperatures were of little issue with both well below freezing. Winds were of little to no issue as well with sustained wind values around 10 mph or less during much of the event. ||All together, conditions, while not extreme, provided the opportunity for prolonged light to moderate snow across much of the southern half to two thirds of Iowa due to persistent warm air advection/wrap-around precipitation around the surface and mid-level low pressure centers. Resulting snowfall amounts generally ranged from around 4 to 10 inches, with heaviest bands between the Highway 30 and Highway 34 corridors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary front found it self draped across portions of northern Missouri and southern and southeast Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 27th. Fortunately, given the evening event, severe weather was fairly limited, yielding larger concerns for heavy rainfall. Environmentally, MUCAPE values were around 1000 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 40-50 kts and fading over time, precipitable water values around 1.5 to 1.8 inches, and at least moderate moisture transport into the area. While the best conditions for any severe weather potential and excessive rainfall were down into northern Missouri, areas of southern Iowa saw multiple rounds of showers and storms during the evening and overnight, resulting in heavy rainfall around 2 to 3 inches. Fortunately, little to no water issues were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.40 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved from the southern Plains into the Great Lakes Monday, February 11 into Tuesday, February 12, bringing snow, freezing rain and some sleet to the area. Significant snow accumulations of 6 to 9 inches fell across portions of northeast Iowa and far northwest Illinois. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from a light glaze to around two tenths of an inch, especially in parts of northwest Illinois. ||This storm was preceded by two other icing events, on February 5th, and 7th.  The storm on the 12th combined with preexisting ice and strong west winds between 40 and 50 mph to cause rather widespread tree limb, power pole, and power line damage across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS COOP observer reported 8.7 inches of snow 2 miles west of Marion, Iowa. Other reports of 6.5 to 8 inches were recieved in Linn county.

Winter continued its grip on Iowa, with persistent cold air advection after the departure of a combination of blizzard conditions, freezing rain, and blowing snow. As the parent surface low and 500 mb trough departed the area, through the 7th of February, gusty northwest winds to 20 to 25+ mph ushered in a push of Arctic air that peaked overnight on the 7th into the morning of the 8th. As a result, morning lows dropped down to around -10 degrees F across portions of northern and northwest Iowa, while near zero to below zero temperatures prevailed all the way to the Iowa-Missouri border.  While the surface pressure gradient and resulting winds relaxed through the evening of the 7th, the bitter cold did not require strong winds to result in persistent -25 to -35 degree F wind chill values overnight and into the morning hours. Winds in the 10 to 20 mph range were sufficient to achieve the previously mentioned wind chill values.  Incoming surface high pressure continued to relax winds, allowing wind chill values to warm above the -20 degrees F range or warmer by noon on the 8th.

A 971mb bomb cyclone moved out of the central Rockies on Wednesday, March 13, 2019 and helped to create widespread, moderate to major, and in many cases historic, flooding across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. From 4 to 15 inches of snow cover remained across the mid Missouri River valley, and the ground was frozen with existing frost depths of 15 to 23 inches. Warm temperatures allowed all of the snow to rapidly melt and record moisture allowed 1 to 2.5 inches of rain to fall over a 48 hour period.  Due to the frozen ground, and that 1 to 2 feet of thick ice remained in area rivers, widespread, and in many cases, catastrophic flooding developed. Due to expansive levee breaches on many rivers and streams, the flood damage was particularly devastating to many communities. Four individuals lost their lives due to the extreme conditions, and several hundred people required rescue via air or boat. Tens of thousands of people were evacuated from their homes or businesses. Nearly 50 levees were breached on the Platte, Elkhorn and Missouri Rivers due to the large volume of water. One such levee breach along the Platte River near Leshara, Nebraska forced the evacuation of National Weather Service Omaha, located in Valley, Nebraska for 8 days. Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts presented US$1.3 billion preliminary damage estimates for the entire state, including specific sector losses of: $439 million infrastructure (including roads and bridges), $85 million private homes and businesses, $400 million livestock and $440 million crop. About 2,000 miles of state roads including 15 Nebraska state highway bridges and numerous local bridges, roughly 20 percent of the entire state road system were impacted. Iowa Governor Kim Reynolds provided a $1.6 billion preliminary damage estimate due to flooding across the state. U.S. Interstate 29, extending north-south along the Missouri River, was inundated and partially destroyed, as was the Interstate 680 bypass around Council Bluffs-Omaha metropolitan area. Also noteworthy was damage to Offutt Air Force Base located near Bellevue, Nebraska. One-third of the base���s main runway was inundated, along with one-third of Offutt���s buildings, with several hundred employees, and several families displaced. Preliminary damage estimates released by the Department of Defense are on the order of $450 million. Nebraska and Iowa severe flooding impacts culminated in Federal Disaster Declaration 4420.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thiere was for flooding along the Boyer River. The river gauge at Logan, operated by the USGS, peaked at 25.33 feet at 7 pm on March 13th. Major flood stage is 25 feet. Preliminary information shows this peak is the highest on record.  Portions of the town of Missouri Valley flooded from the flood. Highway 30 closed in Woodbine. A levee topped by floodwaters at 296th street in Missouri Valley, on the east side of the levee around 2 miles northeast of town. Also 2 breaches in the levee around Willow Creek. Evacuations took place in Missouri Valley.  Property damage totals are for the entire county from flooding of the Boyer River, Willow Creek, and Missouri River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another week, another winter storm system slated to affect the region. In this instance, a maturing 500 mb trough was pivoting through the region, planting Iowa firmly within its cross hairs and in subsequent strong northwest flow in its wake. At the mid and low levels, low pressure center tracks predominantly traversed southern/southeast Iowa from the afternoon of the 23rd and on into the 24th before continuing to track northeast into Wisconsin and across Lake Michigan. The surface low deepened throughout its journey, predominantly in the 994 to 990 mb range while in Iowa, before deepening to the low 980s by the time it reached Lake Michigan.  Its continued strengthening and tight gradient on its backside led to a prolong period of strong northwest winds, including sustained values in excess of 30 to 35 mph and gusts pushing 45 to 50 mph at times. A few gusts to 55 mph or higher reported through the event. All the while, widespread 8 to 12 or more inches of snow had fallen or were just ending as the strong winds began to take hold and severely affect conditions. Throughout the period of strongest winds, drifts many feet high (up to 8 feet in spots on I-35) formed across the warned area and dropped visibilities to near zero regularly. Many reports of thundersnow and visibility in some towns down to just blocks or less. Widespread tow bans, even road closures, were seen, as well as numerous accidents and rescues of both passenger vehicles and semi-trailers. Traffic was reported down to speeds of around 20 mph or so on I-35 near Clear Lake before the interstate was closed. Much of Interstate 35 between Ames and the Minnesota border did not open until late in the day Monday, the 25th after blizzard conditions ended late Sunday, the 24th. Besides the large travel impacts, this storm impacted farmers as well. One sheep breeder near Mason City had a barn roof collapse in two spots from the wind and snow. Some sheep were still trapped in the barn and had to be rescued. Two-thirds of the flock were lost when the barn collapsed. It took many hours to dig out the barn and rescue the remaining animals.

At the mid and low levels, low pressure center tracks predominantly traversed southern/southeast Iowa from the afternoon of the 23rd and on into the 24th before continuing to track northeast into Wisconsin and across Lake Michigan. The surface low deepened throughout its journey, predominantly in the 994 to 990 mb range while in Iowa, before deepening to the low 980s by the time it reached Lake Michigan.  Its continued strengthening and tight gradient on its backside led to a prolong period of strong northwest winds, including sustained values in excess of 30 to 35 mph and gusts pushing 45 to 50 mph at times. A few gusts to 55 mph or higher reported through the event. All the while, widespread 8 to 12 or more inches of snow had fallen or were just ending as the strong winds began to take hold and severely affect conditions. Throughout the period of strongest winds, drifts many feet high (up to 8 feet in spots on I-35) formed across the warned area and dropped visibilities to near zero regularly. Many reports of thundersnow and visibility in some towns down to just blocks or less. Widespread tow bans, even road closures, were seen, as well as numerous accidents and rescues of both passenger vehicles and semi-trailers. Traffic was reported down to speeds of around 20 mph or so on I-35 near Clear Lake before the interstate was closed. Much of Interstate 35 between Ames and the Minnesota border did not open until late in the day Monday, the 25th after blizzard conditions ended late Sunday, the 24th. Besides the large travel impacts, this storm impacted farmers as well. One sheep breeder near Mason City had a barn roof collapse in two spots from the wind and snow. Some sheep were still trapped in the barn and had to be rescued. Two-thirds of the flock were lost when the barn collapsed. It took many hours to dig out the barn and rescue the remaining animals.

After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

A winter storm moved from the southern Plains into the Great Lakes Monday, February 11 into Tuesday, February 12, bringing snow, freezing rain and some sleet to the area. Significant snow accumulations of 6 to 9 inches fell across portions of northeast Iowa and far northwest Illinois. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from a light glaze to around two tenths of an inch, especially in parts of northwest Illinois. ||This storm was preceded by two other icing events, on February 5th, and 7th.  The storm on the 12th combined with preexisting ice and strong west winds between 40 and 50 mph to cause rather widespread tree limb, power pole, and power line damage across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official weather observers reported 7.5 inches of snow 2 mile west of the Dubuque Regional Airport.  Significant blowing and drifting snow was widespread over the area with white out conditions at times lingering through the day of February 12. Power outages from down power lines, power poles, and tree branch damage were widespread.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter continued its grip on Iowa, with persistent cold air advection after the departure of a combination of blizzard conditions, freezing rain, and blowing snow. As the parent surface low and 500 mb trough departed the area, through the 7th of February, gusty northwest winds to 20 to 25+ mph ushered in a push of Arctic air that peaked overnight on the 7th into the morning of the 8th. As a result, morning lows dropped down to around -10 degrees F across portions of northern and northwest Iowa, while near zero to below zero temperatures prevailed all the way to the Iowa-Missouri border.  While the surface pressure gradient and resulting winds relaxed through the evening of the 7th, the bitter cold did not require strong winds to result in persistent -25 to -35 degree F wind chill values overnight and into the morning hours. Winds in the 10 to 20 mph range were sufficient to achieve the previously mentioned wind chill values.  Incoming surface high pressure continued to relax winds, allowing wind chill values to warm above the -20 degrees F range or warmer by noon on the 8th.

A relatively broad upper, maturing upper level trough, moved across the upper Plains on the 16th and 17th, providing the opportunity for widespread winter weather. Further down the atmosphere, and 850mb low pressure center tracked across southwest and southern Iowa, predominantly in the Saturday night through Sunday evening time frame. At the surface, a rather diffuse surface low tracked across central Missouri and on through central and southern Illinois. Surface and low-level temperatures were of little issue with both well below freezing. Winds were of little to no issue as well with sustained wind values around 10 mph or less during much of the event. ||All together, conditions, while not extreme, provided the opportunity for prolonged light to moderate snow across much of the southern half to two thirds of Iowa due to persistent warm air advection/wrap-around precipitation around the surface and mid-level low pressure centers. Resulting snowfall amounts generally ranged from around 4 to 10 inches, with heaviest bands between the Highway 30 and Highway 34 corridors.

Throughout the period of strongest winds, drifts many feet high (up to 8 feet in spots on I-35) formed across the warned area and dropped visibilities to near zero regularly. Many reports of thundersnow and visibility in some towns down to just blocks or less. Widespread tow bans, even road closures, were seen, as well as numerous accidents and rescues of both passenger vehicles and semi-trailers. Traffic was reported down to speeds of around 20 mph or so on I-35 near Clear Lake before the interstate was closed. Much of Interstate 35 between Ames and the Minnesota border did not open until late in the day Monday, the 25th after blizzard conditions ended late Sunday, the 24th. Besides the large travel impacts, this storm impacted farmers as well. One sheep breeder near Mason City had a barn roof collapse in two spots from the wind and snow. Some sheep were still trapped in the barn and had to be rescued. Two-thirds of the flock were lost when the barn collapsed. It took many hours to dig out the barn and rescue the remaining animals.

Another week, another winter storm system slated to affect the region. In this instance, a maturing 500 mb trough was pivoting through the region, planting Iowa firmly within its cross hairs and in subsequent strong northwest flow in its wake. At the mid and low levels, low pressure center tracks predominantly traversed southern/southeast Iowa from the afternoon of the 23rd and on into the 24th before continuing to track northeast into Wisconsin and across Lake Michigan. The surface low deepened throughout its journey, predominantly in the 994 to 990 mb range while in Iowa, before deepening to the low 980s by the time it reached Lake Michigan.  Its continued strengthening and tight gradient on its backside led to a prolong period of strong northwest winds, including sustained values in excess of 30 to 35 mph and gusts pushing 45 to 50 mph at times. A surface low pressure system developed on the lee side of the Rockies late November 25th and November 26th. As it departed the front range, it moved eastward through Kansas before turning northeasterly into Iowa and the Great Lakes Region during the evening of the 26th and through the 27th. While substantially cold temperatures were not in place within the area or north of the area, it was cold enough for snow to be the primary precipitation type across much of northwest and northern Iowa. Areas further south remained warm enough to see predominantly rain. As the system departed, it also provided a number of hours of strong gusty winds due to a tight surface pressure gradient on the backside of the surface low. With around 2 to 6 inches of snowfall and winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, travel was hazardous at times, including significantly reduced visibilities. Fortunately, much of the worst conditions occurred during the evening and overnight hours.

A winter storm moved from the southern Plains into the Great Lakes Monday, February 11 into Tuesday, February 12, bringing snow, freezing rain and some sleet to the area. Significant snow accumulations of 6 to 9 inches fell across portions of northeast Iowa and far northwest Illinois. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from a light glaze to around two tenths of an inch, especially in parts of northwest Illinois. ||This storm was preceded by two other icing events, on February 5th, and 7th.  The storm on the 12th combined with preexisting ice and strong west winds between 40 and 50 mph to cause rather widespread tree limb, power pole, and power line damage across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 6 inches of snow in Marengo, Iowa. Significant blowing and drifting snow was widespread over the area with white out conditions at times lingering through the day of February 12. Power outages from down power lines, power poles, and tree branch damage were widespread.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winter continued its grip on Iowa, with persistent cold air advection after the departure of a combination of blizzard conditions, freezing rain, and blowing snow. As the parent surface low and 500 mb trough departed the area, through the 7th of February, gusty northwest winds to 20 to 25+ mph ushered in a push of Arctic air that peaked overnight on the 7th into the morning of the 8th. As a result, morning lows dropped down to around -10 degrees F across portions of northern and northwest Iowa, while near zero to below zero temperatures prevailed all the way to the Iowa-Missouri border.  While the surface pressure gradient and resulting winds relaxed through the evening of the 7th, the bitter cold did not require strong winds to result in persistent -25 to -35 degree F wind chill values overnight and into the morning hours. Winds in the 10 to 20 mph range were sufficient to achieve the previously mentioned wind chill values.  Incoming surface high pressure continued to relax winds, allowing wind chill values to warm above the -20 degrees F range or warmer by noon on the 8th.

A relatively broad upper, maturing upper level trough, moved across the upper Plains on the 16th and 17th, providing the opportunity for widespread winter weather. Further down the atmosphere, and 850mb low pressure center tracked across southwest and southern Iowa, predominantly in the Saturday night through Sunday evening time frame. At the surface, a rather diffuse surface low tracked across central Missouri and on through central and southern Illinois. Surface and low-level temperatures were of little issue with both well below freezing. Winds were of little to no issue as well with sustained wind values around 10 mph or less during much of the event. ||All together, conditions, while not extreme, provided the opportunity for prolonged light to moderate snow across much of the southern half to two thirds of Iowa due to persistent warm air advection/wrap-around precipitation around the surface and mid-level low pressure centers. Resulting snowfall amounts generally ranged from around 4 to 10 inches, with heaviest bands between the Highway 30 and Highway 34 corridors.

Winds in the 10 to 20 mph range were sufficient to achieve the previously mentioned wind chill values.  Incoming surface high pressure continued to relax winds, allowing wind chill values to warm above the -20 degrees F range or warmer by noon on the 8th.

A winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest and brought a round of snow to northeast Iowa from February 11th into the 12th. Snowfall amounts ranged from around 5 inches up to 11 inches. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches near Dorchester (Allamakee County). As the storm exited the region, the winds increased and caused blowing and drifting snow to occur that resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Most schools were closed on February 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 7 and 9 inches of snow fell across Clayton County. The highest reported total was 9.6 inches in Strawberry Point. As the snow was ending, the winds increased and created blowing and drifting snow that resulted in hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system developed on the lee side of the Rockies late November 25th and November 26th. As it departed the front range, it moved eastward through Kansas before turning northeasterly into Iowa and the Great Lakes Region during the evening of the 26th and through the 27th. While substantially cold temperatures were not in place within the area or north of the area, it was cold enough for snow to be the primary precipitation type across much of northwest and northern Iowa. Areas further south remained warm enough to see predominantly rain. As the system departed, it also provided a number of hours of strong gusty winds due to a tight surface pressure gradient on the backside of the surface low. With around 2 to 6 inches of snowfall and winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, travel was hazardous at times, including significantly reduced visibilities. Fortunately, much of the worst conditions occurred during the evening and overnight hours.

A winter storm moved from the southern Plains into the Great Lakes Monday, February 11 into Tuesday, February 12, bringing snow, freezing rain and some sleet to the area. Significant snow accumulations of 6 to 9 inches fell across portions of northeast Iowa and far northwest Illinois. Ice accumulations from freezing rain ranged from a light glaze to around two tenths of an inch, especially in parts of northwest Illinois. ||This storm was preceded by two other icing events, on February 5th, and 7th.  The storm on the 12th combined with preexisting ice and strong west winds between 40 and 50 mph to cause rather widespread tree limb, power pole, and power line damage across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS COOP Observer reported 5 inches of snow in Sigourney, Iowa. Significant blowing and drifting snow was widespread over the area with white out conditions at times lingering through the day of February 12. Power outages from down power lines, power poles, and tree branch damage were widespread.

Winter continued its grip on Iowa, with persistent cold air advection after the departure of a combination of blizzard conditions, freezing rain, and blowing snow. As the parent surface low and 500 mb trough departed the area, through the 7th of February, gusty northwest winds to 20 to 25+ mph ushered in a push of Arctic air that peaked overnight on the 7th into the morning of the 8th. As a result, morning lows dropped down to around -10 degrees F across portions of northern and northwest Iowa, while near zero to below zero temperatures prevailed all the way to the Iowa-Missouri border.  While the surface pressure gradient and resulting winds relaxed through the evening of the 7th, the bitter cold did not require strong winds to result in persistent -25 to -35 degree F wind chill values overnight and into the morning hours. Winds in the 10 to 20 mph range were sufficient to achieve the previously mentioned wind chill values.  Incoming surface high pressure continued to relax winds, allowing wind chill values to warm above the -20 degrees F range or warmer by noon on the 8th.

A strong winter storm dumped 8 to 12 inches of snow across most of northeast Iowa from February 23rd into the 24th. As the snow was winding down, the winds increased with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph common along with gusts of 45 to 55 mph. This created blizzard conditions for much of the 24th with major drifting, white-outs and numerous road closures. The effects of the blizzard continued into the 25th when most schools were closed. The highest reported snowfall amount was 12 inches in Carpenter (Mitchell County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 55 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport (Fayette County). In New Hampton (Chickasaw County) between 40 and 50 stranded motorists were housed in emergency shelters set up in a local church and museum. A state of emergency was declared in Mitchell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 7 to 8 inches of snow fell across Chickasaw County. As the snow was ending, winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts that created major drifting, white-outs and road closures, including U.S. Highway 63. The impacts from the blizzard conditions caused schools to be closed on February 25th. The highest reported snowfall total was 8 inches in New Hampton. The highest recorded wind gust was 48 mph by a mesonet station near Ionia. Emergency shelters were set up at a local church and museum in New Hampton to house between 40 and 50 stranded motorists.

The highest recorded wind gust was 51 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Charles City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

February continued to remind Iowans that the mild start to winter (Dec into early Jan) did not mean the latter half of winter would continue to be mild. In this instance, a deep 500 mb trough was maturing through the central third of the United States. The mid-level and surface low pressure features developed in the Oklahoma Panhandle region on the 11th of February and proceeded to slide and deepen as they moved northeast and into the area late on the 11th and on through the 12th. Their tracks generally took them just to the southeast of the state, putting much of eastern half of Iowa in line to see inches of snowfall, gusty winds, and falling temperatures. ||As relatively warm and moist air rode the warm conveyor belt around the low pressure system, a number of areas from roughly Interstate 35 on east experienced periods of moderate snowfall that resulted in widespread 5 to 8 inches reports. As the snowfall began to wind down in magnitude on the morning of the 12th, winds picked up and carried on through the day with sustained winds often in the 25 to 30 mph range and gusts pushing 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter hits continued for the Iowa area, with yet another system moving through and dropping inches of snowfall across a wide area. In this instance, the storm system under performed expectations compared to initial forecasts, as seen with a widespread winter storm warning of which only about one third to one half of the counties verified. ||The general synoptic conditions were similar to previous events, with a mature upper level trough pivoting through, accompanied by 850mb low, and surface low features. Winds were also a non-issue during the event. One of the primary distinctions with this system, versus the system just a few days prior that outperformed expectations, were the tracks of the 850mb and surface features. In this instance, both tracked much further north, predominantly across northwest Iowa. As a result, the areas of best prolonged light to moderate snowfall resided over northern Iowa and areas further north. The resulting snowfall ranged from around 6 to 9 inches across northern Iowa, 3 to 5 inches through central Iowa, and 2 to 4 inches across southern Iowa.

The mid-level and surface low pressure features developed in the Oklahoma Panhandle region on the 11th of February and proceeded to slide and deepen as they moved northeast and into the area late on the 11th and on through the 12th. Their tracks generally took them just to the southeast of the state, putting much of eastern half of Iowa in line to see inches of snowfall, gusty winds, and falling temperatures. ||As relatively warm and moist air rode the warm conveyor belt around the low pressure system, a number of areas from roughly Interstate 35 on east experienced periods of moderate snowfall that resulted in widespread 5 to 8 inches reports. As the snowfall began to wind down in magnitude on the morning of the 12th, winds picked up and carried on through the day with sustained winds often in the 25 to 30 mph range and gusts pushing 40 mph.

A relatively broad upper, maturing upper level trough, moved across the upper Plains on the 16th and 17th, providing the opportunity for widespread winter weather. Further down the atmosphere, and 850mb low pressure center tracked across southwest and southern Iowa, predominantly in the Saturday night through Sunday evening time frame. At the surface, a rather diffuse surface low tracked across central Missouri and on through central and southern Illinois. Surface and low-level temperatures were of little issue with both well below freezing. Winds were of little to no issue as well with sustained wind values around 10 mph or less during much of the event. ||All together, conditions, while not extreme, provided the opportunity for prolonged light to moderate snow across much of the southern half to two thirds of Iowa due to persistent warm air advection/wrap-around precipitation around the surface and mid-level low pressure centers. Resulting snowfall amounts generally ranged from around 4 to 10 inches, with heaviest bands between the Highway 30 and Highway 34 corridors.

A strong winter storm dumped 8 to 12 inches of snow across most of northeast Iowa from February 23rd into the 24th. As the snow was winding down, the winds increased with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph common along with gusts of 45 to 55 mph. This created blizzard conditions for much of the 24th with major drifting, white-outs and numerous road closures. The effects of the blizzard continued into the 25th when most schools were closed. The highest reported snowfall amount was 12 inches in Carpenter (Mitchell County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 55 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport (Fayette County). In New Hampton (Chickasaw County) between 40 and 50 stranded motorists were housed in emergency shelters set up in a local church and museum. A state of emergency was declared in Mitchell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 9 to 12 inches of snow fell across Mitchell County. As the snow was ending, winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts that created major drifting, white-outs and road closures, including U.S. Highway 218 and State Highway 9. The impacts from the blizzard conditions caused schools to be closed on February 25th. A state of emergency was declared for the county. The highest reported snowfall total was 12 inches in Carpenter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More snow moved through the area during starting during the afternoon on Saturday, February 16 and lingering into Sunday morning February 17. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 9.5 inches occurred. Winds remained on the lighter side during the event generally less than 15 mph.  A winter weather advisory was issued for the entire area on Friday, and a few counties were upgraded to a winter storm warning Saturday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported up to 6.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter hits continued for the Iowa area, with yet another system moving through and dropping inches of snowfall across a wide area. In this instance, the storm system under performed expectations compared to initial forecasts, as seen with a widespread winter storm warning of which only about one third to one half of the counties verified. ||The general synoptic conditions were similar to previous events, with a mature upper level trough pivoting through, accompanied by 850mb low, and surface low features. Winds were also a non-issue during the event. One of the primary distinctions with this system, versus the system just a few days prior that outperformed expectations, were the tracks of the 850mb and surface features. In this instance, both tracked much further north, predominantly across northwest Iowa. As a result, the areas of best prolonged light to moderate snowfall resided over northern Iowa and areas further north. The resulting snowfall ranged from around 6 to 9 inches across northern Iowa, 3 to 5 inches through central Iowa, and 2 to 4 inches across southern Iowa.

A strong storm moved through the region, bringing mixed wintry precipitation, heavy snow, and very strong winds. The storm created widespread blizzard conditions and 6 to 12 inches of snow. 24 hours of freezing fog, freezing rain, and a wintry mix led up to the blizzard, which compounded travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearby counties reported one quarter mile visibility and wind gusts 35 to 50 mph which created whiteout conditions. 4 to 8 inches of snow was reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 971mb bomb cyclone moved out of the central Rockies on Wednesday, March 13, 2019 and helped to create widespread, moderate to major, and in many cases historic, flooding across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. From 4 to 15 inches of snow cover remained across the mid Missouri River valley, and the ground was frozen with existing frost depths of 15 to 23 inches. Warm temperatures allowed all of the snow to rapidly melt and record moisture allowed 1 to 2.5 inches of rain to fall over a 48 hour period.  Due to the frozen ground, and that 1 to 2 feet of thick ice remained in area rivers, widespread, and in many cases, catastrophic flooding developed. Due to expansive levee breaches on many rivers and streams, the flood damage was particularly devastating to many communities. Four individuals lost their lives due to the extreme conditions, and several hundred people required rescue via air or boat. Tens of thousands of people were evacuated from their homes or businesses. Nearly 50 levees were breached on the Platte, Elkhorn and Missouri Rivers due to the large volume of water. One such levee breach along the Platte River near Leshara, Nebraska forced the evacuation of National Weather Service Omaha, located in Valley, Nebraska for 8 days. Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts presented US$1.3 billion preliminary damage estimates for the entire state, including specific sector losses of: $439 million infrastructure (including roads and bridges), $85 million private homes and businesses, $400 million livestock and $440 million crop. About 2,000 miles of state roads including 15 Nebraska state highway bridges and numerous local bridges, roughly 20 percent of the entire state road system were impacted. Iowa Governor Kim Reynolds provided a $1.6 billion preliminary damage estimate due to flooding across the state. U.S. Interstate 29, extending north-south along the Missouri River, was inundated and partially destroyed, as was the Interstate 680 bypass around Council Bluffs-Omaha metropolitan area. Also noteworthy was damage to Offutt Air Force Base located near Bellevue, Nebraska. One-third of the base���s main runway was inundated, along with one-third of Offutt���s buildings, with several hundred employees, and several families displaced. Preliminary damage estimates released by the Department of Defense are on the order of $450 million. Nebraska and Iowa severe flooding impacts culminated in Federal Disaster Declaration 4420.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt caused the Soldier River to start flooding. IA 141 closed in both directions. County road L32, 1 mile west of the Ute area, and county road L61 near Ute also closed. Flood damage totals for the county are in the Maple Creek flooding entry below.
