EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration snow event occurred in Iowa and northern Illinois, from the evening of February 16th, through the  evening of the 17th.  A slow moving winter storm system spread light to moderate snow across the region from late Saturday evening (2/16) into the evening hours of Sunday (2/17). Snowfall amounts of 2 to 6 inches fell across much of the area, with the higher amounts occurring further west in west central and central Iowa where 6 to 9 inches of snow fell.  Snowfall totals between a half inch and 2 inches were reported over portions of northeast Missouri, southeast Iowa, and west central Illinois. Snow-covered and slick roads were common, with minor blowing and drifting snow in some open areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS COOP reported  8.1 inches of snow in North Liberty Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread freezing rain, sleet, and rain to the region Thursday morning February, 7th across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri. This event fell on the heels of another freezing rain event on Feb. 5th.  Slick roads, driveways, and sidewalks were common with numerous accidents on major highways. Ice accumulations between a tenth and two tenths of an inch were common. Some higher ice amounts were reported in Stephenson county in Illinois.  ||Strong winds and cold air were found behind this storm with winds gusting between 35 and 50 mph. This caused many tree limbs to fall due to the weight of the ice. Widespread power line damage occurred in southeast Iowa, and northwest Illinois due to the strong west winds and ice accumulation from both the event on February 5 and the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported that freezing rain created slick traveling conditions, 1 mile north of Lowden.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Le Claire (LECI4) began the month above its major flood stage of 13.5 feet and reached a crest level of 16.51 feet at 1030 AM May 2nd.  The river fell below major flood stage in the morning on May 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought heavy rain and strong winds to all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and northeast Missouri. On the back side of this storm, high winds swept through the region, with wind gusts over 60 mph common over locations generally along and north of Interstate 80 in Iowa and Illinois. This long period of high winds brought large amounts of blowing snow from over 30 miles away from where it fell in northeast Iowa. Even though it only snowed a very small amount, well under 1 inch in northeast Iowa, this blowing snow became so significant that ground blizzard conditions took place the morning and afternoon of February 24th. North to south roads in Buchanan and Delaware counties in Iowa became impassable due to the blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 62 mph was reported as part of a long duration high wind event.

A surface low pressure system developed on the lee side of the Rockies late November 25th and November 26th. As it departed the front range, it moved eastward through Kansas before turning northeasterly into Iowa and the Great Lakes Region during the evening of the 26th and through the 27th. While substantially cold temperatures were not in place within the area or north of the area, it was cold enough for snow to be the primary precipitation type across much of northwest and northern Iowa. Areas further south remained warm enough to see predominantly rain. As the system departed, it also provided a number of hours of strong gusty winds due to a tight surface pressure gradient on the backside of the surface low. With around 2 to 6 inches of snowfall and winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, travel was hazardous at times, including significantly reduced visibilities. Fortunately, much of the worst conditions occurred during the evening and overnight hours.

This long period of high winds brought large amounts of blowing snow from over 30 miles away from where it fell in northeast Iowa. Even though it only snowed a very small amount, well under 1 inch in northeast Iowa, this blowing snow became so significant that ground blizzard conditions took place the morning and afternoon of February 24th. North to south roads in Buchanan and Delaware counties in Iowa became impassable due to the blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread reported winds in this area were 45 mph sustained with gusts to 60 mph were reported as part of a long duration high wind event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter hits continued for the Iowa area, with yet another system moving through and dropping inches of snowfall across a wide area. In this instance, the storm system under performed expectations compared to initial forecasts, as seen with a widespread winter storm warning of which only about one third to one half of the counties verified. ||The general synoptic conditions were similar to previous events, with a mature upper level trough pivoting through, accompanied by 850mb low, and surface low features. Winds were also a non-issue during the event. One of the primary distinctions with this system, versus the system just a few days prior that outperformed expectations, were the tracks of the 850mb and surface features. In this instance, both tracked much further north, predominantly across northwest Iowa. As a result, the areas of best prolonged light to moderate snowfall resided over northern Iowa and areas further north. The resulting snowfall ranged from around 6 to 9 inches across northern Iowa, 3 to 5 inches through central Iowa, and 2 to 4 inches across southern Iowa.

February continued to remind Iowans that the mild start to winter (Dec into early Jan) did not mean the latter half of winter would continue to be mild. In this instance, a deep 500 mb trough was maturing through the central third of the United States. The mid-level and surface low pressure features developed in the Oklahoma Panhandle region on the 11th of February and proceeded to slide and deepen as they moved northeast and into the area late on the 11th and on through the 12th. Their tracks generally took them just to the southeast of the state, putting much of eastern half of Iowa in line to see inches of snowfall, gusty winds, and falling temperatures. ||As relatively warm and moist air rode the warm conveyor belt around the low pressure system, a number of areas from roughly Interstate 35 on east experienced periods of moderate snowfall that resulted in widespread 5 to 8 inches reports. As the snowfall began to wind down in magnitude on the morning of the 12th, winds picked up and carried on through the day with sustained winds often in the 25 to 30 mph range and gusts pushing 40 mph.

Most of northeast Iowa was covered with another six inches or more of snow from a storm system that passed across the region from late on February 19th into the 20th. Most snowfall amounts were in the 6 to 9 inch range with the highest reported total being 9.5 inches near Nora Springs (Floyd County). Numerous school districts utilized two hour late starts on the 21st following the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 7 inches of snow fell across Chickasaw County. The highest reported total was 7.3 inches in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm dumped 8 to 12 inches of snow across most of northeast Iowa from February 23rd into the 24th. As the snow was winding down, the winds increased with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph common along with gusts of 45 to 55 mph. This created blizzard conditions for much of the 24th with major drifting, white-outs and numerous road closures. The effects of the blizzard continued into the 25th when most schools were closed. The highest reported snowfall amount was 12 inches in Carpenter (Mitchell County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 55 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport (Fayette County). In New Hampton (Chickasaw County) between 40 and 50 stranded motorists were housed in emergency shelters set up in a local church and museum. A state of emergency was declared in Mitchell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that up to 4 inches of snow fell across Fayette County. As the snow was ending, winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts that created major drifting, white-outs and road closures, including State Highway 150. The impacts from the blizzard conditions caused schools to be closed on February 25th. The highest recorded wind gust was 55 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy rainfall are not uncommon throughout the spring and summer months, but given the very wet spring across Iowa, soils were primed and unable to hold copious amounts of new rainfall. The overall setup for this event was somewhat typical with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, and 850 mb low level jet aiding in convective initiation and moisture transport, precipitable water values around 1.5 to 1.75 inches, and other marginal supporting factors. ||Convection initiated across portions of southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas late in the evening on the 20th and quickly propagated eastward through the overnight and morning hours. Eventually, two decaying waves of storms moved through portions of western Iowa and predominantly provided small hail and efficient heavy rainfall. A few locations reported as much as 3 to 5 inches of rainfall, which helped initiate flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported road closure due to flooding near the intersection of Easter Ave and 170th St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 25th, a number of storms were able to initiate along a relatively stationary boundary situated across southern Iowa. On the warm side of the boundary surface conditions were in the mid 80s for temperature and upper 60s to low 70s dewpoints.  Additionally, the conditions yielded an environment with 2000-3000+ J/kg SB/MUCAPE, 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear, 500-700 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and precipitable water values around 1.2-1.5 inches.  ||Ultimately, storms developed along the boundary during the mid afternoon hours, initially producing sub-severe conditions. As the initial wave of storms moved eastward, a second wave of development occured during the evening, of which the storms were better able to organize and produce numerous reports of quarter to larger than golf ball sized hail. A few storms were also able to produce damaging winds as they too progressed eastward.  The second wave of storms were aided by the low level jet, initiating a series of training storms over portions of southwest and south central Iowa where copious rainfall produced a small handful of flash flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight convection across south-central and southeast Minnesota began to slide southward along a potent MUCAPE gradient over eastern Iowa. While sub-severe initially, the line of storms formed a QLCS structure and eventually strengthened to the point of producing numerous hail and damaging wind reports as it continued to move southward. ||The convective environment included MUCAPE values ranging from 2000-4000 J/kg MUCAP from east to west and 30 to 40 kts of effective bulk shear nearly orthogonal to the line of storms. Effectively, the storms were able to sustain long enough and produce a cold pool that was able to continue to initiate convection throughout the morning and into the afternoon as it moved out of Iowa and into increasingly more unstable environments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail via the mPING app.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms and heavy rainfall moved up into Iowa from northern Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd of June. Ahead of the storms, a warm front lifted into and through much of the state, bringing with it temperatures into the low to mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Support for storms was decent with MUCAPE values of 2000+ J/kg reaching roughly the Interstate 80 corridor, effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range, and strong low level moisture transport boosting precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches.  ||All in all, a handful of severe storm reports (hail and wind) were seen as storms moved into the state from the south and eventually transitioned more into a rain threat that came to fruition over the eastern half of the Des Moines metro. A number of heavy rainfall amounts in excess of 2 to 4 inches were received, producing multiple flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree and the tree fell on a deck and car in Ankeny.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary severe thunderstorm brought both a damaging wind gust and a isolated area of flash flooding to a small area of Lee County, Iowa, in Keokuk, and also in adjacent areas of Hancock county, Illinois. This was the only severe storm of the afternoon that day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported blown down around the city of Keokuk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the mid morning hours of June 5th, an area of dissipating storms over Iowa, flared up into a single intense supercell thunderstorm, with extremely large hail. This storm produce golf ball to baseball sized hail as it moved through far southeast Iowa and into far northeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported baseball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessively wet spring flooding season continued into early June, with several tributary rivers remaining above major flood status the first days of June, before falling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wapsipinicon River at De Witt 4 South remained above major flood stage of 12.5 feet from the end of May through the early morning hours of June 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessively wet spring flooding season continued into early June, with several tributary rivers remaining above major flood status the first days of June, before falling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Skunk River at Augusta Iowa, remained above major flood stage of 20.0 feet from the end of May through June 2nd, when it fell below 20 feet in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms and heavy rainfall moved up into Iowa from northern Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd of June. Ahead of the storms, a warm front lifted into and through much of the state, bringing with it temperatures into the low to mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Support for storms was decent with MUCAPE values of 2000+ J/kg reaching roughly the Interstate 80 corridor, effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range, and strong low level moisture transport boosting precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches.  ||All in all, a handful of severe storm reports (hail and wind) were seen as storms moved into the state from the south and eventually transitioned more into a rain threat that came to fruition over the eastern half of the Des Moines metro. A number of heavy rainfall amounts in excess of 2 to 4 inches were received, producing multiple flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported estimated 60 mph winds and pea sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.23 inches over the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a moderate to heavy rainfall event providing 2 to 3 inches just prior to this, the pump was primed so to speak for flooding potential. A generally stationary front resided south of the area into Missouri, but upper level and mid level flow strongly suggested storms would track north of the surface boundary and across portions of southern and central Iowa. The initial wave of rain was initiated by a mid-upper level short wave riding the parent flow, triggering storms in the Oklahoma-Kansas region by late morning and traveled northeast throughout the day, eventually reaching Iowa by the late afternoon and evening. The initial round exited and subsided by midnight, only to see a second round initiated by the low level jet back in northeast and central Kansas. That second round made its way into southern Iowa by around 6 am and continued throughout the morning. When all said and done, heavy rainfall in excess of 5 inches was reported in a few locations, as well as multiple reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 1.50 inches from 8am to 3:30pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level storm system tracked across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri Thursday night and Friday morning.  This system brought a long duration snow event to the region, with totals mainly between 2 and 6 inches.  This was also the most snow to ever fall on Halloween for much of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer in Clinton reported 3.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary front found it self draped across portions of northern Missouri and southern and southeast Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 27th. Fortunately, given the evening event, severe weather was fairly limited, yielding larger concerns for heavy rainfall. Environmentally, MUCAPE values were around 1000 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 40-50 kts and fading over time, precipitable water values around 1.5 to 1.8 inches, and at least moderate moisture transport into the area. While the best conditions for any severe weather potential and excessive rainfall were down into northern Missouri, areas of southern Iowa saw multiple rounds of showers and storms during the evening and overnight, resulting in heavy rainfall around 2 to 3 inches. Fortunately, little to no water issues were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.97 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3 inch tree limbs down on the campus of UNI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level storm system tracked across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri Thursday night and Friday morning.  This system brought a long duration snow event to the region, with totals mainly between 2 and 6 inches.  This was also the most snow to ever fall on Halloween for much of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer in Manchester reported 2.9 inches of snow. A trained spotter in Hopkinton reported 4.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

Strong to severe storms and heavy rainfall moved up into Iowa from northern Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd of June. Ahead of the storms, a warm front lifted into and through much of the state, bringing with it temperatures into the low to mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Support for storms was decent with MUCAPE values of 2000+ J/kg reaching roughly the Interstate 80 corridor, effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range, and strong low level moisture transport boosting precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches.  ||All in all, a handful of severe storm reports (hail and wind) were seen as storms moved into the state from the south and eventually transitioned more into a rain threat that came to fruition over the eastern half of the Des Moines metro. A number of heavy rainfall amounts in excess of 2 to 4 inches were received, producing multiple flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ankeny Regional Airport AWOS recorded heavy rainfall of 2.06 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.05 inches over the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level storm system tracked across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri Thursday night and Friday morning.  This system brought a long duration snow event to the region, with totals mainly between 2 and 6 inches.  This was also the most snow to ever fall on Halloween for much of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer in Anamosa reported 3.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 25th, a number of storms were able to initiate along a relatively stationary boundary situated across southern Iowa. On the warm side of the boundary surface conditions were in the mid 80s for temperature and upper 60s to low 70s dewpoints.  Additionally, the conditions yielded an environment with 2000-3000+ J/kg SB/MUCAPE, 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear, 500-700 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and precipitable water values around 1.2-1.5 inches.  ||Ultimately, storms developed along the boundary during the mid afternoon hours, initially producing sub-severe conditions. As the initial wave of storms moved eastward, a second wave of development occured during the evening, of which the storms were better able to organize and produce numerous reports of quarter to larger than golf ball sized hail. A few storms were also able to produce damaging winds as they too progressed eastward.  The second wave of storms were aided by the low level jet, initiating a series of training storms over portions of southwest and south central Iowa where copious rainfall produced a small handful of flash flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported lots of quarter sized hail with a few to half dollar in size. Significant crop damage was noted to the north of this location with corn and bean fields decimated. Hail size that did damage to the fields was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet fall pattern resulted in major flooding on a few sites along the Mississippi River in October, 2019. Keithsburg, Illinois and Burlington, Iowa were both over major flood stage due to an extended wet weather pattern from September through early October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at the BRLI4 gage rose above major flood stage of 18.0 feet on the 8th, and crested at 18.36 feet at 1 PM CST on the 15th. It feel back blow 18.0 feet on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet fall pattern resulted in major flooding on a few sites along the Mississippi River in October, 2019. Keithsburg, Illinois and Burlington, Iowa were both over major flood stage due to an extended wet weather pattern from September through early October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at the KHBI2 gage rose above major flood stage of 17.0 feet on the 13th, and crested at 17.12 feet at 1 AM CST on the 15th. It fell back below 17.0 feet on the 17th.

Additionally, the conditions yielded an environment with 2000-3000+ J/kg SB/MUCAPE, 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear, 500-700 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and precipitable water values around 1.2-1.5 inches.  ||Ultimately, storms developed along the boundary during the mid afternoon hours, initially producing sub-severe conditions. As the initial wave of storms moved eastward, a second wave of development occured during the evening, of which the storms were better able to organize and produce numerous reports of quarter to larger than golf ball sized hail. A few storms were also able to produce damaging winds as they too progressed eastward.  The second wave of storms were aided by the low level jet, initiating a series of training storms over portions of southwest and south central Iowa where copious rainfall produced a small handful of flash flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail, relayed via local media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The contiguous United States generally found itself dominated by zonal upper level flow, especially across the northern half to third of the country. Within that flow, multiple short waves moved through the Dakotas, Minnesota, and parts of Iowa as they trekked eastward. As a result, multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms were seen across those areas, especially during the evening and overnight hours as they slid down from Minnesota. The overall environment was not substantial, but was able to support a few strong storms with MUCAPE values around 2000 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts, and CAPE values around 400-600 J/kg in the hail growth regions. Ultimately, only a pair of near-severe to severe hail reports were received, with otherwise non-severe hail prevailing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of Iowa remained in the ring of fire so to speak, with northwest upper level flow dominating. As such, convection initiated well north of the region in areas of the Dakotas and Minnesota and progressed southward towards the area. In this instance, a strong east to west gradient of MUCAPE values existed (2000-4000+ J/kg) and at least semi-supportive effective bulk shear into Iowa of 30 to 40 kts. Additionally, there were elevated DCAPE values, raising some concern for damaging wind potential. ||As the storms congealed and progressed overnight, they formed a QLCS system that was primarily focused over eastern Iowa, producing damaging winds numerous damaging wind reports. Within the Des Moines NWS Office area of responsibility, only two reports were received, with one being quite substantial.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported tree limbs down about 3 inches in diameter in his neighbor's yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms and heavy rainfall moved up into Iowa from northern Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd of June. Ahead of the storms, a warm front lifted into and through much of the state, bringing with it temperatures into the low to mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Support for storms was decent with MUCAPE values of 2000+ J/kg reaching roughly the Interstate 80 corridor, effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range, and strong low level moisture transport boosting precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches.  ||All in all, a handful of severe storm reports (hail and wind) were seen as storms moved into the state from the south and eventually transitioned more into a rain threat that came to fruition over the eastern half of the Des Moines metro. A number of heavy rainfall amounts in excess of 2 to 4 inches were received, producing multiple flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported that a tree landed on a car, and nearby another tree was down in the street with some power lines down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weak mid-level wave moved across the area,  a few storms fired along a frontal boundary.  While the environment featured 1500-2000 J/kg MLCAPE, the deep layer shear was 20 knots or less, and this resulted in most storms weakening fairly rapidly after development.  Isolated storms ended up with strong enough updrafts to produce hail.

An isolated thunderstorm developed late morning in a zone of enhanced near-surface vorticity, where the 0-3km CAPE allowed for rapid acceleration to the updraft.  During the initial development phase, a brief landspout and funnel cloud quickly formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo provided of a funnel cloud between Fostoria and Everly. Photo relayed via broadcast media. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed late morning in a zone of enhanced near-surface vorticity, where the 0-3km CAPE allowed for rapid acceleration to the updraft.  During the initial development phase, a brief landspout and funnel cloud quickly formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout spun up near the intersection of 220th Street and 280th Avenue. The tornado traveled slowly eastward over barren cropland, dissipating before reaching 290th Avenue. The average path width was 10 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported dime to quarter sized hail and small branches broken in the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved out of central South Dakota and across northeast South Dakota late night, spurred by a strong low-level jet and elevated instability up to 3000 J/kg. Cells developing along the southern flank of this system quickly became severe with scattered reports of large hail and damaging wind.||Another cell developed midday behind the initial lines of convection, and produced a long swath of crop damage due to wind driven hail from south of Moneta, to near Everly and Spencer, to north of Ruthven.  A storm survey revealed that the hail damage varied from partial to nearly total crop devastation. While the width of the path was generally 2 to 3 miles, the path of devastation did vary up to 7 miles wide at one point north and west of Spencer.  Hail size varied from dime size to ping-pong ball size along the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branch down.

Increasingly humid conditions, a weak low pressure system, and an impinging northern weak cold front combined to create an environment ripe for strong to severe storms across northern and central Iowa, with much of the severe activity through central Iowa. ||The resulting environment from the aforementioned ingredients included SBCAPE values in the 2000-3000+ J/kg range, marginal effective bulk shear around 30 kts, 400-600 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and at least a few hours with steep low level lapse rates around or greater than 8 deg C/km. All in all while the environment did not lend much support to long lived strong to severe storms, it was more than enough for brief periods of severe storms as many pulsed up and down prior to congealing into multi-cellular clusters and eventually an mesoscale convective system by the evening. Most reports revolved around severe and damaging winds, with a few instances of hail, heavy rainfall, and a land-spout reported north of Whitten in Hardin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection across the Dakotas during the early morning hours of the 27th trekked eastward across southern Minnesota and northern Iowa throughout the morning and early afternoon, carrying with it strong winds and at least small hail. Synoptically, an upper level shortwave trough was moving through the area, while a relatively stationary fronts were draped across southern Minnesota. The environment yielded a supportive environment for sustained convection/QLCS propagation with MUCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg CAPE and 30-50 kts of effective bulk shear oriented relatively normal to the orientation of the QLCS.  ||Even with the supportive environment and radar depictions, only 2 severe reports were received within the Des Moines CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported quarter sized hail.

The environment yielded a supportive environment for sustained convection/QLCS propagation with MUCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg CAPE and 30-50 kts of effective bulk shear oriented relatively normal to the orientation of the QLCS.  ||Even with the supportive environment and radar depictions, only 2 severe reports were received within the Des Moines CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported 60 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight convection across south-central and southeast Minnesota began to slide southward along a potent MUCAPE gradient over eastern Iowa. While sub-severe initially, the line of storms formed a QLCS structure and eventually strengthened to the point of producing numerous hail and damaging wind reports as it continued to move southward. ||The convective environment included MUCAPE values ranging from 2000-4000 J/kg MUCAP from east to west and 30 to 40 kts of effective bulk shear nearly orthogonal to the line of storms. Effectively, the storms were able to sustain long enough and produce a cold pool that was able to continue to initiate convection throughout the morning and into the afternoon as it moved out of Iowa and into increasingly more unstable environments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported awning damage to a business on Church St. between Weller and Sheridan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported a 10 to 12 inch tree branch down on a power line. Also tree debris across a 3 to 4 block swath on the north side of town.

All in all while the environment did not lend much support to long lived strong to severe storms, it was more than enough for brief periods of severe storms as many pulsed up and down prior to congealing into multi-cellular clusters and eventually an mesoscale convective system by the evening. Most reports revolved around severe and damaging winds, with a few instances of hail, heavy rainfall, and a land-spout reported north of Whitten in Hardin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail via the mPING app.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of June 30th. These storms primarily produced damaging winds with trees and power lines blown down from near Osage (Mitchell County) southeast to near Strawberry Point (Clayton County). Lots of trees and power lines were blown down across much of Winneshiek County, the southern half of Allamakee County and the northern sections of Fayette County. A 65 mph wind gust occurred near Fredericksburg (Chickasaw County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some shingles were blown off a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tornado moved through open cropland.  No damage indicators were impacted, therefore the rating was EFU.  Path was found using high resolution satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 4.25 inches over the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasingly humid conditions, a weak low pressure system, and an impinging northern weak cold front combined to create an environment ripe for strong to severe storms across northern and central Iowa, with much of the severe activity through central Iowa. ||The resulting environment from the aforementioned ingredients included SBCAPE values in the 2000-3000+ J/kg range, marginal effective bulk shear around 30 kts, 400-600 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and at least a few hours with steep low level lapse rates around or greater than 8 deg C/km. All in all while the environment did not lend much support to long lived strong to severe storms, it was more than enough for brief periods of severe storms as many pulsed up and down prior to congealing into multi-cellular clusters and eventually an mesoscale convective system by the evening. Most reports revolved around severe and damaging winds, with a few instances of hail, heavy rainfall, and a land-spout reported north of Whitten in Hardin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported heavy rainfall of 2.28 inches. Beginning and ending times estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of June 30th. These storms primarily produced damaging winds with trees and power lines blown down from near Osage (Mitchell County) southeast to near Strawberry Point (Clayton County). Lots of trees and power lines were blown down across much of Winneshiek County, the southern half of Allamakee County and the northern sections of Fayette County. A 65 mph wind gust occurred near Fredericksburg (Chickasaw County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Monona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight convection across south-central and southeast Minnesota began to slide southward along a potent MUCAPE gradient over eastern Iowa. While sub-severe initially, the line of storms formed a QLCS structure and eventually strengthened to the point of producing numerous hail and damaging wind reports as it continued to move southward. ||The convective environment included MUCAPE values ranging from 2000-4000 J/kg MUCAP from east to west and 30 to 40 kts of effective bulk shear nearly orthogonal to the line of storms. Effectively, the storms were able to sustain long enough and produce a cold pool that was able to continue to initiate convection throughout the morning and into the afternoon as it moved out of Iowa and into increasingly more unstable environments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager and Appanoose County Dispatch wind damage in Moravia. They reported roof damage to buildings, siding damage to buildings, and multiple trees with limbs 10 to 12 inches in diameter down.  Winds went through the center of town and the tree damage was widespread.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas near and south of Interstate 90 experienced the first dangerous heat of the year. Afternoon and early evening temperatures from 90 to 95, along with dew point readings from 72 to 77 resulted in maximum heat index values of 100 to 105 degrees. Some higher heat index readings included 106 degrees at Yankton, 103 degrees 5 miles south southeast of Centerville and Le Mars, 102 degrees at Parkston, White Lake and Sioux City, and 101 degrees at Gregory. Several roads buckled due to the heat near Sioux City.

The Mississippi River remained high during the month of May and heavy rains that fell in the later part of the month resulted in the river rising back above major flood stage in late May south of Clinton Iowa. The river remained above major flood up to the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at LeClaire began June above its major flood of 13.5 feet and crested at 15.08 feet on June 1st around 9 PM.  This is preliminarily the 5th highest crest on record.  It feel back below major flood around 5 AM on June 10th.|The Mississippi River at Rock Island began the month above its major flood of 18 feet and crested at 21.68 feet around 7 PM on June 1st. This is preliminarily the 6th highest rest on record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many rivers were in flood at the end of May and the wet period continued into June. Some points went out of flood through early June, and a few continued until mid June. The Des Moines River at Ottumwa continued in flood throughout the month of June mainly due to dam releases upstream from Lake Red Rock. Much of the flooding at the Des Moines River at Eddyville was also due to dam releases upstream from Lake Red Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Southeast 6th Street in Des Moines had crested in May 2019 and was continuing to fall as the month started. The river finally went below flood stage on the 3rd of June 2019.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The excessively wet spring flooding season continued into early June, with several tributary rivers remaining above major flood status the first days of June, before falling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wapsipinicon River at De Witt 4 South remained above major flood stage of 12.5 feet from the end of May through the early morning hours of June 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved out of central South Dakota and across northeast South Dakota late night, spurred by a strong low-level jet and elevated instability up to 3000 J/kg. Cells developing along the southern flank of this system quickly became severe with scattered reports of large hail and damaging wind.||Another cell developed midday behind the initial lines of convection, and produced a long swath of crop damage due to wind driven hail from south of Moneta, to near Everly and Spencer, to north of Ruthven.  A storm survey revealed that the hail damage varied from partial to nearly total crop devastation. While the width of the path was generally 2 to 3 miles, the path of devastation did vary up to 7 miles wide at one point north and west of Spencer.  Hail size varied from dime size to ping-pong ball size along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

The Des Moines River at Ottumwa continued in flood throughout the month of June mainly due to dam releases upstream from Lake Red Rock. Much of the flooding at the Des Moines River at Eddyville was also due to dam releases upstream from Lake Red Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Eddyville crested at 64.56 feet on 3 June 2019 at 02:30 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight convection across south-central and southeast Minnesota began to slide southward along a potent MUCAPE gradient over eastern Iowa. While sub-severe initially, the line of storms formed a QLCS structure and eventually strengthened to the point of producing numerous hail and damaging wind reports as it continued to move southward. ||The convective environment included MUCAPE values ranging from 2000-4000 J/kg MUCAP from east to west and 30 to 40 kts of effective bulk shear nearly orthogonal to the line of storms. Effectively, the storms were able to sustain long enough and produce a cold pool that was able to continue to initiate convection throughout the morning and into the afternoon as it moved out of Iowa and into increasingly more unstable environments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 6 to 8 inch diameter tree limbs snapped as well as damage to a residential roof in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 25th, a number of storms were able to initiate along a relatively stationary boundary situated across southern Iowa. On the warm side of the boundary surface conditions were in the mid 80s for temperature and upper 60s to low 70s dewpoints.  Additionally, the conditions yielded an environment with 2000-3000+ J/kg SB/MUCAPE, 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear, 500-700 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and precipitable water values around 1.2-1.5 inches.  ||Ultimately, storms developed along the boundary during the mid afternoon hours, initially producing sub-severe conditions. As the initial wave of storms moved eastward, a second wave of development occured during the evening, of which the storms were better able to organize and produce numerous reports of quarter to larger than golf ball sized hail. A few storms were also able to produce damaging winds as they too progressed eastward.  The second wave of storms were aided by the low level jet, initiating a series of training storms over portions of southwest and south central Iowa where copious rainfall produced a small handful of flash flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported quarter sized hail and small limb damage. This is a delayed report and time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

A mesoscale convective complex moved out of central South Dakota and across northeast South Dakota late night, spurred by a strong low-level jet and elevated instability up to 3000 J/kg. Cells developing along the southern flank of this system quickly became severe with scattered reports of large hail and damaging wind.||Another cell developed midday behind the initial lines of convection, and produced a long swath of crop damage due to wind driven hail from south of Moneta, to near Everly and Spencer, to north of Ruthven.  A storm survey revealed that the hail damage varied from partial to nearly total crop devastation. While the width of the path was generally 2 to 3 miles, the path of devastation did vary up to 7 miles wide at one point north and west of Spencer.  Hail size varied from dime size to ping-pong ball size along the path.

Increasingly humid conditions, a weak low pressure system, and an impinging northern weak cold front combined to create an environment ripe for strong to severe storms across northern and central Iowa, with much of the severe activity through central Iowa. ||The resulting environment from the aforementioned ingredients included SBCAPE values in the 2000-3000+ J/kg range, marginal effective bulk shear around 30 kts, 400-600 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and at least a few hours with steep low level lapse rates around or greater than 8 deg C/km. All in all while the environment did not lend much support to long lived strong to severe storms, it was more than enough for brief periods of severe storms as many pulsed up and down prior to congealing into multi-cellular clusters and eventually an mesoscale convective system by the evening. Most reports revolved around severe and damaging winds, with a few instances of hail, heavy rainfall, and a land-spout reported north of Whitten in Hardin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported some roads washed out near the Grundy-Hardin County line, as well as some roads washed out near Owasa in Hardin County.

A line of thunderstorms tracked southeast across northeast Iowa into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the afternoon and evening of Sunday, June 30, 2019, bringing widespread downed trees and power lines as 50-70 mph winds rolled through.  The strongest winds were recorded in Dubuque County, where a wind gust of 76 mph was observed at the Dubuque Regional Airport.  Several hometowns were without power after the storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barn partially destroyed and trees snapped off.

A heavy rainfall across the Des Moines metro and surrounding areas during the evening of June 22nd allowed river levels to rise above flood stage on the Des Moines River and also on the Raccoon River in locations around the Des Moines metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Southeast 6th Street in Des Moines crested twice at 25.11 feet during the period of flood. The first on 23 June 2019 at 17:45 UTC, and the second on 25 June 2019 at 12:45 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front Sunday afternoon across portions of eastern Iowa.  The storms were weak, but still were able to produce three brief landspout tornadoes over the region.  Additionally, brief heavy rain and wind gusts up to 50 mph were seen with the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak landspout tornado moved through rural areas southwest of Manchester, IA. Evidence for the tornado is through photo and video, but no damage was observed along its path to assign a rating. This tornado began in eastern Buchanan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of strong storms moved over southeast Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central Illinois the afternoon and evening of June 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temporary flood barrier system in downtown Burlington failed, flooding the Port of Burlington Welcome Center, the Memorial Auditorium, and affecting several other businesses with water from the Mississippi River which was over major flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.70 inches over the past 24 hours.

A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of July 20th. These storms produced some sporadic wind damage as they moved across the area. Trees were blown down in Stacyville (Mitchell County) and near Elkader (Clayton County). In Charles City (Floyd County), a tree was blown down onto a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto power lines north of Elkader.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy rainfall are not uncommon throughout the spring and summer months, but given the very wet spring across Iowa, soils were primed and unable to hold copious amounts of new rainfall. The overall setup for this event was somewhat typical with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, and 850 mb low level jet aiding in convective initiation and moisture transport, precipitable water values around 1.5 to 1.75 inches, and other marginal supporting factors. ||Convection initiated across portions of southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas late in the evening on the 20th and quickly propagated eastward through the overnight and morning hours. Eventually, two decaying waves of storms moved through portions of western Iowa and predominantly provided small hail and efficient heavy rainfall. A few locations reported as much as 3 to 5 inches of rainfall, which helped initiate flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calhoun County Sheriff reported water over the road at the junction of Highway 4 and Highway 175 east of Lake City.

Hail size varied from dime size to ping-pong ball size along the path.

A mesoscale convective complex moved out of central South Dakota and across northeast South Dakota late night, spurred by a strong low-level jet and elevated instability up to 3000 J/kg. Cells developing along the southern flank of this system quickly became severe with scattered reports of large hail and damaging wind.||Another cell developed midday behind the initial lines of convection, and produced a long swath of crop damage due to wind driven hail from south of Moneta, to near Everly and Spencer, to north of Ruthven.  A storm survey revealed that the hail damage varied from partial to nearly total crop devastation. While the width of the path was generally 2 to 3 miles, the path of devastation did vary up to 7 miles wide at one point north and west of Spencer.  Hail size varied from dime size to ping-pong ball size along the path.

Increasingly humid conditions, a weak low pressure system, and an impinging northern weak cold front combined to create an environment ripe for strong to severe storms across northern and central Iowa, with much of the severe activity through central Iowa. ||The resulting environment from the aforementioned ingredients included SBCAPE values in the 2000-3000+ J/kg range, marginal effective bulk shear around 30 kts, 400-600 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and at least a few hours with steep low level lapse rates around or greater than 8 deg C/km. All in all while the environment did not lend much support to long lived strong to severe storms, it was more than enough for brief periods of severe storms as many pulsed up and down prior to congealing into multi-cellular clusters and eventually an mesoscale convective system by the evening. Most reports revolved around severe and damaging winds, with a few instances of hail, heavy rainfall, and a land-spout reported north of Whitten in Hardin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down that was blocking the road at the intersection of 270th and Y Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Oppressive temperatures in the mid 90's to around 100 degrees along with dew point readings in the mid to upper 70's produced very dangerous heat conditions with heat index readings in excess of 105 degrees for much of the afternoon and early evening. Numerous roads across the region buckled from the heat.||Some of the more extreme heat index readings experienced during the afternoon included 113 degrees at Wagner, 112 degrees at Le Mars, Canton and Mitchell, 111 degrees at Cherokee, Sioux City, Huron and Yankton, and 110 degrees at Sioux Falls, Colton and White Lake.

A line of thunderstorms tracked southeast across northeast Iowa into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the afternoon and evening of Sunday, June 30, 2019, bringing widespread downed trees and power lines as 50-70 mph winds rolled through.  The strongest winds were recorded in Dubuque County, where a wind gust of 76 mph was observed at the Dubuque Regional Airport.  Several hometowns were without power after the storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous 2 to 4 inch diameter tree branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of June 4th, a severe thunderstorm developed over northern Scott county Iowa, producing very heavy rain, small hail, and damaging winds. As this storm moved east into portions of Henry, Whiteside, and Bureau counties in Illinois,  it intensified and become a high precipitation supercell thunderstorm, with winds over 80 mph, and torrential rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported winds of 50 to 60 mph, with torrential rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across portions of eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and northwest Illinois Saturday evening, June 15,  bringing damaging winds, heavy downpours, frequent lightning, large hail, and a tornado.  Many areas saw winds between 30-50 mph during the evening hours, with higher winds of 60-70 mph reported in Iowa and Benton counties in Iowa.   ||The storms produced three tornadoes in Des Moines County (west of  Mediapolis, IA and south of Oakville, IA) between 7:50 pm and 8:30 pm.||Two tornadoes south of Oakville were rated EF2 and downed numerous trees, severely damaged a house displacing it off its foundation, destroyed outbuildings, and downed power poles.||The tornado west of Mediapolis remained in an open field with no observable damage to assign a rating.||Another tornado occurred just northeast of Keithsburg in Mercer County Illinois. This tornado was rated EF1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front Sunday afternoon across portions of eastern Iowa.  The storms were weak, but still were able to produce three brief landspout tornadoes over the region.  Additionally, brief heavy rain and wind gusts up to 50 mph were seen with the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak landspout tornado moved through rural areas south of Anamosa, IA.  Evidence for the tornado is through photos from emergency management, with no damage observed along its path to assign a rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.76 in their rain gauge this morning. This is a 24 hour report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy rainfall are not uncommon throughout the spring and summer months, but given the very wet spring across Iowa, soils were primed and unable to hold copious amounts of new rainfall. The overall setup for this event was somewhat typical with a shortwave moving through the parent flow, and 850 mb low level jet aiding in convective initiation and moisture transport, precipitable water values around 1.5 to 1.75 inches, and other marginal supporting factors. ||Convection initiated across portions of southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas late in the evening on the 20th and quickly propagated eastward through the overnight and morning hours. Eventually, two decaying waves of storms moved through portions of western Iowa and predominantly provided small hail and efficient heavy rainfall. A few locations reported as much as 3 to 5 inches of rainfall, which helped initiate flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several roads in Boone barricaded earlier due to heavy rainfall. Water has receded now.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms and heavy rainfall moved up into Iowa from northern Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd of June. Ahead of the storms, a warm front lifted into and through much of the state, bringing with it temperatures into the low to mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Support for storms was decent with MUCAPE values of 2000+ J/kg reaching roughly the Interstate 80 corridor, effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range, and strong low level moisture transport boosting precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches.  ||All in all, a handful of severe storm reports (hail and wind) were seen as storms moved into the state from the south and eventually transitioned more into a rain threat that came to fruition over the eastern half of the Des Moines metro. A number of heavy rainfall amounts in excess of 2 to 4 inches were received, producing multiple flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported hail that lasted about 5 minutes, with largest stones the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 25th, a number of storms were able to initiate along a relatively stationary boundary situated across southern Iowa. On the warm side of the boundary surface conditions were in the mid 80s for temperature and upper 60s to low 70s dewpoints.  Additionally, the conditions yielded an environment with 2000-3000+ J/kg SB/MUCAPE, 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear, 500-700 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and precipitable water values around 1.2-1.5 inches.  ||Ultimately, storms developed along the boundary during the mid afternoon hours, initially producing sub-severe conditions. As the initial wave of storms moved eastward, a second wave of development occured during the evening, of which the storms were better able to organize and produce numerous reports of quarter to larger than golf ball sized hail. A few storms were also able to produce damaging winds as they too progressed eastward.  The second wave of storms were aided by the low level jet, initiating a series of training storms over portions of southwest and south central Iowa where copious rainfall produced a small handful of flash flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Corps of Engineers reported golf ball sized hail to dispatch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado with 120 mph peak winds touched down doing damage to trees and the roof of a house.  As it moved east it, strengthened causing more tree damage and siding damage to a house.  The tornado then reached peak strength, destroying a farm outbuilding, throwing two pieces of farming equipment 40 yards, and then moving a pickup truck through the air approx 20 yards.  A house at this location suffered major damage, as it was moved off it's foundation.  All the windows were blown out, a bolted down garage was destroyed and lofted.  Three power poles were also snapped at this location.  The tornado then turned northeast, destroying numerous trees.  Once it came down the bluff, it hit power lines causing them to lean over.  The tornado ended shortly after this.
