EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 971mb bomb cyclone moved out of the central Rockies on Wednesday, March 13, 2019 and helped to create widespread, moderate to major, and in many cases historic, flooding across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. From 4 to 15 inches of snow cover remained across the mid Missouri River valley, and the ground was frozen with existing frost depths of 15 to 23 inches. Warm temperatures allowed all of the snow to rapidly melt and record moisture allowed 1 to 2.5 inches of rain to fall over a 48 hour period.  Due to the frozen ground, and that 1 to 2 feet of thick ice remained in area rivers, widespread, and in many cases, catastrophic flooding developed. Due to expansive levee breaches on many rivers and streams, the flood damage was particularly devastating to many communities. Four individuals lost their lives due to the extreme conditions, and several hundred people required rescue via air or boat. Tens of thousands of people were evacuated from their homes or businesses. Nearly 50 levees were breached on the Platte, Elkhorn and Missouri Rivers due to the large volume of water. One such levee breach along the Platte River near Leshara, Nebraska forced the evacuation of National Weather Service Omaha, located in Valley, Nebraska for 8 days. Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts presented US$1.3 billion preliminary damage estimates for the entire state, including specific sector losses of: $439 million infrastructure (including roads and bridges), $85 million private homes and businesses, $400 million livestock and $440 million crop. About 2,000 miles of state roads including 15 Nebraska state highway bridges and numerous local bridges, roughly 20 percent of the entire state road system were impacted. Iowa Governor Kim Reynolds provided a $1.6 billion preliminary damage estimate due to flooding across the state. U.S. Interstate 29, extending north-south along the Missouri River, was inundated and partially destroyed, as was the Interstate 680 bypass around Council Bluffs-Omaha metropolitan area. Also noteworthy was damage to Offutt Air Force Base located near Bellevue, Nebraska. One-third of the base���s main runway was inundated, along with one-third of Offutt���s buildings, with several hundred employees, and several families displaced. Preliminary damage estimates released by the Department of Defense are on the order of $450 million. Nebraska and Iowa severe flooding impacts culminated in Federal Disaster Declaration 4420.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two levee breeches resulted in flash flooding from the Missouri River.  One of the hardest hit areas was  the town of Pacific Junction.  The entire town was flooding by water from the river. US Highway 34 in Mills County sustained extreme damage to the roadway. Interstate 29 in Mills County sustained damage as well south of US 34 interchange on I-29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 971mb bomb cyclone moved out of the central Rockies on Wednesday, March 13, 2019 and helped to create widespread, moderate to major, and in many cases historic, flooding across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. From 4 to 15 inches of snow cover remained across the mid Missouri River valley, and the ground was frozen with existing frost depths of 15 to 23 inches. Warm temperatures allowed all of the snow to rapidly melt and record moisture allowed 1 to 2.5 inches of rain to fall over a 48 hour period.  Due to the frozen ground, and that 1 to 2 feet of thick ice remained in area rivers, widespread, and in many cases, catastrophic flooding developed. Due to expansive levee breaches on many rivers and streams, the flood damage was particularly devastating to many communities. Four individuals lost their lives due to the extreme conditions, and several hundred people required rescue via air or boat. Tens of thousands of people were evacuated from their homes or businesses. Nearly 50 levees were breached on the Platte, Elkhorn and Missouri Rivers due to the large volume of water. One such levee breach along the Platte River near Leshara, Nebraska forced the evacuation of National Weather Service Omaha, located in Valley, Nebraska for 8 days. Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts presented US$1.3 billion preliminary damage estimates for the entire state, including specific sector losses of: $439 million infrastructure (including roads and bridges), $85 million private homes and businesses, $400 million livestock and $440 million crop. About 2,000 miles of state roads including 15 Nebraska state highway bridges and numerous local bridges, roughly 20 percent of the entire state road system were impacted. Iowa Governor Kim Reynolds provided a $1.6 billion preliminary damage estimate due to flooding across the state. U.S. Interstate 29, extending north-south along the Missouri River, was inundated and partially destroyed, as was the Interstate 680 bypass around Council Bluffs-Omaha metropolitan area. Also noteworthy was damage to Offutt Air Force Base located near Bellevue, Nebraska. One-third of the base���s main runway was inundated, along with one-third of Offutt���s buildings, with several hundred employees, and several families displaced. Preliminary damage estimates released by the Department of Defense are on the order of $450 million. Nebraska and Iowa severe flooding impacts culminated in Federal Disaster Declaration 4420.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported water covering the road on Madison Avenue due to heavy rains causing Mosquito Creek to flood.  Water was rising rapidly in an apartment complex parking lot near the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasingly humid conditions, a weak low pressure system, and an impinging northern weak cold front combined to create an environment ripe for strong to severe storms across northern and central Iowa, with much of the severe activity through central Iowa. ||The resulting environment from the aforementioned ingredients included SBCAPE values in the 2000-3000+ J/kg range, marginal effective bulk shear around 30 kts, 400-600 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and at least a few hours with steep low level lapse rates around or greater than 8 deg C/km. All in all while the environment did not lend much support to long lived strong to severe storms, it was more than enough for brief periods of severe storms as many pulsed up and down prior to congealing into multi-cellular clusters and eventually an mesoscale convective system by the evening. Most reports revolved around severe and damaging winds, with a few instances of hail, heavy rainfall, and a land-spout reported north of Whitten in Hardin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement relayed reports of a tree down in Maxwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 1 and 3 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable areal flooding and flooding of small streams. Numerous roads, bridges and homes were compromised by flood water, especially in areas near Hornick, Danbury, Holly Springs, and Moville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in extensive damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weakly organized storms developed along a cold front during the afternoon, with 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE and deep layer shear of up to 25 knots. There were isolated occurrences of strong winds and hail. An additional post-frontal isolated storm developed during the evening and contained strong and damaging wind.

A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late morning and afternoon of June 27th. These storms dropped hail, ranging from dime to quarter sized, across portions of Floyd, Howard and Chickasaw Counties. The largest reported hail was quarter sized near Elma (Howard County). The storms also produced some damaging winds that blew down trees near Elkader (Clayton County) and in Strawberry Point (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell northeast of Elma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms tracked southeast across northeast Iowa into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the afternoon and evening of Sunday, June 30, 2019, bringing widespread downed trees and power lines as 50-70 mph winds rolled through.  The strongest winds were recorded in Dubuque County, where a wind gust of 76 mph was observed at the Dubuque Regional Airport.  Several hometowns were without power after the storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LARGE 4 TO 6 inch diameter tree limbs blown down.

Severe thunderstorms tracked across portions of eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and northwest Illinois Saturday evening, June 15,  bringing damaging winds, heavy downpours, frequent lightning, large hail, and a tornado.  Many areas saw winds between 30-50 mph during the evening hours, with higher winds of 60-70 mph reported in Iowa and Benton counties in Iowa.   ||The storms produced three tornadoes in Des Moines County (west of  Mediapolis, IA and south of Oakville, IA) between 7:50 pm and 8:30 pm.||Two tornadoes south of Oakville were rated EF2 and downed numerous trees, severely damaged a house displacing it off its foundation, destroyed outbuildings, and downed power poles.||The tornado west of Mediapolis remained in an open field with no observable damage to assign a rating.||Another tornado occurred just northeast of Keithsburg in Mercer County Illinois. This tornado was rated EF1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second EF2 tornado with winds to 120 mph,  touched down just east of the first one initially damaging trees and the roof of a mobile home.  As it moved east, it strengthened as it went down the bluff.  Here it destroyed a pig farm building along with taking down a wooden high power transmission line.  One other house along with numerous trees sustained damage from the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With temperatures peaking in the mid to upper 90's and dew points in the lower to mid 70's, heat index values ranged from 100 to 108 during the afternoon and early evening hours.||A 16-month old female died around 1500CST after being left alone in a vehicle in Sioux City for an extended period of time.

After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

Two instances of moderate rainfall accompanied consecutive frontal passages across parts of northwest Iowa. The subsequent runoff produced brief minor flooding in upper portions of the Little Sioux River and its tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall reached 4.59 inches 3 miles west northwest of Everly, and was generally 3.00 to 5.00 inches in the upper tributaries of the Little Sioux River Basin.  The Ocheyedan River near Spencer (gage site SPOI4) reached minor flood stage and crested 0.43 feet above flood stage on October 7.  Main impacts occurred with flooding of agricultural land west of Spencer. Crop damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 25th, a number of storms were able to initiate along a relatively stationary boundary situated across southern Iowa. On the warm side of the boundary surface conditions were in the mid 80s for temperature and upper 60s to low 70s dewpoints.  Additionally, the conditions yielded an environment with 2000-3000+ J/kg SB/MUCAPE, 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear, 500-700 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and precipitable water values around 1.2-1.5 inches.  ||Ultimately, storms developed along the boundary during the mid afternoon hours, initially producing sub-severe conditions. As the initial wave of storms moved eastward, a second wave of development occured during the evening, of which the storms were better able to organize and produce numerous reports of quarter to larger than golf ball sized hail. A few storms were also able to produce damaging winds as they too progressed eastward.  The second wave of storms were aided by the low level jet, initiating a series of training storms over portions of southwest and south central Iowa where copious rainfall produced a small handful of flash flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported golf ball sized hail fell for several minutes before becoming smaller in size. A severe weather spotter reported small branches snapped at the same time as the hail.

A mesoscale convective complex moved out of central South Dakota and across northeast South Dakota late night, spurred by a strong low-level jet and elevated instability up to 3000 J/kg. Cells developing along the southern flank of this system quickly became severe with scattered reports of large hail and damaging wind.||Another cell developed midday behind the initial lines of convection, and produced a long swath of crop damage due to wind driven hail from south of Moneta, to near Everly and Spencer, to north of Ruthven.  A storm survey revealed that the hail damage varied from partial to nearly total crop devastation. While the width of the path was generally 2 to 3 miles, the path of devastation did vary up to 7 miles wide at one point north and west of Spencer.  Hail size varied from dime size to ping-pong ball size along the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel size with occasional quarter size.  Strong winds accompanied hail.

A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency manager reported a swath of corn blown down between Collins and Maxwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold frontal boundary initiated storms overnight across portions of north central and northeast South Dakota that congealed into a quasi-linear convective system. That complex of storms moved east southeast into northwest Iowa and portions of central Iowa through the mid-morning hours of the 18th. As the storms had moved ahead of the best forcing/parent boundary storms remained sub-severe and weakened through the morning and into the early afternoon. As some semblance of diurnal recovery occurred, a few storms were able to rejuvenate and produce a handful marginally severe to severe wind and hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poweshiek County Rd. Department reports a power pole blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.31 inches over the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gradual decrease in river levels occurred as water was routed downstream along the Little Sioux River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the Ocheyedan River near Spencer (gage site SPOI4) began the month at 2.12 feet above flood stage.  Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded, and further impeded spring planting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

A 971mb bomb cyclone moved out of the central Rockies on Wednesday, March 13, 2019 and helped to create widespread, moderate to major, and in many cases historic, flooding across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. From 4 to 15 inches of snow cover remained across the mid Missouri River valley, and the ground was frozen with existing frost depths of 15 to 23 inches. Warm temperatures allowed all of the snow to rapidly melt and record moisture allowed 1 to 2.5 inches of rain to fall over a 48 hour period.  Due to the frozen ground, and that 1 to 2 feet of thick ice remained in area rivers, widespread, and in many cases, catastrophic flooding developed. Due to expansive levee breaches on many rivers and streams, the flood damage was particularly devastating to many communities. Four individuals lost their lives due to the extreme conditions, and several hundred people required rescue via air or boat. Tens of thousands of people were evacuated from their homes or businesses. Nearly 50 levees were breached on the Platte, Elkhorn and Missouri Rivers due to the large volume of water. One such levee breach along the Platte River near Leshara, Nebraska forced the evacuation of National Weather Service Omaha, located in Valley, Nebraska for 8 days. Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts presented US$1.3 billion preliminary damage estimates for the entire state, including specific sector losses of: $439 million infrastructure (including roads and bridges), $85 million private homes and businesses, $400 million livestock and $440 million crop. About 2,000 miles of state roads including 15 Nebraska state highway bridges and numerous local bridges, roughly 20 percent of the entire state road system were impacted. Iowa Governor Kim Reynolds provided a $1.6 billion preliminary damage estimate due to flooding across the state. U.S. Interstate 29, extending north-south along the Missouri River, was inundated and partially destroyed, as was the Interstate 680 bypass around Council Bluffs-Omaha metropolitan area. Also noteworthy was damage to Offutt Air Force Base located near Bellevue, Nebraska. One-third of the base���s main runway was inundated, along with one-third of Offutt���s buildings, with several hundred employees, and several families displaced. Preliminary damage estimates released by the Department of Defense are on the order of $450 million. Nebraska and Iowa severe flooding impacts culminated in Federal Disaster Declaration 4420.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was significant flooding across the county.  On the Missouri River,. The river gauge at Omaha, operated by the USGS, peaked at 34.41 feet at 8 am on March 17th. Moderate flood stage is 32 feet. Preliminarily this peak is the 3rd highest on record. The primary impact from this flooding was Interstate 29 and Interstate 680 being closed due to being under water. East and west bound Highway 6 is closed as Keg and Little Keg Creeks were out of banks.  The heavy rains caused mudslides, and the most serious one was in Council Bluffs on Harry Langdon Boulevard, which closed the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasingly humid conditions, a weak low pressure system, and an impinging northern weak cold front combined to create an environment ripe for strong to severe storms across northern and central Iowa, with much of the severe activity through central Iowa. ||The resulting environment from the aforementioned ingredients included SBCAPE values in the 2000-3000+ J/kg range, marginal effective bulk shear around 30 kts, 400-600 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and at least a few hours with steep low level lapse rates around or greater than 8 deg C/km. All in all while the environment did not lend much support to long lived strong to severe storms, it was more than enough for brief periods of severe storms as many pulsed up and down prior to congealing into multi-cellular clusters and eventually an mesoscale convective system by the evening. Most reports revolved around severe and damaging winds, with a few instances of hail, heavy rainfall, and a land-spout reported north of Whitten in Hardin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated 60 to 70 mph wind gusts near Blank Park Zoo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late morning and afternoon of June 27th. These storms dropped hail, ranging from dime to quarter sized, across portions of Floyd, Howard and Chickasaw Counties. The largest reported hail was quarter sized near Elma (Howard County). The storms also produced some damaging winds that blew down trees near Elkader (Clayton County) and in Strawberry Point (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed the Upper Iowa River out of its banks near Dorchester. The river crested around a half foot above the flood stage at 14.6 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms and heavy rainfall moved up into Iowa from northern Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd of June. Ahead of the storms, a warm front lifted into and through much of the state, bringing with it temperatures into the low to mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Support for storms was decent with MUCAPE values of 2000+ J/kg reaching roughly the Interstate 80 corridor, effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range, and strong low level moisture transport boosting precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches.  ||All in all, a handful of severe storm reports (hail and wind) were seen as storms moved into the state from the south and eventually transitioned more into a rain threat that came to fruition over the eastern half of the Des Moines metro. A number of heavy rainfall amounts in excess of 2 to 4 inches were received, producing multiple flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported multiple water rescues from motorists whose cars stalled out in ponded water in parts of the city of Des Moines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 25th, a number of storms were able to initiate along a relatively stationary boundary situated across southern Iowa. On the warm side of the boundary surface conditions were in the mid 80s for temperature and upper 60s to low 70s dewpoints.  Additionally, the conditions yielded an environment with 2000-3000+ J/kg SB/MUCAPE, 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear, 500-700 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and precipitable water values around 1.2-1.5 inches.  ||Ultimately, storms developed along the boundary during the mid afternoon hours, initially producing sub-severe conditions. As the initial wave of storms moved eastward, a second wave of development occured during the evening, of which the storms were better able to organize and produce numerous reports of quarter to larger than golf ball sized hail. A few storms were also able to produce damaging winds as they too progressed eastward.  The second wave of storms were aided by the low level jet, initiating a series of training storms over portions of southwest and south central Iowa where copious rainfall produced a small handful of flash flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported hail slightly larger than golf balls, as well as damage to flowers and crops.

A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.12 inches over the past 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: While the strongest winds of 30 to 40 mph occurred over several hours, a peak wind gust of 58 mph was measured 3 miles west northwest of Spencer at 2151CST.

Nearly 50 levees were breached on the Platte, Elkhorn and Missouri Rivers due to the large volume of water. One such levee breach along the Platte River near Leshara, Nebraska forced the evacuation of National Weather Service Omaha, located in Valley, Nebraska for 8 days. Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts presented US$1.3 billion preliminary damage estimates for the entire state, including specific sector losses of: $439 million infrastructure (including roads and bridges), $85 million private homes and businesses, $400 million livestock and $440 million crop. About 2,000 miles of state roads including 15 Nebraska state highway bridges and numerous local bridges, roughly 20 percent of the entire state road system were impacted. Iowa Governor Kim Reynolds provided a $1.6 billion preliminary damage estimate due to flooding across the state. U.S. Interstate 29, extending north-south along the Missouri River, was inundated and partially destroyed, as was the Interstate 680 bypass around Council Bluffs-Omaha metropolitan area. Also noteworthy was damage to Offutt Air Force Base located near Bellevue, Nebraska. One-third of the base���s main runway was inundated, along with one-third of Offutt���s buildings, with several hundred employees, and several families displaced. Preliminary damage estimates released by the Department of Defense are on the order of $450 million. Nebraska and Iowa severe flooding impacts culminated in Federal Disaster Declaration 4420.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Levee being overtopped 1 to 1 1/2 miles north of the Mills and Fremont county line. Evacuations took place along the river and near Bartlett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 25th, a number of storms were able to initiate along a relatively stationary boundary situated across southern Iowa. On the warm side of the boundary surface conditions were in the mid 80s for temperature and upper 60s to low 70s dewpoints.  Additionally, the conditions yielded an environment with 2000-3000+ J/kg SB/MUCAPE, 30-40 kts of effective bulk shear, 500-700 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and precipitable water values around 1.2-1.5 inches.  ||Ultimately, storms developed along the boundary during the mid afternoon hours, initially producing sub-severe conditions. As the initial wave of storms moved eastward, a second wave of development occured during the evening, of which the storms were better able to organize and produce numerous reports of quarter to larger than golf ball sized hail. A few storms were also able to produce damaging winds as they too progressed eastward.  The second wave of storms were aided by the low level jet, initiating a series of training storms over portions of southwest and south central Iowa where copious rainfall produced a small handful of flash flood reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported Highway 34 closed in both directions at Kent Corner due to water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

Increasingly humid conditions, a weak low pressure system, and an impinging northern weak cold front combined to create an environment ripe for strong to severe storms across northern and central Iowa, with much of the severe activity through central Iowa. ||The resulting environment from the aforementioned ingredients included SBCAPE values in the 2000-3000+ J/kg range, marginal effective bulk shear around 30 kts, 400-600 J/kg CAPE in the hail growth region, and at least a few hours with steep low level lapse rates around or greater than 8 deg C/km. All in all while the environment did not lend much support to long lived strong to severe storms, it was more than enough for brief periods of severe storms as many pulsed up and down prior to congealing into multi-cellular clusters and eventually an mesoscale convective system by the evening. Most reports revolved around severe and damaging winds, with a few instances of hail, heavy rainfall, and a land-spout reported north of Whitten in Hardin county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in Gladbrook. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 1 and 3 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable areal flooding and flooding of small streams. Numerous roads, bridges and homes were compromised by flood water, especially in areas near Hornick, Danbury, Holly Springs, and Moville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in extensive damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Des Moines International Airport ASOS recorded heavy rainfall of 2.74 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 971mb bomb cyclone moved out of the central Rockies on Wednesday, March 13, 2019 and helped to create widespread, moderate to major, and in many cases historic, flooding across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. From 4 to 15 inches of snow cover remained across the mid Missouri River valley, and the ground was frozen with existing frost depths of 15 to 23 inches. Warm temperatures allowed all of the snow to rapidly melt and record moisture allowed 1 to 2.5 inches of rain to fall over a 48 hour period.  Due to the frozen ground, and that 1 to 2 feet of thick ice remained in area rivers, widespread, and in many cases, catastrophic flooding developed. Due to expansive levee breaches on many rivers and streams, the flood damage was particularly devastating to many communities. Four individuals lost their lives due to the extreme conditions, and several hundred people required rescue via air or boat. Tens of thousands of people were evacuated from their homes or businesses. Nearly 50 levees were breached on the Platte, Elkhorn and Missouri Rivers due to the large volume of water. One such levee breach along the Platte River near Leshara, Nebraska forced the evacuation of National Weather Service Omaha, located in Valley, Nebraska for 8 days. Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts presented US$1.3 billion preliminary damage estimates for the entire state, including specific sector losses of: $439 million infrastructure (including roads and bridges), $85 million private homes and businesses, $400 million livestock and $440 million crop. About 2,000 miles of state roads including 15 Nebraska state highway bridges and numerous local bridges, roughly 20 percent of the entire state road system were impacted. Iowa Governor Kim Reynolds provided a $1.6 billion preliminary damage estimate due to flooding across the state. U.S. Interstate 29, extending north-south along the Missouri River, was inundated and partially destroyed, as was the Interstate 680 bypass around Council Bluffs-Omaha metropolitan area. Also noteworthy was damage to Offutt Air Force Base located near Bellevue, Nebraska. One-third of the base���s main runway was inundated, along with one-third of Offutt���s buildings, with several hundred employees, and several families displaced. Preliminary damage estimates released by the Department of Defense are on the order of $450 million. Nebraska and Iowa severe flooding impacts culminated in Federal Disaster Declaration 4420.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Nishnabotna River. The river gage above Hamburg, operated by the USGS, peaked at 31.07 feet at 11 am on March 16th. Moderate flood stage is 27.5 feet while major flood stage is 33 feet. Preliminary  informations shows this peak is the 3rd highest on record. Flooding also occurred along the Missouri River. The river gage at Nebraska City, operated by the USGS, peaked at 30.15 feet at 6 pm on March 16th. Minor flood stage is 18 feet, moderate flood stage is 23 feet while major flood stage is 27 feet. Preliminary data shows this to be a record crest. Flooding continued into the next month. Levees 601, 594 and 575 failed flooding Bartlett, Percival, Hamburg, and the truck stop area near Interstate 29. Dollar estimate is from the Army Corps of Engineers Omaha District. Hospitals and businesses were impacted by the flood.  Floods devastated livestock, grain storage, and fields that had yet to be planted.Water systems in Hamburg were incapacitated and overcome with high water. Three men were driving  in Hamburg and drove around a road closed barrieron the evening of the 15th. They continued driving until they hit flood water, where the current swept their vehicle away. The men tried to swim to safety. Two of the men were found holding onto trees in the floodwaters. The third was found in a ditch near a residence trying to get warm. One of the men died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms tracked southeast across northeast Iowa into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the afternoon and evening of Sunday, June 30, 2019, bringing widespread downed trees and power lines as 50-70 mph winds rolled through.  The strongest winds were recorded in Dubuque County, where a wind gust of 76 mph was observed at the Dubuque Regional Airport.  Several hometowns were without power after the storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large cedar tree snapped off 2 feet above ground and a small outbuilding was destroyed at 2214  108TH ST, Hazelton, Iowa.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall around October 1 which averaged 1.00 to 1.50 inches around Sioux Falls and 2.00 to 3.00 inches in the Rock River Basin pushed the Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) into minor flood. The river crested 1.38 feet above flood stage on October 7. Significant flooding of agricultural land occurred, with a few county and township roads briefly inundated near the river. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A carryover from a severe thunderstorm episode on September 30, weakening storms just after midnight along an advancing frontal boundary produced an isolated instance of sub-severe hail.

A surface low pressure system developed on the lee side of the Rockies late November 25th and November 26th. As it departed the front range, it moved eastward through Kansas before turning northeasterly into Iowa and the Great Lakes Region during the evening of the 26th and through the 27th. While substantially cold temperatures were not in place within the area or north of the area, it was cold enough for snow to be the primary precipitation type across much of northwest and northern Iowa. Areas further south remained warm enough to see predominantly rain. As the system departed, it also provided a number of hours of strong gusty winds due to a tight surface pressure gradient on the backside of the surface low. With around 2 to 6 inches of snowfall and winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, travel was hazardous at times, including significantly reduced visibilities. Fortunately, much of the worst conditions occurred during the evening and overnight hours.

Portions of Iowa remained in the ring of fire so to speak, with northwest upper level flow dominating. As such, convection initiated well north of the region in areas of the Dakotas and Minnesota and progressed southward towards the area. In this instance, a strong east to west gradient of MUCAPE values existed (2000-4000+ J/kg) and at least semi-supportive effective bulk shear into Iowa of 30 to 40 kts. Additionally, there were elevated DCAPE values, raising some concern for damaging wind potential. ||As the storms congealed and progressed overnight, they formed a QLCS system that was primarily focused over eastern Iowa, producing damaging winds numerous damaging wind reports. Within the Des Moines NWS Office area of responsibility, only two reports were received, with one being quite substantial.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee relayed storm damage reports of trees snapped and uprooted from roughly 2 miles south of Horton. The path of damage continued for about 3 miles to the south and was roughly 1 mile wide. This is a delayed report and time estimated from radar. Note, the damaging gust front was just out ahead of the main thunderstorm complex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.63 inches over the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of June 30th. These storms primarily produced damaging winds with trees and power lines blown down from near Osage (Mitchell County) southeast to near Strawberry Point (Clayton County). Lots of trees and power lines were blown down across much of Winneshiek County, the southern half of Allamakee County and the northern sections of Fayette County. A 65 mph wind gust occurred near Fredericksburg (Chickasaw County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down north of Osage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms tracked southeast across northeast Iowa into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois during the afternoon and evening of Sunday, June 30, 2019, bringing widespread downed trees and power lines as 50-70 mph winds rolled through.  The strongest winds were recorded in Dubuque County, where a wind gust of 76 mph was observed at the Dubuque Regional Airport.  Several hometowns were without power after the storms passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and utility lines blown down.

Two instances of moderate rainfall accompanied consecutive frontal passages across parts of northwest Iowa. The subsequent runoff produced brief minor flooding in upper portions of the Little Sioux River and its tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall from October 1-5 totaled 4.81 inches at Lake Park, 4.61 inches at Spirit Lake, 3.85 inches 3 miles south-southeast of Lake Park and 3.34 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford. The Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4) climbed into minor flood and crested 1.24 feet above flood stage on October 6. Flooding impacts were generally to agricultural areas near the river, however, water did briefly inundate 190th Avenue near 240th Street. Property and crop damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms and heavy rainfall moved up into Iowa from northern Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 22nd of June. Ahead of the storms, a warm front lifted into and through much of the state, bringing with it temperatures into the low to mid 80s and dew points in the low to mid 70s. Support for storms was decent with MUCAPE values of 2000+ J/kg reaching roughly the Interstate 80 corridor, effective bulk shear in the 30 to 40 kt range, and strong low level moisture transport boosting precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches.  ||All in all, a handful of severe storm reports (hail and wind) were seen as storms moved into the state from the south and eventually transitioned more into a rain threat that came to fruition over the eastern half of the Des Moines metro. A number of heavy rainfall amounts in excess of 2 to 4 inches were received, producing multiple flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported calls stalled out near the intersection of Euclid and Delaware Ave from high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.65 inches at Rock Rapids and 1.51 inches at Rock Valley) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. North Main Street in Rock Valley was evacuated between 17th and 20th Street, and U.S. Highway 18 west of Rock Valley was closed between March 14-16 due to water flowing over the roadway.  Flooding concerns led to cancellation of school in Rock Valley on Friday, March 15. The Rock River at Rock Valley (gage site RKVI4) reached the third highest stage on record cresting 4.61 feet above flood stage on March 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90. As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River above Canton (gage site CBSS2) crested 2.03 feet above flood stage on July 18, impacting mainly crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With afternoon destabilization of the air mass, reaching 1500-2000 J/kg MLCAPE, scattered thunderstorms developed along the outflow pool from earlier decaying convection.  Deep-layer shear remained quite marginal, supporting multi-cell organization with brief stronger updrafts resulting in sub-severe hail.

Reports from the 13h through the 19th in this entry. ||A relatively deep and widespread snowpack existed across the region during early to mid March. Many areas in central and northern Iowa had around a foot to two feet of snow on the ground, along with many areas in upstream river basins in Minnesota and the Dakotas having similar or deeper snowpacks on the ground. Additionally, with the below normal temperatures throughout the majority of the 2019 year to date, soils were frozen to depths of a foot or more throughout the aforementioned areas. All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported US 18 closed between Britt and Garner due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of increased releases from Gavins Point Dam and the widespread 2 to 4 inches of rainfall between May 21 and May 27 across much of southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska led to flooding of the lower Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increased releases from upstream Gavins Point Dam along with runoff from heavy rainfall over the preceding week produced minor flooding on the Missouri River at Sioux City (gage site SSCN1). Hamilton Boulevard and a few park areas along the river near Interstate 29 were flooded as the river rose to 0.22 feet above flood stage on May 31. The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system pushed through during the early morning hours of July 17. Runoff from rainfall of 2 to 4 inches upstream of Rock Rapids resulted in a very brief period of minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall early on July 17 measured 2.45 inches at Rock Rapids and 1.82 inches 3 miles north of Rock Rapids.  Runoff from the rainfall brought the Rock River at Rock Rapids (gage site RAPI4) to a crest of 0.17 feet above flood stage at 1545CST on July 17.  Impacts were generally limited to brief flooding of agricultural lands near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 2.12 inches at Hawarden and 1.75 inches at Akron) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. South Dakota Highway 50 and 48 were both inundated by water for over two weeks, along with the bridge east of Jefferson. The Big Sioux River near Akron (gage site AKRI4) reached the second highest crest on record at 7.82 feet above flood stage on March 16. The river remained in moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

South Dakota Highway 50 and Iowa Highway 3 were both inundated by water for numerous days, with several farm buildings engulfed in flood waters. The Big Sioux River near Richland (gage site BSRS2) reached the second highest crest on record at 7.5 feet above flood stage on March 16. The river remained in moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

The Floyd River at Alton (gage site ALTI4) reached the second highest stage on record cresting 8.64 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system developed on the lee side of the Rockies late November 25th and November 26th. As it departed the front range, it moved eastward through Kansas before turning northeasterly into Iowa and the Great Lakes Region during the evening of the 26th and through the 27th. While substantially cold temperatures were not in place within the area or north of the area, it was cold enough for snow to be the primary precipitation type across much of northwest and northern Iowa. Areas further south remained warm enough to see predominantly rain. As the system departed, it also provided a number of hours of strong gusty winds due to a tight surface pressure gradient on the backside of the surface low. With around 2 to 6 inches of snowfall and winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, travel was hazardous at times, including significantly reduced visibilities. Fortunately, much of the worst conditions occurred during the evening and overnight hours.

All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported water over the road has resulted in the evacuation of 2 to 3 homes at Nelson Ave and Riverside Trail near Coalville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.42 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands and several county roads north of Iowa Highway 9. Little Sioux River near Montgomery (gage site MLSI4) reached the second highest stage on record cresting 3.32 feet above flood stage on March 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October started with another round of showers and storms moving across northeast Iowa. Some of these storms produced locally heavy rains across the southeast section of Clayton County. Radar estimates indicated rainfall amounts were generally under two inches, but with the rain falling on already saturated soils, rapid runoff occurred. Rabbit Creek near Edgewood (Clayton County) went out of its banks with flood waters covering Glacier Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from locally heavy rains pushed Rabbit Creek out of its banks near Edgewood with the flood waters covering Glacier Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet dug into the southwestern U.S. on November 25 and ejected into the plains by early November 26, where a surface low pressure area began to organize in the southern High Plains. The mid-level trough and surface cyclone intensified rapidly lifting toward the western Great Lakes by the morning of November 27. With sharp baroclinicity in place, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall from 4 to 9 inches blanketed far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and much of southwest Minnesota. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph near and east of Interstate 29, which resulted in periodic visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile. Some heavier snowfall amounts across northwest Iowa included 8.6 inches at Sanborn, 8.2 inches at Spencer, 8.1 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford, 7.0 inches at Storm Lake and Cherokee, 6.5 inches at Primghar, and 5.9 inches at Sioux City and Le Mars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With already saturated soils from a wet September, runoff from more rainfall events caused flooding to occur along the Mississippi River. The river went out of its banks at McGregor and Guttenberg (Clayton County) during the middle part of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from rainfall events pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks at McGregor. The river crested almost a foot above the flood stage at 16.77 feet on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed on a southwest to northeast  orientated front across southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms produced two tornado touchdowns, one near Shenandoah and the other near Red Oak.  No damage was reported.  Also the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall amounts with 2.5 inches reported in Oakland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet dug into the southwestern U.S. on November 25 and ejected into the plains by early November 26, where a surface low pressure area began to organize in the southern High Plains. The mid-level trough and surface cyclone intensified rapidly lifting toward the western Great Lakes by the morning of November 27. With sharp baroclinicity in place, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall from 4 to 9 inches blanketed far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and much of southwest Minnesota. The snow was accompanied by northwest winds with gusts as high as 35 to 50 mph near and east of Interstate 29, which resulted in periodic visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile. Some heavier snowfall amounts across northwest Iowa included 8.6 inches at Sanborn, 8.2 inches at Spencer, 8.1 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford, 7.0 inches at Storm Lake and Cherokee, 6.5 inches at Primghar, and 5.9 inches at Sioux City and Le Mars.

A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.20 inches at Lake Park and 1.78 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands, with water inundating 125th St. northeast of Lake Park.  The West Fork of the Little Sioux River near Lake Park (gage site LPRI4) crested once on March 14 at moderate flood stage, and then reached the second highest stage on record cresting 2.79 feet above flood stage on March 18.

A surface low pressure system developed on the lee side of the Rockies late November 25th and November 26th. As it departed the front range, it moved eastward through Kansas before turning northeasterly into Iowa and the Great Lakes Region during the evening of the 26th and through the 27th. While substantially cold temperatures were not in place within the area or north of the area, it was cold enough for snow to be the primary precipitation type across much of northwest and northern Iowa. Areas further south remained warm enough to see predominantly rain. As the system departed, it also provided a number of hours of strong gusty winds due to a tight surface pressure gradient on the backside of the surface low. With around 2 to 6 inches of snowfall and winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, travel was hazardous at times, including significantly reduced visibilities. Fortunately, much of the worst conditions occurred during the evening and overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than average snowfall through the winter created a wet snowpack across much of Iowa which was still present into early March. A large storm system impacted the region through the middle of March which brought heavy rain and warm temperatures to the state. This system allowed much of the snowpack to melt across the state within a few days to a week. This heavy rain fell on frozen ground allowing nearly all of the rainfall and snowmelt to runoff into the river systems. This led to flooding on nearly all rivers and nearly all river points throughout central Iowa. The majority of the river flooding had ended with rivers returning to their banks by late March. However portions of the upper Des Moines Basin remained in flood into April. These points were the Des Moines River at Fort Dodge, and the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville, Emmetsburg, and Humboldt. Many of the rises on the rivers were also due to ice jams that formed on the river channel due to break up jams as temperatures warmed and river levels rose.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek at New Hartford crested at 13.21 feet on 15 March 2019 at 09:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A juicy atmosphere with a lack of organizational support (effective bulk shear) made for a messy convective environment across portions of the state on the 16th. During the morning hours convection from central and eastern Nebraska drifted into western Iowa, producing heavy rainfall. Another round of developed across northwest Iowa during the morning and rolled across northern Iowa and southern Minnesota, producing a lone severe wind gust. Later in the afternoon, storms were able to initiate along old outflow boundaries and were able to produce localized heavy rainfall, a weak tornado, and a other funnel cloud reports. ||While support was lackluster, there were indications for the possibility of a funnel cloud or weak tornado during the afternoon with supportive SBCAPE in excess of 2000 J/kg, weak boundaries in the area, and most importantly very supportive 0-3km cape values in excess of 150 J/kg for low level stretching.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville Municipal Airport ASOS recorded a wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to recent days, the atmosphere was highly unstable with SB/MUCAPE values in excess of 3000 J/kg, but was devoid of large scale forcing and ability to organize any storms. Specifically on this day, another weak boundary/outflow boundary was situated in the area that was enough to initiate storms by mid afternoon. Other conditions included weak mean layer winds, negligible bulk effective shear, and precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 inches. All told, the situation was ripe for slow moving storms to produce heavy rainfall and flooding potential over the area, which came to fruition in a few isolated cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported numerous county roads across southern Kossuth County closed and/or washed out due to flash flooding from the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight convection originating in south central South Dakota made its way across southern South Dakota overnight and eventually turned southeast as it approached Iowa. Through the morning hours conditions along and ahead of the line of convection included 30 to 40+ kts of 0-6km shear roughly orthogonal to the line, MUCAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg, precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches, and consistent moisture transport into the region. All in all, a setup for consistent and sustained convection through the morning and afternoon hours. Fortunately, with the progressive nature no updrafts were able to produce severe hail. Given the linear nature, numerous reports of strong winds were received, but only a couple severe level reports. Additionally, heavy rainfall was reported with a few locations picking up in excess of 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public relayed report form personal weather station of wind gusts of 58+ mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was juiced so to speak ahead of a cold front sliding into the region. MUCAPE values very quickly approached and exceeded 4000 J/kg by mid-morning. With the cold front and previous storm outflows the impetus for new convection, discrete storms were not expected, with the prevailing storm type being the linear or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) variety. Additional support for the QLCS mode was seen with, once again the cold frontal and outflow forcing, highly unstable atmosphere, and little to no convective inhibition.  Additional support for strong winds was seen with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg across much of the area  Once a system began to produce a decent cold pool, there would be little to prevent it from progressing. ||That was evident in Iowa as two distinct QLCSs moved through. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup was not spectacular for sustained or widespread severe weather, but did lay the ground for some isolated issues. SB/MUCAPE values built up into the 2000-4000 J/kg range by the early afternoon with the help of dew points into the low to mid 70s and at least semi-supportive lapse rates. While there was not large scale lift, there was a weak boundary laid out across central Iowa from west to east, in part remnants from previous night and morning storms. Fortunately, effective bulk shear values were below 30 knots, reducing the potential for widespread severe weather potential. All in all, with unstable conditions and weak flow, heavy rainfall and flooding become the primary issues for the day though a few other reports including damaging winds and a funnel cloud were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.52 inches near Le Mars) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. A few county roads near Le Mars were inundated, including 160th St. near Highway 60. Water covered a large amount of the airport near crest time. The recreation trail along the river was destroyed, and most bridges damaged at the municipal golf course.  A gas line was sheared off by ice moving through the flood waters, severing service to 95 residences. The Floyd River at Le Mars (gage site LMRI4) reached the third highest stage on record cresting 5.84 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight convection originating in south central South Dakota made its way across southern South Dakota overnight and eventually turned southeast as it approached Iowa. Through the morning hours conditions along and ahead of the line of convection included 30 to 40+ kts of 0-6km shear roughly orthogonal to the line, MUCAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg, precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 to 1.75 inches, and consistent moisture transport into the region. All in all, a setup for consistent and sustained convection through the morning and afternoon hours. Fortunately, with the progressive nature no updrafts were able to produce severe hail. Given the linear nature, numerous reports of strong winds were received, but only a couple severe level reports. Additionally, heavy rainfall was reported with a few locations picking up in excess of 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 2.01 inches. Beginning time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was juiced so to speak ahead of a cold front sliding into the region. MUCAPE values very quickly approached and exceeded 4000 J/kg by mid-morning. With the cold front and previous storm outflows the impetus for new convection, discrete storms were not expected, with the prevailing storm type being the linear or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) variety. Additional support for the QLCS mode was seen with, once again the cold frontal and outflow forcing, highly unstable atmosphere, and little to no convective inhibition.  Additional support for strong winds was seen with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg across much of the area  Once a system began to produce a decent cold pool, there would be little to prevent it from progressing. ||That was evident in Iowa as two distinct QLCSs moved through. First in the morning hours across northern Iowa, and second during the late morning through evening hours from central Iowa and on toward south central and southeast Iowa. In both cases, damaging winds widely prevailed. Across northern Iowa the gust front roared out ahead of the line of convection, continuing to produce severe wind reports in areas of no storms well to the south and east. In central and southeast Iowa, widespread reports of 60 to 70 mph winds and various tree damage (from large limbs to entire trees) were received along a path from roughly Perry, IA through Ottumwa, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some inflatables were blown away from a town celebration. Two injuries were sustained at the town celebration from outdoor flying objects. There were no thunderstorms in the area, as they were to the north, but the strong gust front from the storms up north produced the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm frontal boundary found itself situated across much of central Iowa on the morning of the 18th. With sufficient low level (remnant low level jet) into the boundary, ample moisture, and resulting MUCAPE values around 2000-3000 J/kg, a string of storms were able to fire up during the morning hours. Other supportive elements for organized storms were lackluster, including effective bulk shear under 30 kts. On the up side, CAPE values within the hail growth zone approached 1000 J/kg in some areas, along with DCAPE values approaching 1200 J/kg, lending the potential for brief near-severe to severe hail and winds. Ultimately, few reports were received across the larger area, but the Iowa Falls area did experience both severe hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported pea hail beginning around 841 am local time that grew to nickel to quarter size by 845 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90. As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River above Canton (gage site CBSS2) crested 0.38 feet above flood stage on July 10.  Flooding impact was limited to inundation of crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter weather commenced late evening on Thursday, November 28 with a mix of light snow and freezing rain, which produced up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation before surface temperatures warmed above freezing on the morning of November 30.  A narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall enhanced over the area during the afternoon and evening of November 30, producing 6.9 inches of snowfall at Lake Park and 3.4 inches at Spirit Lake.

Additional support for strong winds was seen with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg across much of the area  Once a system began to produce a decent cold pool, there would be little to prevent it from progressing. ||That was evident in Iowa as two distinct QLCSs moved through. First in the morning hours across northern Iowa, and second during the late morning through evening hours from central Iowa and on toward south central and southeast Iowa. In both cases, damaging winds widely prevailed. Across northern Iowa the gust front roared out ahead of the line of convection, continuing to produce severe wind reports in areas of no storms well to the south and east. In central and southeast Iowa, widespread reports of 60 to 70 mph winds and various tree damage (from large limbs to entire trees) were received along a path from roughly Perry, IA through Ottumwa, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb nearly 1 foot in diameter was broken from the gust front. There was no thunderstorm, as they were to the north, but the strong gust front from the storms up north produced the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed in a very warm, humid, and unstable airmass ahead of a cold front across eastern Iowa during the evening of July 2nd. A thunderstorm produced damaging winds that knocked a tree onto a house in Jones County.  Another thunderstorm produced heavy rains of 2 to 3 inches that caused flash flooding in the city of Clinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported that water was approximately 18 inches deep near Clinton Community College. Some vehicles were stuck in the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast Iowa sweltered under two days of excessive heat in the middle of July.  Heat indices topped out between 100 and 110 on July 18th and between 105 and 120 on July 19th.  Air temperatures during this heat wave generally topped out around 90 while dew points climbed into the lower 80s. The highest calculated heat index was 117 from a mesonet station near McGregor (Clayton County) and the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 100 to 105 were common across Allamakee County on July 18 and 19th. The highest calculated heat index was 105 from a mesonet station near Waterville.
