EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional light freezing drizzle began during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and resulted in a few hundredths of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was juiced so to speak ahead of a cold front sliding into the region. MUCAPE values very quickly approached and exceeded 4000 J/kg by mid-morning. With the cold front and previous storm outflows the impetus for new convection, discrete storms were not expected, with the prevailing storm type being the linear or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) variety. Additional support for the QLCS mode was seen with, once again the cold frontal and outflow forcing, highly unstable atmosphere, and little to no convective inhibition.  Additional support for strong winds was seen with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg across much of the area  Once a system began to produce a decent cold pool, there would be little to prevent it from progressing. ||That was evident in Iowa as two distinct QLCSs moved through. First in the morning hours across northern Iowa, and second during the late morning through evening hours from central Iowa and on toward south central and southeast Iowa. In both cases, damaging winds widely prevailed. Across northern Iowa the gust front roared out ahead of the line of convection, continuing to produce severe wind reports in areas of no storms well to the south and east. In central and southeast Iowa, widespread reports of 60 to 70 mph winds and various tree damage (from large limbs to entire trees) were received along a path from roughly Perry, IA through Ottumwa, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported estimated 60 mph gusts. A few larger tree limbs were reported down, with several smaller branches and twigs down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October started with another round of showers and storms moving across northeast Iowa. Some of these storms produced locally heavy rains across the southeast section of Clayton County. Radar estimates indicated rainfall amounts were generally under two inches, but with the rain falling on already saturated soils, rapid runoff occurred. Rabbit Creek near Edgewood (Clayton County) went out of its banks with flood waters covering Glacier Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from recent rains pushed the Turkey River out of its banks for the second time in less than a week in Garber. The river crested less than a half foot above the flood stage at 17.38 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.52 inches near Le Mars and 1.97 inches near Sioux Center) resulted in flooding of agricultural lands. Perry Creek near Hinton (gage site PCHI4) reached a crest 2.43 feet above flood stage on March 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of showers and thunderstorms progressed across southeast Iowa during the afternoon of July 17th. These storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 MPH at times, causing damage to roofs, carports, siding, and uprooted or damaged trees. Additionally, torrential rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were reported. The torrential rains caused brief flash flooding in the city of Keokuk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of September 12th. These storms produced a weak tornado in the vicinity of Ossian (Winneshiek County) that damaged some farm buildings and numerous trees. A second tornado occurred northwest of Farmersburg (Clayton County). The damage from this tornado was limited to a few trees, some outbuildings and one home had branches impaled through a wall. In Marquette and McGregor (Clayton County) runoff from the heavy rains turned streets into mini-rivers leaving behind mud covered streets and sidewalks. State Highway 76 north of Marquette was closed because of a mudslide and downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down northwest of Farmersburg and moved northeast before dissipating southwest of Monona. Damage from the tornado was limited to some trees, a few outbuildings, and crops. One home did have some tree branches impaled through a wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup was not spectacular for sustained or widespread severe weather, but did lay the ground for some isolated issues. SB/MUCAPE values built up into the 2000-4000 J/kg range by the early afternoon with the help of dew points into the low to mid 70s and at least semi-supportive lapse rates. While there was not large scale lift, there was a weak boundary laid out across central Iowa from west to east, in part remnants from previous night and morning storms. Fortunately, effective bulk shear values were below 30 knots, reducing the potential for widespread severe weather potential. All in all, with unstable conditions and weak flow, heavy rainfall and flooding become the primary issues for the day though a few other reports including damaging winds and a funnel cloud were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public relayed picture of downed tree limbs about 6 inches in diameter and greater, and some minor crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Ottumwa remained in flood from the month of June. It was mainly in flood due to water releases upstream at Lake Red Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Ottumwa had already crested in June and slowly fell through the first portion of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Traditional severe weather parameters were lackluster during this event with MUCAPE values predominantly in the 500 to 1500 J/kg range and negligible effective bulk shear. Even precipitable water values, low level moisture transport, and low level jet strength were on the lower end of the spectrum. One item that was in favor of storm initiation and heavy rainfall though was the presence of a stationary boundary across central Iowa much of July 2nd and 3rd. With the boundary in place and moist low levels, it did not take much to initiate multiple rounds of storms. The primary rounds were seen during the mid to late afternoon on the 2nd, near peak heating, and then overnight, aided by a weak low level jet. By the morning of the 3rd, a number of stations around Fort Dodge and down towards Ames had reported between 2 and 4.5 inches of rainfall. While most locations did not have any water issues, the Fort Dodge urban area did experience a period of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 4.5 inches via rain gage picture. Beginning time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was juiced so to speak ahead of a cold front sliding into the region. MUCAPE values very quickly approached and exceeded 4000 J/kg by mid-morning. With the cold front and previous storm outflows the impetus for new convection, discrete storms were not expected, with the prevailing storm type being the linear or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) variety. Additional support for the QLCS mode was seen with, once again the cold frontal and outflow forcing, highly unstable atmosphere, and little to no convective inhibition.  Additional support for strong winds was seen with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg across much of the area  Once a system began to produce a decent cold pool, there would be little to prevent it from progressing. ||That was evident in Iowa as two distinct QLCSs moved through. First in the morning hours across northern Iowa, and second during the late morning through evening hours from central Iowa and on toward south central and southeast Iowa. In both cases, damaging winds widely prevailed. Across northern Iowa the gust front roared out ahead of the line of convection, continuing to produce severe wind reports in areas of no storms well to the south and east. In central and southeast Iowa, widespread reports of 60 to 70 mph winds and various tree damage (from large limbs to entire trees) were received along a path from roughly Perry, IA through Ottumwa, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mason City Municipal Airport recorded a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of showers and thunderstorms progressed across southeast Iowa during the afternoon of July 17th. These storms produced damaging wind gusts up to 70 MPH at times, causing damage to roofs, carports, siding, and uprooted or damaged trees. Additionally, torrential rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were reported. The torrential rains caused brief flash flooding in the city of Keokuk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that two inches of rain fell with passing storms. This caused isolated flash flooding in Keokuk. An intersection that normally floods during storms had water that came up much deeper than normal and was approximately 4 feet deep.  The water appeared to come up 2 feet on the doors of some businesses. The water had receded by the time of the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of occasional light snowfall and freezing drizzle began during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 29. Snowfall totals reached 2.0 inches in Sioux Center and  1.9 inches in Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa on July 20th.  The first round occurred during the early afternoon, as thunderstorms neared the Mississippi River in eastern Dubuque County.  These storms intensified and produced thunderstorm winds that damaged some roofs in eastern Dubuque. This line of storms also produced a funnel cloud in Cedar County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that several 1 to 2 inch tree branches were down near their residence. They also noted that some shingles were blown off their shed.  The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer and law enforcement reported that a funnel cloud went down half way to the ground before going back up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were two rounds of severe thunderstorms on July 20th.  The second round of storms came in the late afternoon and early evening, and impacted areas along and southwest of a line from Sigourney to Burlington. Strong thunderstorm wind gusts led to several reports of downed limbs and power lines across Van Buren and Keokuk Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported tree limbs down on power lines. The tree limb was on fire. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup was not spectacular for sustained or widespread severe weather, but did lay the ground for some isolated issues. SB/MUCAPE values built up into the 2000-4000 J/kg range by the early afternoon with the help of dew points into the low to mid 70s and at least semi-supportive lapse rates. While there was not large scale lift, there was a weak boundary laid out across central Iowa from west to east, in part remnants from previous night and morning storms. Fortunately, effective bulk shear values were below 30 knots, reducing the potential for widespread severe weather potential. All in all, with unstable conditions and weak flow, heavy rainfall and flooding become the primary issues for the day though a few other reports including damaging winds and a funnel cloud were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public relayed picture of running water over South Bell Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa during the early evening. These low topped supercells interacted with a warm front that was draped across eastern Iowa and prodcued two brief tornado touchdowns near Walcott and Donahue. No damage was reported or observed with these touchdowns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa State Troopers observed a brief tornado near the intersection of 230th Street and 60th Avenue, just north of the I-80 Truckstop in Walcott. The tornado caused no damage from which the National Weather Service could assign an EF-scale rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon with one thunderstorm producing a funnel cloud near Manchester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate reports of a funnel cloud north of Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast Iowa sweltered under two days of excessive heat in the middle of July.  Heat indices topped out between 100 and 110 on July 18th and between 105 and 120 on July 19th.  Air temperatures during this heat wave generally topped out around 90 while dew points climbed into the lower 80s. The highest calculated heat index was 117 from a mesonet station near McGregor (Clayton County) and the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 100 to 115 were common across Floyd County on July 18th and 19th. The highest calculated heat index was 113 from the automated weather observing equipment at the Charles City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast Iowa sweltered under two days of excessive heat in the middle of July.  Heat indices topped out between 100 and 110 on July 18th and between 105 and 120 on July 19th.  Air temperatures during this heat wave generally topped out around 90 while dew points climbed into the lower 80s. The highest calculated heat index was 117 from a mesonet station near McGregor (Clayton County) and the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105 to 115 were common across Howard County on July 19th. The highest calculated heat index was 116 from a mesonet station in Saratoga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup was not spectacular for sustained or widespread severe weather, but did lay the ground for some isolated issues. SB/MUCAPE values built up into the 2000-4000 J/kg range by the early afternoon with the help of dew points into the low to mid 70s and at least semi-supportive lapse rates. While there was not large scale lift, there was a weak boundary laid out across central Iowa from west to east, in part remnants from previous night and morning storms. Fortunately, effective bulk shear values were below 30 knots, reducing the potential for widespread severe weather potential. All in all, with unstable conditions and weak flow, heavy rainfall and flooding become the primary issues for the day though a few other reports including damaging winds and a funnel cloud were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated rain gage recorded heavy rainfall of 2.60 inches. Beginning time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to recent days, the atmosphere was highly unstable with SB/MUCAPE values in excess of 3000 J/kg, but was devoid of large scale forcing and ability to organize any storms. Specifically on this day, another weak boundary/outflow boundary was situated in the area that was enough to initiate storms by mid afternoon. Other conditions included weak mean layer winds, negligible bulk effective shear, and precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 inches. All told, the situation was ripe for slow moving storms to produce heavy rainfall and flooding potential over the area, which came to fruition in a few isolated cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.06 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.27 inches at Spencer and 1.04 inches 4 miles east of Sioux Rapids) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural land. The Little Sioux River at Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) reached the highest stage on record for the fourth time in nine months, cresting 6.13 feet above flood stage on March 16. The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.52 inches near Le Mars) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands, as well as county road C70. The Floyd River at James (gage site JMEI4) reached the highest stage on record cresting 5.48 feet above flood stage on March 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Traditional severe weather parameters were lackluster during this event with MUCAPE values predominantly in the 500 to 1500 J/kg range and negligible effective bulk shear. Even precipitable water values, low level moisture transport, and low level jet strength were on the lower end of the spectrum. One item that was in favor of storm initiation and heavy rainfall though was the presence of a stationary boundary across central Iowa much of July 2nd and 3rd. With the boundary in place and moist low levels, it did not take much to initiate multiple rounds of storms. The primary rounds were seen during the mid to late afternoon on the 2nd, near peak heating, and then overnight, aided by a weak low level jet. By the morning of the 3rd, a number of stations around Fort Dodge and down towards Ames had reported between 2 and 4.5 inches of rainfall. While most locations did not have any water issues, the Fort Dodge urban area did experience a period of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 3.8 inches via picture of rain gage. Beginning time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was juiced so to speak ahead of a cold front sliding into the region. MUCAPE values very quickly approached and exceeded 4000 J/kg by mid-morning. With the cold front and previous storm outflows the impetus for new convection, discrete storms were not expected, with the prevailing storm type being the linear or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) variety. Additional support for the QLCS mode was seen with, once again the cold frontal and outflow forcing, highly unstable atmosphere, and little to no convective inhibition.  Additional support for strong winds was seen with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg across much of the area  Once a system began to produce a decent cold pool, there would be little to prevent it from progressing. ||That was evident in Iowa as two distinct QLCSs moved through. First in the morning hours across northern Iowa, and second during the late morning through evening hours from central Iowa and on toward south central and southeast Iowa. In both cases, damaging winds widely prevailed. Across northern Iowa the gust front roared out ahead of the line of convection, continuing to produce severe wind reports in areas of no storms well to the south and east. In central and southeast Iowa, widespread reports of 60 to 70 mph winds and various tree damage (from large limbs to entire trees) were received along a path from roughly Perry, IA through Ottumwa, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported penny sized hail, as well as sapling trees snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While it was not a complete slam dunk, it was a fairly textbook setup for potential funnel clouds across northern Iowa. A stationary boundary was draped across northern Iowa with attendant SBCAPE values in excess of 3000 J/kg, 0-3 km CAPE values around 100 J/kg, and LCL values around or just above 1000 m.  By the  early evening hours, storms initiated along the boundary, producing periods mostly periods of heavy rain and small hail. One funnel cloud report was received in the Curlew, IA area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spencer Radio Group relayed public image of a funnel cloud south of Curlew. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain, rain, rain was the name of the game here. An open wave upper level trough was sliding through the region, with an attendant surface cold frontal boundary moving through Iowa later on the 28th and through the 29th. Overall, the setup pointed towards fairly long periods of rainfall with consistent southwest low level flow, precipitable water values approaching 1.8 to 2.0 inches, and a relatively slow moving boundary.  Ultimately heavy rainfall was all that was experienced, with a few locations clocking in in excess of 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.3 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional light freezing drizzle began during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and resulted in a few hundredths of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was juiced so to speak ahead of a cold front sliding into the region. MUCAPE values very quickly approached and exceeded 4000 J/kg by mid-morning. With the cold front and previous storm outflows the impetus for new convection, discrete storms were not expected, with the prevailing storm type being the linear or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) variety. Additional support for the QLCS mode was seen with, once again the cold frontal and outflow forcing, highly unstable atmosphere, and little to no convective inhibition.  Additional support for strong winds was seen with DCAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg across much of the area  Once a system began to produce a decent cold pool, there would be little to prevent it from progressing. ||That was evident in Iowa as two distinct QLCSs moved through. First in the morning hours across northern Iowa, and second during the late morning through evening hours from central Iowa and on toward south central and southeast Iowa. In both cases, damaging winds widely prevailed. Across northern Iowa the gust front roared out ahead of the line of convection, continuing to produce severe wind reports in areas of no storms well to the south and east. In central and southeast Iowa, widespread reports of 60 to 70 mph winds and various tree damage (from large limbs to entire trees) were received along a path from roughly Perry, IA through Ottumwa, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ottumwa Regional Airport ASOS recorded a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup was not spectacular for sustained or widespread severe weather, but did lay the ground for some isolated issues. SB/MUCAPE values built up into the 2000-4000 J/kg range by the early afternoon with the help of dew points into the low to mid 70s and at least semi-supportive lapse rates. While there was not large scale lift, there was a weak boundary laid out across central Iowa from west to east, in part remnants from previous night and morning storms. Fortunately, effective bulk shear values were below 30 knots, reducing the potential for widespread severe weather potential. All in all, with unstable conditions and weak flow, heavy rainfall and flooding become the primary issues for the day though a few other reports including damaging winds and a funnel cloud were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flooding causing road closures across portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to recent days, the atmosphere was highly unstable with SB/MUCAPE values in excess of 3000 J/kg, but was devoid of large scale forcing and ability to organize any storms. Specifically on this day, another weak boundary/outflow boundary was situated in the area that was enough to initiate storms by mid afternoon. Other conditions included weak mean layer winds, negligible bulk effective shear, and precipitable water values in excess of 1.5 inches. All told, the situation was ripe for slow moving storms to produce heavy rainfall and flooding potential over the area, which came to fruition in a few isolated cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported some road closures in portions of the county. Some  kayakers required rescue as flood waters rose on an area river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening along an outflow-reinforced frontal zone as a zonal pattern aloft sent a minor wave rippling from central South Dakota into southern Minnesota during the evening.  A low-level jet of 30 knots interacted with the boundary to create a favorable environment for redeveloping and training storms over a several hour period, which progressed very slowly southward through portions of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burr Oak Creek above Perkins (gage site BOCI4) responded to heavy rainfall during the early evening of July 1 by vaulting quickly to a moderate flood stage, with a crest of 3.05 feet above flood stage at 2145CST.  Extensive flooding of agricultural land was the primary impact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado remained in open cropland east and northeast of Minburn.  No damage indicators were impacted, therefore the EFU rating.  The tornado path was discovered using high resolution satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 0.77 inches 3 miles northeast of Battle Creek and 1.42 inches 4 miles south southeast of Arthur) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands. Levees in Ida Grove were nearly topped.  Iowa Highway 175 was closed between Ida Grove and Battle Creek due to water flowing over the roadway. The Maple River at Ida Grove  (gage site MIGI4) reached the highest stage on record cresting 9.76 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain, rain, rain was the name of the game here. An open wave upper level trough was sliding through the region, with an attendant surface cold frontal boundary moving through Iowa later on the 28th and through the 29th. Overall, the setup pointed towards fairly long periods of rainfall with consistent southwest low level flow, precipitable water values approaching 1.8 to 2.0 inches, and a relatively slow moving boundary.  Ultimately heavy rainfall was all that was experienced, with a few locations clocking in in excess of 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamoni Municipal Airport ASOS recorded heavy rainfall of 2.31 inches. Beginning and ending times estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor agricultural flooding resulted as the West Branch of the Floyd River near Struble (gage site STUI4) crested 0.27 feet above flood stage early on July 2.

The Little Sioux River at Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) reached the highest stage on record for the fourth time in nine months, cresting 6.13 feet above flood stage on March 16.  The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Close on the heels of the last winter storm, an amplified upper-air pattern brought another impulse out of the southwestern U.S. early on Friday, November 29 and pushed a shield of light mixed precipitation northeastward across the region with a light accumulation of ice. As the main trough and area of low pressure organized on Saturday, November 30 across Nebraska and deepened as it moved into southern Wisconsin by the morning of Sunday, December 1, additional snowfall developed and was accompanied by strong and gusty northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph by later Saturday night into Sunday morning.||Snowfall totals over the period generally ranged from 6 to 16 inches west of the James River, to less than one inch from northeast Nebraska and far southeast South Dakota toward southern portions of northwest Iowa. Another mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurred on Saturday, November 30 near the Minnesota-Iowa border with between 6 and 9 inches accumulation.||This storm continued into December 1, although a majority of accumulating snowfall ended between 0000 and 0900CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of occasional light snowfall and freezing drizzle began during the evening of Thursday, November 28, and resulted in up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation through the early morning of November 29. Snowfall totals were minimal, only 0.5 inches in Remsen and 0.2 inches in Le Mars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.67 inches at Cherokee) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands and numerous state, county and township roads. The Little Sioux River at Cherokee (gage site CHKI4) reached the highest stage on record cresting 11.40 feet above flood stage on March 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A juicy atmosphere with a lack of organizational support (effective bulk shear) made for a messy convective environment across portions of the state on the 16th. During the morning hours convection from central and eastern Nebraska drifted into western Iowa, producing heavy rainfall. Another round of developed across northwest Iowa during the morning and rolled across northern Iowa and southern Minnesota, producing a lone severe wind gust. Later in the afternoon, storms were able to initiate along old outflow boundaries and were able to produce localized heavy rainfall, a weak tornado, and a other funnel cloud reports. ||While support was lackluster, there were indications for the possibility of a funnel cloud or weak tornado during the afternoon with supportive SBCAPE in excess of 2000 J/kg, weak boundaries in the area, and most importantly very supportive 0-3km cape values in excess of 150 J/kg for low level stretching.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weak tornado captured on camera/video. Remained over open land and was rated EF-Unknown as no damage indicators were impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90. As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) crested 2.99 feet above flood stage on July 6. After less than 24 hours below flood stage during July 14-15, a secondary crest of 3.92 feet above flood stage occurred on July 19, resulting in significant flooding of crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90. As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from June 2019. There were several crests through July, but the most significant for the Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) was a crest 2.54 feet above flood stage on July 19. Significant flooding of crop and pasture land persisted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Relentless rainfall during July 2019 brought basin averages from 6 to 10 inches north of Interstate 90 and 4 to 6 inches south of Interstate 90. As a result, flooding along the Big Sioux River either continued, or was renewed, during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from June, the Big Sioux River near Richland (gage site BSRS2) crested 2.08 feet above flood stage on July 7 and 2.36 feet above flood stage on July 20, resulting in significant flooding of crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved east across the area during the early morning hours of Sunday, August 18. Small hail and downed trees were seen in the Cedar Rapids area and in Cedar county. A brief tornado occurred near Grand Mound, IA around 5:30 am as the storms rolled through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inch diameter branches were blown down between Durant and Bennett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted much of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri early Tuesday morning. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While conditions were not ripe for widespread thunderstorm activity with lackluster forcing and other support, conditions were in place for the potential of a few funnel clouds. Namely, low level 0-3 km CAPE values were around 100 J/kg, lifted condensation levels were around 500 m, and at least weak surface vorticity were present. By the end of the day, only one funnel cloud report was received between Woolstock and Webster City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local fire department reported a brief funnel cloud south of Woolstock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall it was a quiet day weather wise with ongoing northerly to northwesterly flow at the surface and aloft. Initially stratus dominated the across the state before diurnal heating was able to break the deck out into cellular clouds. By the afternoon, a few isolated showers were able to develop across areas of central and eastern Iowa with nominal SBCAPE around 1000 J/kg. Conditions did lend themselves to a possible funnel cloud with lifted condensation levels around 750 m, 0-3 km CAPE around 75 to 100+ J/kg, and at least marginal surface vorticity from I-35 on eastward. A single funnel cloud report was received during the mid to late afternoon in far southeast Webster county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a brief funnel cloud that dissipated after a few minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly routine setup for strong to severe storms presented itself on the 5th of August for large portions of Iowa. A cold front approached from the north/northwest with warm (mid 80s deg F) and humid (low to mid 70s deg F) conditions. In support of potentially severe weather was effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts and SBCAPE values in excess of 3000 J/kg. Ultimately, widespread severe weather was not experienced, but a few storms were able to produce severe wind gusts and damage before congealing into a linear convective system that moved across the eastern half of the state from northwest to southeast. Fortunately, again, the line of storms remained mostly sub-severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public relayed pictures of tree limbs greater than 12 inches in diameter snapped. They estimated gusts to 70 mph. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Generally speaking, a bit of everything occurred during an overnight/early morning severe mesoscale convective system that rolled across Iowa. The predominant weather revolved around damaging winds and heavy rainfall, though a few instances of severe hail and low end tornadoes were experienced as well. Among the reports, numerous trees were downed, buildings damaged or destroyed from straight line winds or one of the weak tornadoes, and numerous instances of 2 to 4 plus inches of heavy rainfall. ||On the setup side of the coin, a lot of factors were in line for the event to potentially be a sustained nocturnal severe weather event. The previous day, the 19th, saw surface high pressure begin to exit the region eastward and allowing southerly return flow to work into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. Tied in with mid to upper 70 dew points and steep mid-level lapse rates, a large area of 4000 plus J/kg CAPE values developed across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa and slowly built eastward. Additionally, effect bulk shear was consistently in the 40 to 50 kt range and low level shear roughly oriented to expected storm motion. As the evening progressed and the low level jet finally began to ramp up, boosting warm air advection and positive moisture flux, storms began to develop and eventually congeal. Organization, at least with regards to strong to severe criteria, was not immediate, waiting until around 5am local time to produce its first severe report. Severe reports then continued as the system moved southeast over the next 4 plus hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The last of three tornadoes produced by a fast moving QLCS.  Tornado began west of Tracy in rural areas producing only crop and tree damage.  As the tornado moved through town, it produced mainly tree and minor structural damage to buildings in the town.  The tornado dissipated on the east side of Tracy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly routine setup for strong to severe storms presented itself on the 5th of August for large portions of Iowa. A cold front approached from the north/northwest with warm (mid 80s deg F) and humid (low to mid 70s deg F) conditions. In support of potentially severe weather was effective bulk shear around 30 to 40 kts and SBCAPE values in excess of 3000 J/kg. Ultimately, widespread severe weather was not experienced, but a few storms were able to produce severe wind gusts and damage before congealing into a linear convective system that moved across the eastern half of the state from northwest to southeast. Fortunately, again, the line of storms remained mostly sub-severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser reported 60 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the early morning hours of August 18th. One of these storms produced some damaging winds in and near Waukon (Allamakee County). Trees and crops were blown over, a camper was rolled onto its side and house windows were broken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with high winds produced damage in and near Waukon. Trees and crops were blown over, a camper was rolled onto its side and house windows were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone sagging slowly southward across the region interacted with a zone of slightly unstable air on its southern flank to produce a band of snowfall, which became heavy at times during several hours between 0800-1300CST. Snowfall was at such a rate (frequent visibility of less than a half mile) that numerous non-injury accidents occurred for many locations near and north of Interstate 90. Snowfall was generally from 3 to 5 inches over a 6 to 9 hour period. Greatest snowfall amounts were reported at Lester (6.0 inches) and Sibley (5.5 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was pivoting across the Northern Plains and the Canadian Plains August 17th, with a surface low reflection dragging a cold front through the Dakotas and northern Minnesota.  The aforementioned cold front triggered widespread storms during the afternoon hours of the 17th, slowly propagating eastward. By late evening, a portion of the line of storms formed into an organized linear convective system, with bowing segments, that entered northwest Iowa. Through the overnight hours, the system continued to move across Iowa from northwest to southeast, slowly losing its overall organization and threat for damaging winds. Even though it lost overall strength, with MUCAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg, precipitable water values in excess of 1.7 inches, and strong low level moisture influx, efficient and heavy rainfall continued as it moved across the state. Many areas received between 2 and 3 inches, with the majority of it falling in around 90 minutes or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported heavy rainfall of 1.04 inches in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The large scale pattern on the 28th and 29th was such that an upper level trough was moving across southern Canada and the northern United States, with its attendant surface low center over Hudson Bay. The surface cold front extended southwest out of the low all the way through Minnesota and back into parts of South Dakota and northern Nebraska by the early morning hours on the 29th. Widespread convection was not realized through much of the overnight, instead giving way to scattered storms forced by a combination of a marginal low level jet and good warm air and moisture advection. The environment yielded MUCAPE values around 2000 J/kg along with effective bulk shear in the neighborhood of 30 to 40 kts. While both DCAPE and hail CAPE values showed some concern for both threats, severe hail was the only threat realized from received reports as a few storms were able to sustain organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WHO TV relayed public report of quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was pivoting across the Northern Plains and the Canadian Plains August 17th, with a surface low reflection dragging a cold front through the Dakotas and northern Minnesota.  The aforementioned cold front triggered widespread storms during the afternoon hours of the 17th, slowly propagating eastward. By late evening, a portion of the line of storms formed into an organized linear convective system, with bowing segments, that entered northwest Iowa. Through the overnight hours, the system continued to move across Iowa from northwest to southeast, slowly losing its overall organization and threat for damaging winds. Even though it lost overall strength, with MUCAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg, precipitable water values in excess of 1.7 inches, and strong low level moisture influx, efficient and heavy rainfall continued as it moved across the state. Many areas received between 2 and 3 inches, with the majority of it falling in around 90 minutes or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.36 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of August 11th, an isolated thunderstorm developed over Linn County Iowa, near Cedar Rapids. This storm was weak in nature, but developed a highly visible funnel cloud, that at times, came very close to the ground. A tornado warning was issued, and numerous calls were received from the public and media about this funnel. Though it came very close to the ground, the continuous video and photos of this funnel at close range show that it did not touch the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous public reports of a funnel were received. Though clear evidence of a well developed funnel was reported, it appears the circulation never reached the ground.

A frontal zone sagging slowly southward across the region interacted with a zone of slightly unstable air on its southern flank to produce a band of snowfall, which became heavy at times during several hours between 0800-1300CST. Snowfall was at such a rate (frequent visibility of less than a half mile) that numerous non-injury accidents occurred for many locations near and north of Interstate 90. Snowfall was generally from 3 to 5 inches over a 6 to 9 hour period. Greatest snowfall amounts were reported at Lester (6.0 inches) and Sibley (5.5 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Generally speaking, a bit of everything occurred during an overnight/early morning severe mesoscale convective system that rolled across Iowa. The predominant weather revolved around damaging winds and heavy rainfall, though a few instances of severe hail and low end tornadoes were experienced as well. Among the reports, numerous trees were downed, buildings damaged or destroyed from straight line winds or one of the weak tornadoes, and numerous instances of 2 to 4 plus inches of heavy rainfall. ||On the setup side of the coin, a lot of factors were in line for the event to potentially be a sustained nocturnal severe weather event. The previous day, the 19th, saw surface high pressure begin to exit the region eastward and allowing southerly return flow to work into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. Tied in with mid to upper 70 dew points and steep mid-level lapse rates, a large area of 4000 plus J/kg CAPE values developed across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa and slowly built eastward. Additionally, effect bulk shear was consistently in the 40 to 50 kt range and low level shear roughly oriented to expected storm motion. As the evening progressed and the low level jet finally began to ramp up, boosting warm air advection and positive moisture flux, storms began to develop and eventually congeal. Organization, at least with regards to strong to severe criteria, was not immediate, waiting until around 5am local time to produce its first severe report. Severe reports then continued as the system moved southeast over the next 4 plus hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via mPING, trees uprooted or snapped; roof blown off. Time estimated from radar.

Small hail and downed trees were seen in the Cedar Rapids area and in Cedar county. A brief tornado occurred near Grand Mound, IA around 5:30 am as the storms rolled through the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The state found itself under broad upper level ridging to zonal flow, allowing for a weak cold front to slide into the state and stall around the Interstate 80 corridor during the evening and overnight hours. The environment was fairly ripe for thunderstorm activity, including 2000-3000+ J/kg SBCAPE and hail CAPE values around 500 J/kg, but lacked supportive effective bulk shear with values generally around 30 kts or less.  With the front stalling across central Iowa, and the thermodynamic environment what it was, thunderstorms predominantly lingered across central Iowa through the evening and overnight hours, producing periods of severe hail during the first couple of hours and transitioning to sub-severe storms and a predominant heavy rainfall threat. In the end, there were a number of reports of quarter to near golf ball sized hail, followed by heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3+ inches by the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported up to quarter sized hail along Pheasant Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was in place with a surface boundary stretched across Iowa from Storm Lake southeastward into southeast Iowa. This boundary provided the focus for thunderstorm development throughout the late afternoon and evening hours. The low level environment provided the needed conditions for many of these storms to rotate and produce funnel clouds and brief, weak tornadoes. Many of the tornadoes that occurred were brief touchdowns in open ares with no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mahaska County Sheriff, Poweshiek County Sheriff, and trained spotters reported on and off funnel clouds in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The hits just kept on rolling, with yet another pair of rounds of strong to severe storms, heavy rainfall, and flooding. Through much of the end of May, the synoptic setup across the region remained relatively unchanged, including this event. Upper level flow continued to be out of the SW, precipitable water values remained in excess of 1.5 inches, and a relatively stationary boundary remained over the area. In addition to that broad setup, MUCAPE values hovered in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, bulk effective shear was supportive in the 40 to 60 kts range, and low level moisture flow continued to move into the area.  ||The 28th saw two rounds of strong to severe storms move through from the west and southwest, each causing at least a few severe reports and contributing to overall heavy rainfall and eventually flash flooding. Severe reports were generally relegated to west central and southwest Iowa, where storms were strongest and able to utilize the greatest amounts of effective bulk shear, while heavy rainfall and flash flooding prevailed over already wet areas of south-central and southeastern Iowa. Fortunately flash flooding was predominantly relegated to road closures, though some flooding did linger into the 29th.||Note, the morning round of strong to severe storms is covered in another Storm Data event entry (27-28 May 2019).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel to quarter sized hail in addition to very heavy rainfall near Attica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued along the Missouri River through the month of May. Increased flood levels prompted the closure of Interstate 29 once again, from Highway 34 southward to the Missouri Border, and from I680/29 split northward to the I680/80 split. Gavins Point Dam releases were increased from 55,000 cfs to 75,000 cfs by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 34 closed due to High water from the Missouri River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active weather pattern continued across portions of Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 25th into the 26th, with heavy rain the prevailing hazard this time around. A pair of severe reports were seen during the evening of the 25th though, as storms were able to tap into a thermodynamic environment with 2000+ J/kg MUCAPE. While the heaviest rainfall remained south of Iowa, into northern Missouri, MUCAPE values around 1000 J/kg, precipitable water around 1.5 to 1.7 inches, and the 850 mb jet nose reaching to the Iowa-Missouri border allowed periods of heavy rainfall to occur, mainly residing in the 2 to 3 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.52 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of convective complexes, rolled through the state within a roughly 24 to 36 hour period. The first of which was linear in nature and started its life back in central and western Kansas during the evening of the 26th and made its way towards Iowa with the help of SW 500mb flow and a potent 850 mb low level jet. The second system originated across south central and western Nebraska and propagated eastward as a mesoscale convective systems along a relatively sharp MUCAPE gradient.  ||Generally speaking in both cases MUCAPE values were around 500-1500 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 30 to 50 kts, and aided by the low level jet (especially on the morning of the 27th).  Given the progressive nature of the storms, heavy rainfall and flooding was generally not seen, but a few locations were able to see around 2 inches of rainfall over the roughly 24 hour period between the main rounds of storms. The main results of the storms were multiple severe wind gust and wind damage reports, though a couple severe hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported heavy patio furniture blown over/across yard. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup over Iowa was primed for a heavy rainfall event for a few reasons, including persistent SW 500 mb flow, a strong and SW oriented 850 mb jet, strong low level moisture transport, and elevated CAPE around 1000-2000 J/kg. Precipitable water values, given the strong moisture transport into the area, rose into the neighborhood of 1.7 inches throughout the event as well. While effective bulk shear values were supportive of organized convection, that generally did not come to fruition given the widespread lifting with persistent low level warm air/theta-e advection and 850 mb jet into the region. As a result, the vast majority of reports and general consequences revolved around heavy rainfall and flooding, including a number of road closures. A couple severe wind gusts and damage reports were received in addition to the water issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 4.60 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmosphere with strong potential instability of 2500-3000 J/kg along with deep-layer shear of 40 to 50 knots led to the development of scattered nocturnal storms in the warm advection zone ahead of an eastward moving shortwave along the Nebraska and South Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large branches removed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northeast into Iowa overnight.  While overall instability was around 500 J/KG, the 0-3km MLCAPE was over 125 J/KG across southwest Iowa accounting for around a third of the overall updraft strength.  Effective helicity values were nearly 500 m2/s2.   The strongly sheared environment along with decent near surface instability led to the development of two tornadoes across southwest Iowa during the night.  The first tornado was an EF-1 and virtually destroyed an old farm building southeast of Anita.  The second more powerful tornado occurred southeast of Adair and impacted a few different farmsteads.  The final farmstead encountered received EF-2 damage producing 1 injury and 1 fatality.  The tornado crossed Interstate 80 and then remained in rural cropland for the remainder of the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado entered from Adair County and continued to move north through rural areas.  Only tree damage and minor power line damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active weather pattern continued across portions of Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 25th into the 26th, with heavy rain the prevailing hazard this time around. A pair of severe reports were seen during the evening of the 25th though, as storms were able to tap into a thermodynamic environment with 2000+ J/kg MUCAPE. While the heaviest rainfall remained south of Iowa, into northern Missouri, MUCAPE values around 1000 J/kg, precipitable water around 1.5 to 1.7 inches, and the 850 mb jet nose reaching to the Iowa-Missouri border allowed periods of heavy rainfall to occur, mainly residing in the 2 to 3 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.04 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, fairly unorganized surface low pressure system slid across the state on the 18th through the 19th, becoming more organized over time. In the meantime, it produced multiple rounds of showers and storms over large areas of southern and eastern Iowa, primarily resulting in heavy rainfall and sub-severe thunderstorms.  Periods of 1000-2000 J/kg MUCAPE and negligible bulk effective shear suggested a limited at best severe threat, while strong 850 mb moisture transport and precipitable water values between 1.4 and 1.6 inches pointed towards an increased heavy rainfall threat. Ultimately, heavy rainfall values prevailed and were in the 2 to 3 inch range across southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.39 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup over Iowa was primed for a heavy rainfall event for a few reasons, including persistent SW 500 mb flow, a strong and SW oriented 850 mb jet, strong low level moisture transport, and elevated CAPE around 1000-2000 J/kg. Precipitable water values, given the strong moisture transport into the area, rose into the neighborhood of 1.7 inches throughout the event as well. While effective bulk shear values were supportive of organized convection, that generally did not come to fruition given the widespread lifting with persistent low level warm air/theta-e advection and 850 mb jet into the region. As a result, the vast majority of reports and general consequences revolved around heavy rainfall and flooding, including a number of road closures. A couple severe wind gusts and damage reports were received in addition to the water issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.19 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0600-0900CST brought a light glaze of ice accumulation. After a period of rainfall late morning, precipitation changed to snow|in the afternoon. Visibility briefly dropped to 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times between 1400-1800, with total accumulation of 2.3 inches at Spirit Lake and 1.7 inches at Lake Park. Northwest winds increased by evening and gusted as high as 36 mph 3 miles south southeast of Lake Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was in place with a surface boundary stretched across Iowa from Storm Lake southeastward into southeast Iowa. This boundary provided the focus for thunderstorm development throughout the late afternoon and evening hours. The low level environment provided the needed conditions for many of these storms to rotate and produce funnel clouds and brief, weak tornadoes. Many of the tornadoes that occurred were brief touchdowns in open ares with no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed and moved southwest before becoming nearly stationary in open cropland. Persisted for several minutes before dissipating. Rated EF-Unknown as no damage indicators were impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northeast into Iowa overnight.  While overall instability was around 500 J/KG, the 0-3km MLCAPE was over 125 J/KG across southwest Iowa accounting for around a third of the overall updraft strength.  Effective helicity values were nearly 500 m2/s2.   The strongly sheared environment along with decent near surface instability led to the development of two tornadoes across southwest Iowa during the night.  The first tornado was an EF-1 and virtually destroyed an old farm building southeast of Anita.  The second more powerful tornado occurred southeast of Adair and impacted a few different farmsteads.  The final farmstead encountered received EF-2 damage producing 1 injury and 1 fatality.  The tornado crossed Interstate 80 and then remained in rural cropland for the remainder of the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cass County Emergency Manager reported several outbuildings damaged and power poles down along Glendale Road about 1/2 mile east of Highway 148, southeast of Anita. This is a delayed report and time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The state found itself under broad upper level ridging to zonal flow, allowing for a weak cold front to slide into the state and stall around the Interstate 80 corridor during the evening and overnight hours. The environment was fairly ripe for thunderstorm activity, including 2000-3000+ J/kg SBCAPE and hail CAPE values around 500 J/kg, but lacked supportive effective bulk shear with values generally around 30 kts or less.  With the front stalling across central Iowa, and the thermodynamic environment what it was, thunderstorms predominantly lingered across central Iowa through the evening and overnight hours, producing periods of severe hail during the first couple of hours and transitioning to sub-severe storms and a predominant heavy rainfall threat. In the end, there were a number of reports of quarter to near golf ball sized hail, followed by heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3+ inches by the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.38 inches from 7am Thursday through 9am Friday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued along the Missouri River through the month of May. Increased flood levels prompted the closure of Interstate 29 once again, from Highway 34 southward to the Missouri Border, and from I680/29 split northward to the I680/80 split. Gavins Point Dam releases were increased from 55,000 cfs to 75,000 cfs by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 29 closed again from Highway 34 south to the Missouri Border.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mills county Emergency Management is recommending residents prepare for evacuation west of Interstate 29. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along the outflow from weakened morning storms, additional storms fired rapidly during the mid-afternoon hours crossing from South Dakota into northwest Iowa. The environment was considerably unstable with up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE, but deep-layer bulk shear was only 20-25 knots and resulted in only weakly organized storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea size, but a few stones as large as quarters.

Along the outflow from weakened morning storms, additional storms fired rapidly during the mid-afternoon hours crossing from South Dakota into northwest Iowa. The environment was considerably unstable with up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE, but deep-layer bulk shear was only 20-25 knots and resulted in only weakly organized storms.

The state found itself under broad upper level ridging to zonal flow, allowing for a weak cold front to slide into the state and stall around the Interstate 80 corridor during the evening and overnight hours. The environment was fairly ripe for thunderstorm activity, including 2000-3000+ J/kg SBCAPE and hail CAPE values around 500 J/kg, but lacked supportive effective bulk shear with values generally around 30 kts or less.  With the front stalling across central Iowa, and the thermodynamic environment what it was, thunderstorms predominantly lingered across central Iowa through the evening and overnight hours, producing periods of severe hail during the first couple of hours and transitioning to sub-severe storms and a predominant heavy rainfall threat. In the end, there were a number of reports of quarter to near golf ball sized hail, followed by heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3+ inches by the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.25 inches since 10:30 pm Thursday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was in place with a surface boundary stretched across Iowa from Storm Lake southeastward into southeast Iowa. This boundary provided the focus for thunderstorm development throughout the late afternoon and evening hours. The low level environment provided the needed conditions for many of these storms to rotate and produce funnel clouds and brief, weak tornadoes. Many of the tornadoes that occurred were brief touchdowns in open ares with no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a funnel cloud visible from Ames.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The hits just kept on rolling, with yet another pair of rounds of strong to severe storms, heavy rainfall, and flooding. Through much of the end of May, the synoptic setup across the region remained relatively unchanged, including this event. Upper level flow continued to be out of the SW, precipitable water values remained in excess of 1.5 inches, and a relatively stationary boundary remained over the area. In addition to that broad setup, MUCAPE values hovered in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, bulk effective shear was supportive in the 40 to 60 kts range, and low level moisture flow continued to move into the area.  ||The 28th saw two rounds of strong to severe storms move through from the west and southwest, each causing at least a few severe reports and contributing to overall heavy rainfall and eventually flash flooding. Severe reports were generally relegated to west central and southwest Iowa, where storms were strongest and able to utilize the greatest amounts of effective bulk shear, while heavy rainfall and flash flooding prevailed over already wet areas of south-central and southeastern Iowa. Fortunately flash flooding was predominantly relegated to road closures, though some flooding did linger into the 29th.||Note, the morning round of strong to severe storms is covered in another Storm Data event entry (27-28 May 2019).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WHO TV reported water over the road on Highway 2 east of Promise City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of convective complexes, rolled through the state within a roughly 24 to 36 hour period. The first of which was linear in nature and started its life back in central and western Kansas during the evening of the 26th and made its way towards Iowa with the help of SW 500mb flow and a potent 850 mb low level jet. The second system originated across south central and western Nebraska and propagated eastward as a mesoscale convective systems along a relatively sharp MUCAPE gradient.  ||Generally speaking in both cases MUCAPE values were around 500-1500 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 30 to 50 kts, and aided by the low level jet (especially on the morning of the 27th).  Given the progressive nature of the storms, heavy rainfall and flooding was generally not seen, but a few locations were able to see around 2 inches of rainfall over the roughly 24 hour period between the main rounds of storms. The main results of the storms were multiple severe wind gust and wind damage reports, though a couple severe hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported quarter sized hail that fell between 629 and 634 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup over Iowa was primed for a heavy rainfall event for a few reasons, including persistent SW 500 mb flow, a strong and SW oriented 850 mb jet, strong low level moisture transport, and elevated CAPE around 1000-2000 J/kg. Precipitable water values, given the strong moisture transport into the area, rose into the neighborhood of 1.7 inches throughout the event as well. While effective bulk shear values were supportive of organized convection, that generally did not come to fruition given the widespread lifting with persistent low level warm air/theta-e advection and 850 mb jet into the region. As a result, the vast majority of reports and general consequences revolved around heavy rainfall and flooding, including a number of road closures. A couple severe wind gusts and damage reports were received in addition to the water issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cass County Dispatch reported flash flooding near 740th and Rockport, and 730th and Seattle, near the West Nodaway River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0600-0900CST brought a light glaze of ice accumulation. After a period of rainfall late morning, precipitation changed to snow|in the afternoon. Visibility briefly dropped to 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times between 1400-1800, with total accumulation of 4.0 inches at Sibley. Northwest winds increased by evening and gusted as high as 40 mph 2 miles northeast of Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.75 inches at Canton and 2.19 inches near Harrisburg) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. The Big Sioux River at Fairview (gage site FVWS2) reached the highest crest on record at 10.63 feet above flood stage on March 15. The river remained in minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.75 inches at Canton and 2.19 inches near Harrisburg) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. An operational railroad bridge was completely washed out by flood waters. The Big Sioux River above Canton (gage site CBSS2) reached the third highest crest on record at 9.68 feet above flood stage on March 15. The river remained in major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours, isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River. With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size. Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.

Thunderstorms moved northeast into Iowa overnight.  While overall instability was around 500 J/KG, the 0-3km MLCAPE was over 125 J/KG across southwest Iowa accounting for around a third of the overall updraft strength.  Effective helicity values were nearly 500 m2/s2.   The strongly sheared environment along with decent near surface instability led to the development of two tornadoes across southwest Iowa during the night.  The first tornado was an EF-1 and virtually destroyed an old farm building southeast of Anita.  The second more powerful tornado occurred southeast of Adair and impacted a few different farmsteads.  The final farmstead encountered received EF-2 damage producing 1 injury and 1 fatality.  The tornado crossed Interstate 80 and then remained in rural cropland for the remainder of the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter sized hail via social media. This is a delayed report and time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The state resided to the northeast of a tightening surface low that was tracking northward into eastern Nebraska. As a result, surface winds were sustained out of the east and at times gusting into the 40 to 50 plus mph range. While many locations were able to handle the gusty conditions, a few pockets of the state did experience damage with wind gusts possibly reaching severe criteria due to collapsing showers and/or ability to mix down stronger winds aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported non-thunderstorm wind damage that included three inch tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, fairly unorganized surface low pressure system slid across the state on the 18th through the 19th, becoming more organized over time. In the meantime, it produced multiple rounds of showers and storms over large areas of southern and eastern Iowa, primarily resulting in heavy rainfall and sub-severe thunderstorms.  Periods of 1000-2000 J/kg MUCAPE and negligible bulk effective shear suggested a limited at best severe threat, while strong 850 mb moisture transport and precipitable water values between 1.4 and 1.6 inches pointed towards an increased heavy rainfall threat. Ultimately, heavy rainfall values prevailed and were in the 2 to 3 inch range across southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.14 inches over the last 24 hours.

With the front stalling across central Iowa, and the thermodynamic environment what it was, thunderstorms predominantly lingered across central Iowa through the evening and overnight hours, producing periods of severe hail during the first couple of hours and transitioning to sub-severe storms and a predominant heavy rainfall threat. In the end, there were a number of reports of quarter to near golf ball sized hail, followed by heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3+ inches by the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator reported heavy rainfall of 3.07 inches since 6 pm Thursday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup over Iowa was primed for a heavy rainfall event for a few reasons, including persistent SW 500 mb flow, a strong and SW oriented 850 mb jet, strong low level moisture transport, and elevated CAPE around 1000-2000 J/kg. Precipitable water values, given the strong moisture transport into the area, rose into the neighborhood of 1.7 inches throughout the event as well. While effective bulk shear values were supportive of organized convection, that generally did not come to fruition given the widespread lifting with persistent low level warm air/theta-e advection and 850 mb jet into the region. As a result, the vast majority of reports and general consequences revolved around heavy rainfall and flooding, including a number of road closures. A couple severe wind gusts and damage reports were received in addition to the water issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 2.15 inches. Beginning time estimated from radar.
