EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The hits just kept on rolling, with yet another pair of rounds of strong to severe storms, heavy rainfall, and flooding. Through much of the end of May, the synoptic setup across the region remained relatively unchanged, including this event. Upper level flow continued to be out of the SW, precipitable water values remained in excess of 1.5 inches, and a relatively stationary boundary remained over the area. In addition to that broad setup, MUCAPE values hovered in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, bulk effective shear was supportive in the 40 to 60 kts range, and low level moisture flow continued to move into the area.  ||The 28th saw two rounds of strong to severe storms move through from the west and southwest, each causing at least a few severe reports and contributing to overall heavy rainfall and eventually flash flooding. Severe reports were generally relegated to west central and southwest Iowa, where storms were strongest and able to utilize the greatest amounts of effective bulk shear, while heavy rainfall and flash flooding prevailed over already wet areas of south-central and southeastern Iowa. Fortunately flash flooding was predominantly relegated to road closures, though some flooding did linger into the 29th.||Note, the morning round of strong to severe storms is covered in another Storm Data event entry (27-28 May 2019).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muchakinock Creek gauge went into flood during the evening hours on the 28th and continued into the early afternoon hours of the 29th. Water goes over the road on Highway 92 west of Oskaloosa when it is flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, fairly unorganized surface low pressure system slid across the state on the 18th through the 19th, becoming more organized over time. In the meantime, it produced multiple rounds of showers and storms over large areas of southern and eastern Iowa, primarily resulting in heavy rainfall and sub-severe thunderstorms.  Periods of 1000-2000 J/kg MUCAPE and negligible bulk effective shear suggested a limited at best severe threat, while strong 850 mb moisture transport and precipitable water values between 1.4 and 1.6 inches pointed towards an increased heavy rainfall threat. Ultimately, heavy rainfall values prevailed and were in the 2 to 3 inch range across southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.17 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours, isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River. With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size. Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 5 miles west of Rock Rapids, just east of Fig Avenue north of 160th Street, marked by damage to a corn field. The tornado traveled in a general east-southeast direction, striking a farmstead and flattening a silo within the first half-mile of its track.  Within another minute, the tornado slashed through another farmstead, causing significant damage to a grain bin, large trees, and removed roofing from a home and a livestock shed.  Around three miles west-southwest of Rock Rapids, several power transmission poles were broken.  As the tornado passed just south of Rock Rapids, damage was mainly to trees, with several uprooted.  The final structural damage occurred south of Rock Rapids just east of U.S. Highway 75 with roof panels torn off another livestock shed.  The tornado lifted just over 2 miles southeast of Rock Rapids after crossing County Road K52.  The average path width was 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two forecast points were already in flood at the beginning of May held over from April. The West Fork of the Des River at Humboldt was in flood and finally went out of flood in early May, before seeing two more periods of flood in the middle and again at the end of the month. These will be placed in separate entries. The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville also remained in flood from April and continued to remain in flood through the entire month of May. This forecast point has been in flood since mid March, and will finally go out of flood toward mid June 2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des River crested 6 times through the month of May. The crests are as follows: first, 10.5 ft on 9 May 2019 at 21:30 UTC, second, 10.07 ft on 15 May 2019 at 12:30 UTC, third, 8.88 ft on 23 May 2019 at 01:15 UTC, fourth, 9.28 ft on 25 May 2019 at 07:45 UTC, fifth, 12.3 ft on 28 May 2019 at 12:00 UTC, and sixth at 13.32 ft on 30 May 2019 at 16:45 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of convective complexes, rolled through the state within a roughly 24 to 36 hour period. The first of which was linear in nature and started its life back in central and western Kansas during the evening of the 26th and made its way towards Iowa with the help of SW 500mb flow and a potent 850 mb low level jet. The second system originated across south central and western Nebraska and propagated eastward as a mesoscale convective systems along a relatively sharp MUCAPE gradient.  ||Generally speaking in both cases MUCAPE values were around 500-1500 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 30 to 50 kts, and aided by the low level jet (especially on the morning of the 27th).  Given the progressive nature of the storms, heavy rainfall and flooding was generally not seen, but a few locations were able to see around 2 inches of rainfall over the roughly 24 hour period between the main rounds of storms. The main results of the storms were multiple severe wind gust and wind damage reports, though a couple severe hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported damage to an ISU research farm, including a sheet metal roof section blown off, pole barn damaged, and large grain cart pulled from the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was in place with a surface boundary stretched across Iowa from Storm Lake southeastward into southeast Iowa. This boundary provided the focus for thunderstorm development throughout the late afternoon and evening hours. The low level environment provided the needed conditions for many of these storms to rotate and produce funnel clouds and brief, weak tornadoes. Many of the tornadoes that occurred were brief touchdowns in open ares with no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0500-1000CST brought up to two tenths of an inch of ice accumulation. After a period of rainfall late morning, precipitation changed to snow|in the afternoon. Visibility briefly dropped to 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times between 1200-1700, with total accumulation of 4.1 inches at Sanborn and 4.0 inches at Sheldon. Northwest winds increased by evening and gusted as high as 39 mph at Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.75 inches at Canton and 2.19 inches near Harrisburg) resulted in extensive flooding of agricultural land in the flood plain. An operational railroad bridge was completely washed out by flood waters. The Big Sioux River above Canton (gage site CBSS2) reached the third highest crest on record at 9.68 feet above flood stage on March 15. The river remained in major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was in place with a surface boundary stretched across Iowa from Storm Lake southeastward into southeast Iowa. This boundary provided the focus for thunderstorm development throughout the late afternoon and evening hours. The low level environment provided the needed conditions for many of these storms to rotate and produce funnel clouds and brief, weak tornadoes. Many of the tornadoes that occurred were brief touchdowns in open ares with no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: First of several weak tornadoes on this day. Tornado remained in open farmland several miles south of Schaller.  Rated EF-Unknown as no damage indicators were impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed on a southwest to northeast  orientated front across southwest Iowa.  The thunderstorms produced two tornado touchdowns, one near Shenandoah and the other near Red Oak.  No damage was reported.  Also the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall amounts with 2.5 inches reported in Oakland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very short lived tornado was reported by emergency management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours, isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River. With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size. Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal shed was damaged, along with numerous trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The state found itself under broad upper level ridging to zonal flow, allowing for a weak cold front to slide into the state and stall around the Interstate 80 corridor during the evening and overnight hours. The environment was fairly ripe for thunderstorm activity, including 2000-3000+ J/kg SBCAPE and hail CAPE values around 500 J/kg, but lacked supportive effective bulk shear with values generally around 30 kts or less.  With the front stalling across central Iowa, and the thermodynamic environment what it was, thunderstorms predominantly lingered across central Iowa through the evening and overnight hours, producing periods of severe hail during the first couple of hours and transitioning to sub-severe storms and a predominant heavy rainfall threat. In the end, there were a number of reports of quarter to near golf ball sized hail, followed by heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3+ inches by the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.00 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The hits just kept on rolling, with yet another pair of rounds of strong to severe storms, heavy rainfall, and flooding. Through much of the end of May, the synoptic setup across the region remained relatively unchanged, including this event. Upper level flow continued to be out of the SW, precipitable water values remained in excess of 1.5 inches, and a relatively stationary boundary remained over the area. In addition to that broad setup, MUCAPE values hovered in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, bulk effective shear was supportive in the 40 to 60 kts range, and low level moisture flow continued to move into the area.  ||The 28th saw two rounds of strong to severe storms move through from the west and southwest, each causing at least a few severe reports and contributing to overall heavy rainfall and eventually flash flooding. Severe reports were generally relegated to west central and southwest Iowa, where storms were strongest and able to utilize the greatest amounts of effective bulk shear, while heavy rainfall and flash flooding prevailed over already wet areas of south-central and southeastern Iowa. Fortunately flash flooding was predominantly relegated to road closures, though some flooding did linger into the 29th.||Note, the morning round of strong to severe storms is covered in another Storm Data event entry (27-28 May 2019).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported multiple secondary roads remained under water or closed  in Lucas County from the heavy rainfall and subsequent overland and river flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported multiple secondary secondary roads remained under water or closed, a few culverts known to be damaged or destroyed, and one rural wood truss bridge lost in Davis County from the rainfall and subsequent overland and river flooding yesterday. The Fox River rose over 15 feet in 5 hours at Bloomfield. A rural truss wooden bridge was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued heavy rain and high streamflows led to many rivers going back into flood in late May across central Iowa. Several crests occurred on the Des Moines River at Ottumwa. Many of these were due to planned releases from Lake Red Rock, with some of the water from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Fort Dodge crested at 11.44 feet on 25 May 2019 at 22:45 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup over Iowa was primed for a heavy rainfall event for a few reasons, including persistent SW 500 mb flow, a strong and SW oriented 850 mb jet, strong low level moisture transport, and elevated CAPE around 1000-2000 J/kg. Precipitable water values, given the strong moisture transport into the area, rose into the neighborhood of 1.7 inches throughout the event as well. While effective bulk shear values were supportive of organized convection, that generally did not come to fruition given the widespread lifting with persistent low level warm air/theta-e advection and 850 mb jet into the region. As a result, the vast majority of reports and general consequences revolved around heavy rainfall and flooding, including a number of road closures. A couple severe wind gusts and damage reports were received in addition to the water issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported gravel roads in the area under water, and creeks and streams rising fast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, fairly unorganized surface low pressure system slid across the state on the 18th through the 19th, becoming more organized over time. In the meantime, it produced multiple rounds of showers and storms over large areas of southern and eastern Iowa, primarily resulting in heavy rainfall and sub-severe thunderstorms.  Periods of 1000-2000 J/kg MUCAPE and negligible bulk effective shear suggested a limited at best severe threat, while strong 850 mb moisture transport and precipitable water values between 1.4 and 1.6 inches pointed towards an increased heavy rainfall threat. Ultimately, heavy rainfall values prevailed and were in the 2 to 3 inch range across southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.30 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0600-0800CST brought up to a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation. After a period of rainfall late morning to early afternoon, precipitation changed to snow. Visibility briefly dropped to 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times between 2000-2300, with total accumulation of 3.0 inches 3 miles west northwest of Everly and 2.5 inches at Spencer. Northwest winds increased by late evening and gusted as high as 47 mph at Spencer and 39 mph 3 miles west of Dickens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 1 and 3 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable areal flooding and flooding of small streams. Numerous roads, bridges and homes were compromised by flood water, especially in areas near Hornick, Danbury, Holly Springs, and Moville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wolf Creek was running over its banks onto Old Highway 141 at Humboldt Avenue.  Flooding was blocked by the county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved northeast into Iowa overnight.  While overall instability was around 500 J/KG, the 0-3km MLCAPE was over 125 J/KG across southwest Iowa accounting for around a third of the overall updraft strength.  Effective helicity values were nearly 500 m2/s2.   The strongly sheared environment along with decent near surface instability led to the development of two tornadoes across southwest Iowa during the night.  The first tornado was an EF-1 and virtually destroyed an old farm building southeast of Anita.  The second more powerful tornado occurred southeast of Adair and impacted a few different farmsteads.  The final farmstead encountered received EF-2 damage producing 1 injury and 1 fatality.  The tornado crossed Interstate 80 and then remained in rural cropland for the remainder of the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed south southeast of Adair and moved quickly north.  Initial damage was mainly to trees, grain bins and outbuildings.  Three houses were encountered by the tornado with the first two receiving EF0 damage.  The third house had the roof removed with several of the outside walls collapsing, but the inside walls remaining intact.  Two individuals were sleeping inside when the tornado arrived with one injury and one fatality occurring.  This structure sustained EF2 damage with the tornado passing over Interstate 80 and remaining in rural cropland for the remainder of its path in Adair County.  The tornado then crossed into Guthrie County before dissipating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches, up to 3 inches in diameter, were snapped from trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two forecast points were already in flood at the beginning of May held over from April. The West Fork of the Des River at Humboldt was in flood and finally went out of flood in early May, before seeing two more periods of flood in the middle and again at the end of the month. These will be placed in separate entries. The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Estherville also remained in flood from April and continued to remain in flood through the entire month of May. This forecast point has been in flood since mid March, and will finally go out of flood toward mid June 2019.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Humboldt crested at 10.36 feet on 2 May 2019 at 06:15 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the middle of May and increased outflows from Lake Red Rock allowed the Des Moines River at Eddyville and Ottumwa to go into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested at 12.1 feet on 18 May 2019 at 00:50 UTC. While heavy rain did fall at times during the period, the majority of the reason the river is in flood was due to increased releases from Lake Red Rock.

With the front stalling across central Iowa, and the thermodynamic environment what it was, thunderstorms predominantly lingered across central Iowa through the evening and overnight hours, producing periods of severe hail during the first couple of hours and transitioning to sub-severe storms and a predominant heavy rainfall threat. In the end, there were a number of reports of quarter to near golf ball sized hail, followed by heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 3+ inches by the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel to half dollar sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The hits just kept on rolling, with yet another pair of rounds of strong to severe storms, heavy rainfall, and flooding. Through much of the end of May, the synoptic setup across the region remained relatively unchanged, including this event. Upper level flow continued to be out of the SW, precipitable water values remained in excess of 1.5 inches, and a relatively stationary boundary remained over the area. In addition to that broad setup, MUCAPE values hovered in the 1000-2000 J/kg range, bulk effective shear was supportive in the 40 to 60 kts range, and low level moisture flow continued to move into the area.  ||The 28th saw two rounds of strong to severe storms move through from the west and southwest, each causing at least a few severe reports and contributing to overall heavy rainfall and eventually flash flooding. Severe reports were generally relegated to west central and southwest Iowa, where storms were strongest and able to utilize the greatest amounts of effective bulk shear, while heavy rainfall and flash flooding prevailed over already wet areas of south-central and southeastern Iowa. Fortunately flash flooding was predominantly relegated to road closures, though some flooding did linger into the 29th.||Note, the morning round of strong to severe storms is covered in another Storm Data event entry (27-28 May 2019).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported heavy rainfall of 2.20 inches so far today, and 1.7 inches since 330pm. Beginning time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup over Iowa was primed for a heavy rainfall event for a few reasons, including persistent SW 500 mb flow, a strong and SW oriented 850 mb jet, strong low level moisture transport, and elevated CAPE around 1000-2000 J/kg. Precipitable water values, given the strong moisture transport into the area, rose into the neighborhood of 1.7 inches throughout the event as well. While effective bulk shear values were supportive of organized convection, that generally did not come to fruition given the widespread lifting with persistent low level warm air/theta-e advection and 850 mb jet into the region. As a result, the vast majority of reports and general consequences revolved around heavy rainfall and flooding, including a number of road closures. A couple severe wind gusts and damage reports were received in addition to the water issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported heavy rainfall of 2.25 inches. Beginning time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of convective complexes, rolled through the state within a roughly 24 to 36 hour period. The first of which was linear in nature and started its life back in central and western Kansas during the evening of the 26th and made its way towards Iowa with the help of SW 500mb flow and a potent 850 mb low level jet. The second system originated across south central and western Nebraska and propagated eastward as a mesoscale convective systems along a relatively sharp MUCAPE gradient.  ||Generally speaking in both cases MUCAPE values were around 500-1500 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 30 to 50 kts, and aided by the low level jet (especially on the morning of the 27th).  Given the progressive nature of the storms, heavy rainfall and flooding was generally not seen, but a few locations were able to see around 2 inches of rainfall over the roughly 24 hour period between the main rounds of storms. The main results of the storms were multiple severe wind gust and wind damage reports, though a couple severe hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several 3 plus inch tree branches broken across the Beaverdale neighborhood. Over 2000 residents without power. This is a delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong upper level wave pushed into the western Dakotas during the evening hours, isolated supercellular storms developed across central South Dakota and moved through areas west of the James River. With 2000-2500 J/kg of MLCAPE and deep layer shear of 35 to 45 knots, the isolated storms produced hail up to golf ball size. Soon, an increasing amount of DCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg and increase in 0-3 km shear with development of a strong low-level jet aided in the transition to enhanced linear structures, which then developed a series of mesovortices as the line moved into far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several brief tornadoes developed with mesovortices in southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa, along with areas of enhanced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread swath of wind damage occurred from the eastern shores of West Okoboji Lake to the eastern shore of East Okoboji Lake.  Areas near West Lake Okoboji featured limb and minor building damage with wind speeds of 65 to 75 mph. However, more significant damage occurred east of Highway 71 where large trees were snapped and an office building severely damaged with wind gusts from 75 to 93 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nocturnal thunderstorms moved through during the early morning hours of Friday, May 24, 2019.  A tornado watch was in effect. A flash flood watch was also in effect. A squall line developed, with several possible embedded tornadic rotations. 24 hour rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 8.75 inches, the highest amount in southwest Iowa. River flooding developed on local streams, including the West Nishnabotna River, the Nishnabotna River, The Nodaway River, the Little Nemaha River, Weeping Water Creek, and the Big Blue River. The rainfall also pushed the Missouri River at Rulo back into the moderate category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported 5.5 to 7 inches of rain in Red Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system was in place with a surface boundary stretched across Iowa from Storm Lake southeastward into southeast Iowa. This boundary provided the focus for thunderstorm development throughout the late afternoon and evening hours. The low level environment provided the needed conditions for many of these storms to rotate and produce funnel clouds and brief, weak tornadoes. Many of the tornadoes that occurred were brief touchdowns in open ares with no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local fired department reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was not an extremely hot and humid day across the state, but it did set up for at least a few thunderstorms. A cold front was moving through the state from the northwest through the morning hours, dropping roughly a quarter of the way into the state by the early afternoon. Ahead of the front, temperatures were in the mid to upper 70s, along with dew points in the 50s. Steep low level lapse rates aided in the production of SBCAPE values around 2000 J/kg, while effective bulk shear remained under 30 kts ahead of and along the front. The majority of storms remained sub-severe, but a few were able to produce at least borderline severe hail with the favorable lapse rates and relatively low freezing level.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering stationary front that produced severe hail and heavy rain across central Iowa on the 16th into the 17th remained in the area throughout the day on the 17th. The environment once again was relatively juicy on the warm side of the front, including SBCAPE values in excess of 2000-3000 J/kg in areas and hail CAPE values in excess of 500 J/kg. Fortunately, storms did not generate as much severe weather or localized heavy rainfall, and actually only produced 1 severe hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported dime to quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to heavy rainfall throughout the early to middle portions of the month, the West Fork of the Des Moines River at Humboldt went back into flood for about a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Humboldt crested at 10.49 feet on 11 May 2019 at 00:45 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the middle of May and increased outflows from Lake Red Rock allowed the Des Moines River at Eddyville and Ottumwa to go into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Ottumwa crested at 13.53 feet on 19 May 2019 at 12:10 UTC. While heavy rain did fall at times during the period, the majority of the reason the river is in flood was due to increased releases from Lake Red Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain during the middle of May and increased outflows from Lake Red Rock allowed the Des Moines River at Eddyville and Ottumwa to go into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Eddyville crested at 63.1 feet on 27 May 2019 at 08:15 UTC. While heavy rain did fall at times during the period, the majority of the reason the river is in flood was due to increased releases from Lake Red Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to additional river flooding toward the end of May 2019. Some of the flooding on the Des Moines River at Eddyville was due to planned dam releases from Lake Red Rock combined with heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Eddyville crested twice, with the first crest at 65.61 feet on 29 May 2019 at 15:00 UTC and the second crest at 65.53 feet on 20 May 2019 at 07:30 UTC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, fairly unorganized surface low pressure system slid across the state on the 18th through the 19th, becoming more organized over time. In the meantime, it produced multiple rounds of showers and storms over large areas of southern and eastern Iowa, primarily resulting in heavy rainfall and sub-severe thunderstorms.  Periods of 1000-2000 J/kg MUCAPE and negligible bulk effective shear suggested a limited at best severe threat, while strong 850 mb moisture transport and precipitable water values between 1.4 and 1.6 inches pointed towards an increased heavy rainfall threat. Ultimately, heavy rainfall values prevailed and were in the 2 to 3 inch range across southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.17 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of convective complexes, rolled through the state within a roughly 24 to 36 hour period. The first of which was linear in nature and started its life back in central and western Kansas during the evening of the 26th and made its way towards Iowa with the help of SW 500mb flow and a potent 850 mb low level jet. The second system originated across south central and western Nebraska and propagated eastward as a mesoscale convective systems along a relatively sharp MUCAPE gradient.  ||Generally speaking in both cases MUCAPE values were around 500-1500 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 30 to 50 kts, and aided by the low level jet (especially on the morning of the 27th).  Given the progressive nature of the storms, heavy rainfall and flooding was generally not seen, but a few locations were able to see around 2 inches of rainfall over the roughly 24 hour period between the main rounds of storms. The main results of the storms were multiple severe wind gust and wind damage reports, though a couple severe hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a 62 mph wind gust on the home weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted northeast from the Texas Panhandle early on March 9, reaching the western Great Lakes as an intense closed low. This system included a leading surge of warm air, allowing precipitation to become rain or light freezing rain over a large area, with many locations receiving a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch ice accumulation before colder air wrapped in behind and transitioned precipitation to snowfall. Brief periods of heavier snowfall due to marginally unstable air off the surface led to accumulations of 2 to 6 inches north of Interstate 90 along with travel disruptions, with generally a trace to 2 inches south; the exception being parts of northwest Iowa which received 2 to 4 inches. As the surface low pressure moved across Iowa by later in the afternoon and evening of March 9, northwest winds increased to a gusty 30 to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of freezing rain from 0500-0900CST brought up to a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation. After a period of rainfall late morning, precipitation changed to snow|in the afternoon. Visibility briefly dropped to 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times between 1200-1600, with total accumulation of 1.8 inches at Orange City. Northwest winds increased by evening and gusted as high as 41 mph near Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmosphere with strong potential instability of 2500-3000 J/kg along with deep-layer shear of 40 to 50 knots led to the development of scattered nocturnal storms in the warm advection zone ahead of an eastward moving shortwave along the Nebraska and South Dakota border.

An upper level low moved over Iowa, sparking some showers and thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening. Some of the storms spawned some funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. The storm complex then expanded and produced heavy rain. These heavy rains fell on completely saturated soils, and produced a round of significant flash flooding, which closed many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed portions of Highway 30 in Clarence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event unfolded as heavy rainfall drenched the area on March 13-14, 2019. With frozen and impervious ground, full and rapid runoff was maximized. In addition to the rainfall, between 1 and 3 inches of snow water equivalent remained in the snow pack south of Interstate 90, with 3 to 6 inches of snow water equivalent north of Interstate 90. Fifteen river gage sites measured record stages around the area. Winds gusted to 50 to 70 mph at times, and colder air filtering into the system at the end of the storm produced a light accumulation of ice west of the James River and from 1 to 4 inches of snowfall in parts of southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and heavy rainfall (measured 1.52 inches near Le Mars) resulted in significant flooding of agricultural lands, as well as county road C70. The Floyd River at James (gage site JMEI4) reached the highest stage on record cresting 5.48 feet above flood stage on March 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night. Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line. A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa. Despite weakening as the storms moved out of South Dakota into Iowa, a swath of wind damage continued, albeit more sporadic, with speeds from 60 to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux and Little Sioux Rivers. For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Little Sioux River at Spencer (gage site SPWI4) had an early month crest at 1.40 feet above flood stage on May 2, before falling below flood stage on May 16. Late month rains pushed the river back over flood stage on May 23, reaching a crest in moderate flood stage of 4.02 feet above flood stage on May 30. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain.  The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Generally speaking, there was not much to write home about with regards to the synoptic setup for severe or highly impactful weather. Even so, an environment with nominal MUCAPE (often under 1000 J/kg), high shear (0-1km shear in excess of 30 kts, 0-1km helicity in excess of 200 m2/s2), and consistent low level moisture transport was able to produce weak, brief tornadoes and sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall. ||Specifically on the tornadoes, two brief weak tornadoes occurred in the relatively low CAPE, high shear environment in central Iowa.  Little impact was observed from the tornadoes as they stayed in rural areas and away from structures and towns. ||On the rainfall front, multiple rounds of showers and storms produced numerous reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall throughout southern Iowa, even trickling north into central Iowa in a few instances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time and exact location estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day, another round of storms within upper level zonal flow and low level jet initiated storms. In this instance, the nose of the low level jet oriented itself such that the focus was near and west of the Iowa-Nebraska border. The first storms initiated late in the evening just west of the Missouri River and propagated eastward within an environment of  2000-3000 J/kg MUCAPE, 1000-1300 J/kg DCAPE, and around 30 kts of effective bulk shear. Generally speaking, storms struggled to grow in aerial extent and remain organized. Even so, with the at least marginally supportive environment, a handful of damaging wind reports were received, primarily affecting Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported a large tree limb over 6 inches in diameter severed from a tree in the 150 block of R45 Highway. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow, south moving cold front and an outflow boundary from morning thunderstorms over northern Illinois, combined with a hot and humid environment to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois overnight. Several thunderstorms became severe, and produced large hail and damaging winds. Quarter sized hail was reported across Clinton and Iowa counties, while destructive winds of 70-90 MPH were reported Iowa and Washington Counties. |In addition, torrential rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were reported along the Interstate 80 corridor as storms slowly moved across the area. This lead to flash flooding across many major roadways, including Interstate 80 near Coralville and Highway 965 in Oakdale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large 6 inch diameter tree branch was reported down near the intersection of Kimberly Road and Jersey Ridge Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at New Boston Lock and Dam 17 began the month above its major flood stage of 18.5 feet. The river stage at the beginning of the month was 22.39 feet. The river would rise to a crest of 23.59 feet beginning on May 3rd. This crest is preliminary the 4th highest crest. The river fell below major flood stage around 1 AM on May 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a large tree down at 10th Street and Division.

Rainfall of one to three inches on frozen ground and into a snow pack with between 1 and 3 inches of liquid water equivalent resulted in considerable areal flooding and flooding of small streams. Numerous roads, bridges and homes were compromised by flood water, especially in areas near Hornick, Danbury, Holly Springs, and Moville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding resulted in extensive damage to public infrastructure including numerous county and township roads, culverts, and some bridges.

Showers and thunderstorms tracked eastward across parts of eastern Iowa and northern Illinois on Memorial Day 2019 as a low pressure system lifted a warm front through the region.   | |Some of the storms produced tornadoes during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Very heavy rain also fell in a short amount of time in areas.  Flash flooding occurred in the Quad Cities metro, with several feet of water on many streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many reports of main streets in Davenport under 1 to 2 1/2 feet of water due to torrential rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A  strong tornado touched down northeast of Cantril and continued east for 17 minutes on a 7.5 mile path.  The tornado was 100 yards at its widest.  A  house and a hunting cabin were destroyed.  The damage to the house was due to estimated maximum wind speeds of 140 MPH.  As the tornado moved east, outbuildings and trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux and Little Sioux Rivers. For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Little Sioux River at Linn Grove (gage site LNNI4) hovered near flood stage with an early month crest at 0.24 feet above flood stage on May 12, before falling below flood stage on May 16. Late month rains pushed the river back over flood stage on May 24, reaching a crest in moderate flood stage of 1.63 feet above flood stage on May 31. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated instability from 1000-2000 J/kg along with 40-50 kt deep-layer shear sustained organized convection pushing though southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Winds averaged from 60 to 80 mph along the path of the large scale bow echo, producing significant damage to trees and power lines, and minor damage to buildings along the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs snapped up to four inches diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for strong to severe storms took place on the 24th of September. A cold front entered the state from the northwest during the late afternoon and early evening hours, entering an environment with strong low level moisture inflow, SBCAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear around 40 kts. With frontal forcing and the aforementioned conditions, storms were of little question and the main questions became whether or not severe weather would present itself. Additionally, relatively steep low level lapse rates around 7 to 8 degC/km, around 200 m2/s2 0-1km SR Helicity, and DCAPE around and in excess of 1000 J/kg hinted at increased risks for funnel clouds and damaging winds.||Ultimately, widespread severe weather was avoided, but a handful of storms were able to produce funnel clouds, damaging winds, and localized water issues from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported street flooding along 7th and Walnut and 6th and Sunnyside in Atlantic. Roads were impassable at the time with vehicles stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold frontal boundary initiated storms overnight across portions of north central and northeast South Dakota that congealed into a quasi-linear convective system. That complex of storms moved east southeast into northwest Iowa and portions of central Iowa through the mid-morning hours of the 18th. As the storms had moved ahead of the best forcing/parent boundary storms remained sub-severe and weakened through the morning and into the early afternoon. As some semblance of diurnal recovery occurred, a few storms were able to rejuvenate and produce a handful marginally severe to severe wind and hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported tree and roof damage, including shingles blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported tree damage around Conrad, as well as a couple power lines down. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Generally speaking, there was not much to write home about with regards to the synoptic setup for severe or highly impactful weather. Even so, an environment with nominal MUCAPE (often under 1000 J/kg), high shear (0-1km shear in excess of 30 kts, 0-1km helicity in excess of 200 m2/s2), and consistent low level moisture transport was able to produce weak, brief tornadoes and sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall. ||Specifically on the tornadoes, two brief weak tornadoes occurred in the relatively low CAPE, high shear environment in central Iowa.  Little impact was observed from the tornadoes as they stayed in rural areas and away from structures and towns. ||On the rainfall front, multiple rounds of showers and storms produced numerous reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall throughout southern Iowa, even trickling north into central Iowa in a few instances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado was suggested by a TDS on radar and subsequently confirmed via high resolution satellite imagery.   A short path through rural cropland, hence the EF-U rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Works are writtien.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Gladstone Lock and Dam 18 began the month above its major flood stage of 14 feet. The river stage at the beginning of the month was 17.44 feet.  The river would rise to a crest of 19.67 feet at 500 AM CST on May 3rd. This crest is preliminary the 5th highest crest. The river fell below major flood stage around 10 AM on May 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Works are writtien.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Camanche (CMMI4) began the month above its major flood stage of 20.5 feet and also near a crest level of 22.7 feet. The river stage was falling just after midnight on the first. The river fell below major flood stage late evening on May 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow, south moving cold front and an outflow boundary from morning thunderstorms over northern Illinois, combined with a hot and humid environment to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois overnight. Several thunderstorms became severe, and produced large hail and damaging winds. Quarter sized hail was reported across Clinton and Iowa counties, while destructive winds of 70-90 MPH were reported Iowa and Washington Counties. |In addition, torrential rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were reported along the Interstate 80 corridor as storms slowly moved across the area. This lead to flash flooding across many major roadways, including Interstate 80 near Coralville and Highway 965 in Oakdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of September 12th. These storms produced a weak tornado in the vicinity of Ossian (Winneshiek County) that damaged some farm buildings and numerous trees. A second tornado occurred northwest of Farmersburg (Clayton County). The damage from this tornado was limited to a few trees, some outbuildings and one home had branches impaled through a wall. In Marquette and McGregor (Clayton County) runoff from the heavy rains turned streets into mini-rivers leaving behind mud covered streets and sidewalks. State Highway 76 north of Marquette was closed because of a mudslide and downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tops of several trees were blown down southwest of Alta Vista.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure moved across the Upper Midwest, thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Memorial Day (May 27th) across portions of northeast Iowa. These storms quickly became severe and produced tornadoes across Floyd, Chickasaw and Howard Counties. The first tornado developed near Charles City (Floyd County) and was on the ground for about 7 miles, extensively damaging some buildings at the Floyd County fairgrounds along damaging three houses severe enough to make them inhabitable. Another tornado moved across rural portions of Howard County for 12 miles and damaged a wind turbine and cattle barns. Two other weaker tornadoes also touched down across northwest Chickasaw County and northern Howard County very close to the Minnesota state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down west of Charles City and moved north-northeast before dissipating northwest of Colwell. The tornado did extensive damage to seven buildings at the Floyd County fairgrounds. At a fertilizer plant across the road from the fairgrounds a roof was partially peeled off from one building while the main building was left leaning after having an entire side ripped off.  Three homes along the path were damaged severely enough to be declared inhabitable.

These storms quickly became severe and produced tornadoes across Floyd, Chickasaw and Howard Counties. The first tornado developed near Charles City (Floyd County) and was on the ground for about 7 miles, extensively damaging some buildings at the Floyd County fairgrounds along damaging three houses severe enough to make them inhabitable. Another tornado moved across rural portions of Howard County for 12 miles and damaged a wind turbine and cattle barns. Two other weaker tornadoes also touched down across northwest Chickasaw County and northern Howard County very close to the Minnesota state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado entered into Howard County southwest of Elma as it moved north-northeast and then dissipated after crossing Dale Avenue. Farm buildings were damaged just north of the Chickasaw County line. This was witnessed by area fire departments serving as spotters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle wave in northwest flow aloft moved across southwest Minnesota toward early evening as a warm frontal zone crawled northward toward the Minnesota-Iowa border. An isolated storm developed across southwest Minnesota and intensified in an environment with near 1000 J/kg MLCAPE. With deep shear at only around 30 knots, the storm did not maintain intensity for long as it dissipated moving southeast through northwest Iowa.

Generally speaking, there was not much to write home about with regards to the synoptic setup for severe or highly impactful weather. Even so, an environment with nominal MUCAPE (often under 1000 J/kg), high shear (0-1km shear in excess of 30 kts, 0-1km helicity in excess of 200 m2/s2), and consistent low level moisture transport was able to produce weak, brief tornadoes and sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall. ||Specifically on the tornadoes, two brief weak tornadoes occurred in the relatively low CAPE, high shear environment in central Iowa.  Little impact was observed from the tornadoes as they stayed in rural areas and away from structures and towns. ||On the rainfall front, multiple rounds of showers and storms produced numerous reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall throughout southern Iowa, even trickling north into central Iowa in a few instances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.10 inches over the last 24 hours.

Extensive flooding occurred late May and into June across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri. The last half of May was very wet with 5-8 inches of rain falling over the region. This combined with saturated soils, brought many rivers above Moderate to Major flood levels. Many climate sites only saw 3 days of no precipitation from May 16th to May 31st. |The Wapsipinicon, Iowa, Skunk, Des Moines, Rock, and Green Rivers all recorded a preliminary Top 10 crest on record. The main stem Mississippi also crested again in late May / early June, with sites from Le Claire LD 14 on downstream recording a Top 10 crest. |Several significant flood events also occurred in March - Early May 2019 due to snowmelt, heavy rain, and saturated soil.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River rose above the major flood stage of 27 feet in the late evening  hours May 28th, and crested at 4 AM CST May 30 at 27.9 feet. It feel back below major flood stage late afternoon May 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary settled across northern Kansas, northern Missouri and central Illinois. This front was the focus area for the development of several rounds of showers and thunderstorms that impacted the region. The first round came through during the morning, and produced mainly torrential rainfall, small hail, brief wind gusts and frequent lightning. |The second round of showers and thunderstorms developed and passed across the region during the late afternoon and evening hours. A segment of thunderstorms became severe along and south of Highway 34, and produced damaging wind gusts near 70 MPH. This led to several reports of wind damage from Burlington eastward to Roseville. Additionally, prolonged torrential rainfall over already saturated grounds lead to numerous reports of flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding in Kalona, mainly on south side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed during the evening across western South Dakota, moving east and becoming more organized as the low-level jet increased focus into south central and southeast South Dakota late at night. Deep-layer effective shear reached 35 to 45 knots with MUCAPE to 2000J/kg. 0-3km shear was 25 to 35 knots, largely normal to the orientation of the convective line. A large-scale bowing segment evolved quickly with a long track of damaging wind across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa. Despite weakening as the storms moved out of South Dakota into Iowa, a swath of wind damage continued, albeit more sporadic, with speeds from 60 to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant street flooding with stalled vehicles at 25th Street and Chambers Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a moderate to heavy rainfall event providing 2 to 3 inches just prior to this, the pump was primed so to speak for flooding potential. A generally stationary front resided south of the area into Missouri, but upper level and mid level flow strongly suggested storms would track north of the surface boundary and across portions of southern and central Iowa. The initial wave of rain was initiated by a mid-upper level short wave riding the parent flow, triggering storms in the Oklahoma-Kansas region by late morning and traveled northeast throughout the day, eventually reaching Iowa by the late afternoon and evening. The initial round exited and subsided by midnight, only to see a second round initiated by the low level jet back in northeast and central Kansas. That second round made its way into southern Iowa by around 6 am and continued throughout the morning. When all said and done, heavy rainfall in excess of 5 inches was reported in a few locations, as well as multiple reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water over Highway 63 north of Bloomfield. Power was also reported out across Bloomfield. Time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding occurred late May and into June across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri. The last half of May was very wet with 5-8 inches of rain falling over the region. This combined with saturated soils, brought many rivers above Moderate to Major flood levels. Many climate sites only saw 3 days of no precipitation from May 16th to May 31st. |The Wapsipinicon, Iowa, Skunk, Des Moines, Rock, and Green Rivers all recorded a preliminary Top 10 crest on record. The main stem Mississippi also crested again in late May / early June, with sites from Le Claire LD 14 on downstream recording a Top 10 crest. |Several significant flood events also occurred in March - Early May 2019 due to snowmelt, heavy rain, and saturated soil.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Camanche (CMMI4) rose above its major flood stage of 20.5 feet during the morning of May 30th and continued into June. The river crested in June, with the stage rising to 21.1 feet at the end of May 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary settled across northern Kansas, northern Missouri and central Illinois. This front was the focus area for the development of several rounds of showers and thunderstorms that impacted the region. The first round came through during the morning, and produced mainly torrential rainfall, small hail, brief wind gusts and frequent lightning. |The second round of showers and thunderstorms developed and passed across the region during the late afternoon and evening hours. A segment of thunderstorms became severe along and south of Highway 34, and produced damaging wind gusts near 70 MPH. This led to several reports of wind damage from Burlington eastward to Roseville. Additionally, prolonged torrential rainfall over already saturated grounds lead to numerous reports of flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North English  River, west of Q Avenue  rose above flood stage rapidly due to the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively routine setup for strong to severe storms took place on the 24th of September. A cold front entered the state from the northwest during the late afternoon and early evening hours, entering an environment with strong low level moisture inflow, SBCAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear around 40 kts. With frontal forcing and the aforementioned conditions, storms were of little question and the main questions became whether or not severe weather would present itself. Additionally, relatively steep low level lapse rates around 7 to 8 degC/km, around 200 m2/s2 0-1km SR Helicity, and DCAPE around and in excess of 1000 J/kg hinted at increased risks for funnel clouds and damaging winds.||Ultimately, widespread severe weather was avoided, but a handful of storms were able to produce funnel clouds, damaging winds, and localized water issues from heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported power lines, grain bins, residential and farm building damage damaged or destroyed, heavy tree damage, and an emergency communication tower destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively stationary front found it self draped across portions of northern Missouri and southern and southeast Iowa during the evening and overnight hours of the 27th. Fortunately, given the evening event, severe weather was fairly limited, yielding larger concerns for heavy rainfall. Environmentally, MUCAPE values were around 1000 J/kg, effective bulk shear around 40-50 kts and fading over time, precipitable water values around 1.5 to 1.8 inches, and at least moderate moisture transport into the area. While the best conditions for any severe weather potential and excessive rainfall were down into northern Missouri, areas of southern Iowa saw multiple rounds of showers and storms during the evening and overnight, resulting in heavy rainfall around 2 to 3 inches. Fortunately, little to no water issues were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.20 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of low level jet initiated storms in central Nebraska slowly made their way eastward overnight and during the early morning hours of the 14th. With zonal flow aloft, their general motion was directly eastward. As the low level jet waned in the morning, the overall extent of the storms diminished, but never fully ceased. As diurnal heating increased the storms remained on the leading edge of the gradient, where MUCAPE values crept into the 1000-3000 J/kg neighborhood. While hail cape values were marginal, under 800 J/kg, there was enough effective bulk shear, around 40 to 50 kts at times, to allow storms to organize and produce periods of heavy rainfall and severe hail. The majority of storms produced marginally severe hail, with a couple of reports as large as ping pong balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported half dollar sized hail, via the mPING app.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow, south moving cold front and an outflow boundary from morning thunderstorms over northern Illinois, combined with a hot and humid environment to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois overnight. Several thunderstorms became severe, and produced large hail and damaging winds. Quarter sized hail was reported across Clinton and Iowa counties, while destructive winds of 70-90 MPH were reported Iowa and Washington Counties. |In addition, torrential rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were reported along the Interstate 80 corridor as storms slowly moved across the area. This lead to flash flooding across many major roadways, including Interstate 80 near Coralville and Highway 965 in Oakdale.

An upper level low moved over Iowa, sparking some showers and thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening. Some of the storms spawned some funnel clouds and weak tornadoes. The storm complex then expanded and produced heavy rain. These heavy rains fell on completely saturated soils, and produced a round of significant flash flooding, which closed many roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 22 closed due to water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April continued at the start of May 2019 for many locations along the Big Sioux and Little Sioux Rivers. For many locations, this started the third month of constant flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of flooding from April. The Big Sioux River above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) remained in flood through the month.  Late month rains pushed the river to a crest at 10.33 feet above flood stage on May 29, the third highest stage on record. Significant agricultural flooding persisted, with no opportunity for spring planting throughout the flood plain. County Road 13, Iowa Highway 10 and South Dakota Highway 46 were again subjected to closure at the end of the month. The river remained at major flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Generally speaking, there was not much to write home about with regards to the synoptic setup for severe or highly impactful weather. Even so, an environment with nominal MUCAPE (often under 1000 J/kg), high shear (0-1km shear in excess of 30 kts, 0-1km helicity in excess of 200 m2/s2), and consistent low level moisture transport was able to produce weak, brief tornadoes and sustained periods of moderate to heavy rainfall. ||Specifically on the tornadoes, two brief weak tornadoes occurred in the relatively low CAPE, high shear environment in central Iowa.  Little impact was observed from the tornadoes as they stayed in rural areas and away from structures and towns. ||On the rainfall front, multiple rounds of showers and storms produced numerous reports of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall throughout southern Iowa, even trickling north into central Iowa in a few instances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded heavy rainfall of 2.19 inches over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another day, another round of storms within upper level zonal flow and low level jet initiated storms. In this instance, the nose of the low level jet oriented itself such that the focus was near and west of the Iowa-Nebraska border. The first storms initiated late in the evening just west of the Missouri River and propagated eastward within an environment of  2000-3000 J/kg MUCAPE, 1000-1300 J/kg DCAPE, and around 30 kts of effective bulk shear. Generally speaking, storms struggled to grow in aerial extent and remain organized. Even so, with the at least marginally supportive environment, a handful of damaging wind reports were received, primarily affecting Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported that a machine shed suffered heavy damage, with the roof of the shed being blown off in the 2700 block of G50 Highway in Warren County. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow, south moving cold front and an outflow boundary from morning thunderstorms over northern Illinois, combined with a hot and humid environment to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois overnight. Several thunderstorms became severe, and produced large hail and damaging winds. Quarter sized hail was reported across Clinton and Iowa counties, while destructive winds of 70-90 MPH were reported Iowa and Washington Counties. |In addition, torrential rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were reported along the Interstate 80 corridor as storms slowly moved across the area. This lead to flash flooding across many major roadways, including Interstate 80 near Coralville and Highway 965 in Oakdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward into the area Friday, May 24, sparking widespread  thunderstorms in the morning/early afternoon time frame and again in the early evening.  Two tornadoes were occurred in McDonough County during the early afternoon.  Towards evening, another tornadic storm developed, producing two tornadoes. The first occurred in Johnson County just southwest of Iowa City, and the second in Cedar County just north of West Branch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down briefly, causing tree damage at a farmstead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively cool, but juicy atmosphere existed across the area with southerly surface winds dominating and southwesterly mid and upper level flow. During the early morning hours of the 9th, scattered thunderstorms aided by the low level jet remained predominantly sub-severe with small hail. Through the morning and early afternoon hours, a surface warm front entered and moved across the state from southwest to northeast, all the while increasing MUCAPE values across the southeast half to two thirds of the state into the 2000-4000+ J/kg range. Additionally, moderate to steep low level lapse rates boosted DCAPE values into the 1000-1300 J/kg range, increasing the potential for strong downdrafts and resulting surface winds. Effective bulk shear was marginal through most of the event, yielding around 30 to 40kts. ||Resulting storms generally rolled through the state in waves from southwest to northeast, with at least periods of organization. The primary cluster of storms rolled through during the late afternoon, producing a fairly consistent trail severe thunderstorm winds and thunderstorm wind damage from southwest of Des Moines all the way through the Waterloo area. A second round of at least brief sever storms moved through overnight just southeast of the Des Moines metro, but was short lived unlike the cluster earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minburn Fire Department reported 12 inch diameter tree snapped. The report was relayed by Dallas County Emergency Management and the time is estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated instability from 1000-2000 J/kg along with 40-50 kt deep-layer shear sustained organized convection pushing though southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Winds averaged from 60 to 80 mph along the path of the large scale bow echo, producing significant damage to trees and power lines, and minor damage to buildings along the path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tents destroyed by Arnolds Park Amusement Park.

One person was killed in a single vehicle accident north of Westgate (Fayette County). The driver lost control on icy roads, crashed into a ditch and was killed when ejected from the vehicle. Roads were reported to be icy after some light freezing drizzle had occurred earlier in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a late afternoon single vehicle accident north of Westgate. Light freezing drizzle had moved through the area earlier in the day and the driver lost control of the vehicle on the icy roads and crashed into a ditch. The driver was ejected from the vehicle and died at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of September 12th. These storms produced a weak tornado in the vicinity of Ossian (Winneshiek County) that damaged some farm buildings and numerous trees. A second tornado occurred northwest of Farmersburg (Clayton County). The damage from this tornado was limited to a few trees, some outbuildings and one home had branches impaled through a wall. In Marquette and McGregor (Clayton County) runoff from the heavy rains turned streets into mini-rivers leaving behind mud covered streets and sidewalks. State Highway 76 north of Marquette was closed because of a mudslide and downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused mudslides and pushed down trees across the southeast corner of Allamakee County that closed State Highway 76 south of Waukon Junction. Numerous trails and campgrounds were closed in the Yellow River State Forest because of the flooding. Some county roads were closed because of culvert washouts or debris washed onto them from flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down south of Ossian. The tornado moved northeast before dissipating southeast of Ossian. Several farm buildings and numerous trees were damaged along the path of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two weak tornadoes developed ahead of an approaching boundary in an area of weak instability but strong shear.  No damage occurred with these tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down in an unharvested corn field just north of 180th street about one half mile west of Redwood Avenue.  The tornado traveled northeast remaining in agricultural fields and crossing Redwood Avenue then ending prior to 170th street.  No damage indicators were impacted by the tornado, hence the EF-U rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwestern U.S. in the early morning of Saturday, December 28 pushed eastward through the day, allowing low pressure to organize near the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles. The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29. Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota.||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30. Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas. Snowfall totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches from the James River Valley westward, to 1 to 3 inches across much of northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa.  A record daily precipitation of 1.38 inches was measured in Sioux City on December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain produced a glaze of icing early on Saturday, December 28 before temperatures climbed above freezing. Light mixed precipitation returned and changed quickly to snow early on December 29, then continued into December 30 producing hazardous travel. Snowfall totaled 1.5 inches at Rock Rapids. Wind gusts reached as high as 41 mph at Sioux Falls and 38 mph at Sheldon by early Monday, December 30, resulting in blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough across the southwestern U.S. in the early morning of Saturday, December 28 pushed eastward through the day, allowing low pressure to organize near the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles. The storm intensified as it moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa by 0600CST December 29. Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota.||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30. Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas. Snowfall totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches from the James River Valley westward, to 1 to 3 inches across much of northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa.  A record daily precipitation of 1.38 inches was measured in Sioux City on December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain produced a glaze of icing early on Saturday, December 28 before temperatures climbed above freezing. Light mixed precipitation returned and changed quickly to snow early on December 29, then continued into December 30 producing hazardous travel. Snowfall totaled 1.9 inches at Le Mars. Wind gusts reached as high as 38 mph at Le Mars by early Monday, December 30, resulting in blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across northeast Iowa during the early morning hours of September 19th. These storms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain that caused some gravel roads to washout across northern Howard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped between 2 and 4 inches of rain across northern Howard County. The runoff from this heavy rain caused some washouts of gravel roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Chester, an estimated five inches of rain fell.

Strong warming aloft ahead of the system led to periods of light rain or light mixed precipitation, while heavy snow organized in the trowal over central and northeast South Dakota.||Impacts from the storm became significant as the storm closed off in the mid-levels and slowed movement, only reaching southern Minnesota by 0600CST December 30. Precipitation changed over to snow as colder air collapsed southward, and winds increased to a gusty 40 to 55 mph across southeast South Dakota and adjacent areas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain produced a glaze of icing early on Saturday, December 28 before temperatures climbed above freezing. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front arriving during the afternoon of December 9th, helped create a line of intense snow showers with strong winds and falling temperatures. This snow squall created a short duration burst of snow that included white-out conditions and strong winds, leading to dangerous travel. As this snow squall moved into extreme eastern Iowa, it lost intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources from spotters, webcams, and emergency management reported an intense snow shower with white-out conditions, rapidly falling temperatures, and a sudden change to dangerous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system developed across the eastern Great Plains and Midwest during the afternoon of November 26, and brought a strong cold front across the area during the evening and overnight hours. Several rounds of showers and strong thunderstorms develop along this front, some of which produced severe wind gusts. Damage was limited mainly to trees, and mainly occurred over southeast Iowa and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported estimated thunderstorm winds between 55 to 65 MPH along Highway 61 near Keokuk. The time of the event was estimated by the spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing storm system brought widespread light to moderate snow to all of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on November 10-11, 2019. Snow began on the evening of the 10th and came to an end during the early afternoon hours of the 11th. Snowfall totals ranged from up to an inch south of Interstate 80, to 2 to 4 inches north of the Interstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer south of Stanley reported 3.3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through eastern Nebraska and Western Iowa on November 26-27, 2019. A winter storm watch was issued November 25, Monday afternoon, with the entire area upgraded to a winter storm warning early in the morning on Tuesday November 26. Neither county in Iowa (Harrison, Monona) reached winter storm warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer reported 4 inches of snow.
