EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms early in the morning on July 3 produced very heavy rain. Strong thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto a camper a Nisha Bend Recreation Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reports from the 13h through the 19th in this entry. ||A relatively deep and widespread snowpack existed across the region during early to mid March. Many areas in central and northern Iowa had around a foot to two feet of snow on the ground, along with many areas in upstream river basins in Minnesota and the Dakotas having similar or deeper snowpacks on the ground. Additionally, with the below normal temperatures throughout the majority of the 2019 year to date, soils were frozen to depths of a foot or more throughout the aforementioned areas. All of that primed the area for potential moderate to major flooding issues should a rapid snowmelt and/or rainfall event occur, which unfortunately came to fruition. ||The primary driver was two-fold with warm air advection on the backside of a departing high pressure on the 12th, and a sizable low pressure system that moved through the region on the 13th and 14th. The event began with widespread temperatures and dew points in the upper 30s to low 40s on the 12th with the initial warm air advection, which continued through the overnight hours into the 13th. As the low pressure system developed off the Colorado Rockies, warm air advection continued to push temperatures and dew points up into the upper 40s and low 50s on the 13th and 14th, aiding the rapid snow melt. On top of the rapid snowmelt, which often went from 12 or more inches on the ground to zero in a matter of about two days, light to moderate rainfall occurred ahead of and on the backside of the low pressure system on the 13th and 14th. All combined to cause widespread overland flooding, river flooding, and ice jam issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT reported Iowa 14 closed between US 30 and County Road E27. Other secondary roads across the county were also reported closed due to flooding. Many roads and some bridges were damaged from the flooding. The bridge on 140th St. was a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure strengthened across the area through the week resulting in hot temperatures and high humidity values in place across Iowa for a prolonged period of time. This led to high heat index values through much of the period, especially during the afternoon and early evening hours. Little relief was felt during the overnight hours as low temperatures remained in the 70s to around 80. Heat index values were consistently in the 105 to 115 degree range during much of the daytime period.

After a warm start to October, cooler air began to filter down into the Missouri Valley region. A day plus prior, late on the 10th and into the 11th, a cold front slid through the state, dropping temperatures a solid 10 to 15 degrees. While that itself only put the state into the 40s and upper 30s, it also allowed cooler and drier air to continue to filter in. By the evening of the 12th, the parent low pressure system to the aforementioned cold front remained rooted in northern Minnesota and deepened, yielding southwesterly winds across Iowa. Even with the southerly flow, dew points in the low to mid 20s and mostly clear skies allowed temperatures to drop below freezing statewide. The coldest temperatures, into the mid and uppe 20s, were seen in the valley areas such as the Nishnabotna. The remainder of the state did manage to drop to around 30.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms tracked northeast across central and northeast Iowa September 9th evening into the early morning hours of the 10th, bringing 50-60 mph winds and heavy rain to portions of the region.  The heaviest rain amounts over 1.50 inches, fell across Buchanan and Delaware counties in Iowa and eastern Jo Daviess and northern Stephenson counties in Illinois.  Heavy rain also fell roughly in a line from Tipton, to Maquoketa, to Bellevue, IA.  The strongest winds occurred near La Motte, IA where a grain bin collapsed and numerous tree branches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported  a few trees and branches blown down in Masonville, and 2.20 inches of rain one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported baseball sized hail via picture from social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain-producing showers and storms moved across areas north of Interstate 80 on September 11 and 12.  The higher 2-day rainfall totals were between 3 and 6 inches.  The Dubuque Airport had a record rainfall total of 1.98 on September 11.  On the 12th, the Dubuque Airport picked up another 3.70 of rain making the 2-day total 5.68. ||Flash flooding occurred in Dubuque, where fast moving water over a foot deep poured down streets.  The Mississippi River in the Dubuque area rose about 3 feet while the Pecatonica River near Freeport rose about 6 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. 52 near Sageville was closed around 1 AM due to water over the road.  The road was opened a few hours later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread and significant flash flood was reported in the city of Dubuque and some surrounding areas, including Catfish Creek near Swiss Valley Park.  Reports of significant street flooding and stalled cars were common, and some video showing rapidly water flowing through city streets was seen on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of September 27th, a wave of low pressure moved along a stationary front in eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois, bringing several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms, and tremendously heavy rains. This resulted in some reports of large hail, but mostly very heavy rain totals over 3 inches.  Flash flooding became the primary threat late in the evening, as the storms became continuous over the same areas in northeast Missouri, west central Illinois, and far southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of pea to quarter size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front over eastern Nebraska. Damaging winds along with some large hail occurred in the storms on the 9th during the afternoon across southwest Iowa and late at night over eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A localized downburst flattened corn, caused failure to a door on a metal building, and peeled off its roof and throwing it into another building.  There was damage to vehicles inside the metal shed.  There was also tree limbs down nearby.  This wind event occurred near 120th Street and O Avenue in Page County, between Swanton and Bethesda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A western wave moved onshore and through the Rockies on the 19th and 20th, becoming a closed upper level system as it moved eastward along the Nebraska-South Dakota border. As the system pivoted and turned northeast, the accompanied surface low deepened as it moved northeastward through Iowa. With a tight and tightening surface pressure gradient and 850 mb winds around 50 to 60 kts, strong winds were certain. Even as the tightest gradient moved through during the nocturnal hours, enough mixing was possible to tap into the stronger 900-850 mb winds and the potential for wind gusts approaching 55 to 60 mph. ||While winds were strong and gusty, only Estherville recorded a wind gust at or in excess of 60 mph within the county warning area. The winds caused damage in Kossuth County, including to a communications tower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville Municipal Airport ASOS recorded a 60 mph wind gust.

Heavy rain also fell roughly in a line from Tipton, to Maquoketa, to Bellevue, IA.  The strongest winds occurred near La Motte, IA where a grain bin collapsed and numerous tree branches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grain bin collapsed and multiple trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain-producing showers and storms moved across areas north of Interstate 80 on September 11 and 12.  The higher 2-day rainfall totals were between 3 and 6 inches.  The Dubuque Airport had a record rainfall total of 1.98 on September 11.  On the 12th, the Dubuque Airport picked up another 3.70 of rain making the 2-day total 5.68. ||Flash flooding occurred in Dubuque, where fast moving water over a foot deep poured down streets.  The Mississippi River in the Dubuque area rose about 3 feet while the Pecatonica River near Freeport rose about 6 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was reported down on Clifton Street NE, as relayed by broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system that moved across South Dakota and Minnesota, dragging behind it a cold front that moved into portions of northwest and central Iowa on the 30th of September. As the surface front moved through Iowa, it stalled out and effectively divided the state in half as it oriented it self from southwest to northeast across the state. While the itself stalled, it separated two distinctly different air masses with temperatures in the 50s to low 70s prevailing on the north side and 80s to around 90 prevailing to the south. Surface moisture was also divided with dew points in the 50s on the cool side and low to mid 70s on the warm side. ||Showers and storms persisted north of the front overnight of the 30th into the 1st and during much of the day of the 1st. That activity yielded little more than periods of moderate to heavy rain and sub-severe thunderstorms. Along and south of the front, thermodynamic and kinematics yielded much more vigorous storms and ultimately heavy rainfall and severe weather. SBCAPE profiles yielded 2000 to 3000+ J/kg by early to mid afternoon along with effective bulk shear around 50 to 60 kts, and persistent strong moisture flow yielding precipitable water values around 1.75 to 2.0 inches. Additionally, in the lowest 1 to 3 km, CAPE values exceed 100 J/kg and was accompanied by enhanced low level helicity due to frontal proximity. As a result, virtually all modes of severe weather were possible. In the end, the majority of reports revolved around severe hail, nearing 3 inches in diameter, and heavy rainfall in excess of 3 to 4 inches. A handful of funnel cloud and damaging wind reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WHO TV relayed tennis ball sized hail report. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A western wave moved onshore and through the Rockies on the 19th and 20th, becoming a closed upper level system as it moved eastward along the Nebraska-South Dakota border. As the system pivoted and turned northeast, the accompanied surface low deepened as it moved northeastward through Iowa. With a tight and tightening surface pressure gradient and 850 mb winds around 50 to 60 kts, strong winds were certain. Even as the tightest gradient moved through during the nocturnal hours, enough mixing was possible to tap into the stronger 900-850 mb winds and the potential for wind gusts approaching 55 to 60 mph. ||While winds were strong and gusty, only Estherville recorded a wind gust at or in excess of 60 mph within the county warning area. The winds caused damage in Kossuth County, including to a communications tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall in the upper reaches of the Big Sioux River Basin and 2 to 5 inches from Sioux Falls southward, rises occurred mid-month that routed downstream to cause minor flooding in the lowest sections of the basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (generally 3 to 4 inches around Sioux Falls, 4.72 inches at Canton, 2.51 inches at Hawarden), the Big Sioux River at Sioux City (gage site SXCI4) reached the 3rd highest stage on record of 0.81 feet above flood stage on September 19. Impacts were mainly limited to inundation of agricultural land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the tail of an upper-level wave dragged across northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota in the late afternoon and early evening on September 24, thunderstorms erupted along a pre-frontal trough and along the trailing cold front. Enhanced deep-layer shear along the pre-frontal trough up to 50 knots had a contribution from a slightly veering profile near the surface, which locally enhanced the tornado threat across northwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spurred by a period of excessive precipitation from September 10-12 that resulted in 5 to 10 inches of rainfall in the middle to upper reaches of the Big Sioux River basin and 2 to 5 inches from Sioux Falls downstream, extreme rises occurred mid-month, with many basin locations reaching record stages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After excessive two-day rainfall during September 10-12 (8.74 inches at Flandreau, 7.81 inches at Dell Rapids and generally 3 to 4 inches around Sioux Falls), the Big Sioux River above Canton (gage site CBSS2) reached the second highest stage on record of 10.88 feet above flood stage on September 13. Significant amounts of rural flooding occurred, and high water forced brief closure of U.S. Highway 18 east of Canton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: River levels continued to run high during the month of April. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A temporary flood barrier protecting downtown Davenport, Iowa failed during the afternoon of April 30th. Flood water from the Mississippi River quickly spread over several blocks of downtown Davenport, including the lower floors of many businesses, flooding vehicles in the area, and trapping some people in buildings requiring them the be removed by boat. No injuries or fatalities occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At exactly 333 PM CDT, as seen on the security camera system of a local restaurant, a temporary flood barrier failed, immediately inundating a portion of downtown Davenport. The Mississippi River flood water rushed into a portion of the downtown business district, flooding parked cars in streets and parking lots, and inundating the ground floor and basements of businesses in the immediate area. No injuries or fatalities occurred in this flash flood.
